<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625318 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00292: (2026x) Carrying values using connectors

- page_id: `255625318`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625318/2026x+Carrying+values+using+connectors
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:41.751+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['carrying-values-via-connectors', 'connectors']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

43209831

43209833

43209832

A connector can carry a runtime value over from one connectable element to another if the following three conditions are present

- Datatypes on both elements are the same or compatible (equality of value can be defined), or one is the subtype of another.
- If the elements are nested features, the owner of the nested features must exist.
- If the elements are nested properties, in this example, b1, a connector must be drawn from a3 to the port and from the port to b1 because UML does not have a concrete definition about nested properties. However, SysML does have a definition about nested properties; therefore, the connector can be drawn between nested properties without the help of any ports.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using Port and Connector to connect nested properties.

When primitive datatypes, in this example, n and a1, are bound together, the runtime values related to the role of both connector ends must be equal (but not necessarily the same instance). If the feature value of a data type value on one end changes, the data type value on the opposite end will change as well. As a result, n must equal a1, r must equal a2, s must equal a3, and b must equal a4 as follows.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Connectors connecting primitive datatypes.

43209830

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MT']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Binding Connector' space='']

However, SysML does have a definition about nested properties; therefore, the connector can be drawn between nested properties without the help of any ports. </li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties" ac:alt="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using Port and Connector to Connect Nested Properties.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Carrying values using connectors" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Using Port and Connector to connect nested properties.</h6><p>When primitive datatypes, in this example, n and a1, are bound together, the runtime values related to the role of both connector ends must be equal (but not necessarily the same instance). If the feature value of a data type value on one end changes, the data type value on the opposite end will change as well. As a result, n must equal a1, r must equal a2, s must equal a3, and b must equal a4 as follows. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Connectors Connecting Primitive Datatypes" ac:alt="Connectors Connecting Primitive Datatypes"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Connectors_connecting_primitive_datatypes.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Carrying values using connectors" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Connectors connecting primitive datatypes.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a07061d-ae39-40f2-9a0b-dc390e1391f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">43209830</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Connector" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Binding Connector" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626338 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00293: (2026x) Change Structural Feature Event

- page_id: `255626338`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626338/2026x+Change+Structural+Feature+Event
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:39.738+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models > (2026x) Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['change-structural-feature-event']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Change Structural Feature Event is an Event that occurs when a value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. The Change Structural Feature Event will be accepted by an Accept Change Structural Feature Event action. You can find more information about simulation of an Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action and a Change Structural Feature Event in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Change Structural Feature Event is an Event that occurs when a value of a specified Structural Feature on the Event changes. The Change Structural Feature Event will be accepted by an Accept Change Structural Feature Event action. You can find more information about simulation of an Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action and a Change Structural Feature Event in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Accept Change Structural Feature Event Action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Accept change structural feature Event action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624940 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00294: (2026x) Changing Fork behavior

- page_id: `255624940`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624940/2026x+Changing+Fork+behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:12.135+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to specify whether Forks in Activity Diagrams run in a parallel or sequential way during simulation. You can change the behavior of an individual Fork by adding a constraint with a hardcoded keyword (*sequential* or *parallel*). If a Fork is set to run in parallel, all outgoing edges from that Fork run simultaneously. If a Fork is set to run sequentially, only one outgoing edge from that Fork can run at a time.

If you change the Fork behavior by adding constraints as described in this chapter, this behavior will override the **Run Forks In Parallel** property of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''].

To change Fork behavior during simulation

- In an Activity Diagram, right-click a Fork and do one of the following: [ATTACHMENT filename='changing_fork_behavior.png']
  - If you want the fork to run sequentially, go to Simulation > Occurrence > Sequential .
  - If you want the fork to run in parallel, go to Simulation > Occurrence > Parallel .

After you change the behavior of a Fork as described above, the label indicating the specified behavior is added to the Fork symbol (either *sequential*, or *parallel*).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to specify whether Forks in Activity Diagrams run in a parallel or sequential way during simulation. You can change the behavior of an individual Fork by adding a constraint with a hardcoded keyword (<em>sequential</em> or <em>parallel</em>). If a Fork is set to run in parallel, all outgoing edges from that Fork run simultaneously. If a Fork is set to run sequentially, only one outgoing edge from that Fork can run at a time. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f7663df-f710-4098-8e71-f6081d9bb46f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you change the Fork behavior by adding constraints as described in this chapter, this behavior will override the <strong>Run Forks In Parallel</strong> property of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Simulation Configuration]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To change Fork behavior during simulation</p><hr /><ul><li>In an Activity Diagram, right-click a Fork and do one of the following:<ul><li>If you want the fork to run sequentially, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Occurrence</strong> &gt; <strong>Sequential</strong>.</li><li>If you want the fork to run in parallel, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Occurrence</strong> &gt; <strong>Parallel</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ul><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="changing_fork_behavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Changing Fork behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><p><br />After you change the behavior of a Fork as described above, the label indicating the specified behavior is added to the Fork symbol (either <em>sequential</em>, or <em>parallel</em>).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625109 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00295: (2026x) Checking Behaviors

- page_id: `255625109`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625109/2026x+Checking+Behaviors
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:23.043+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Action languages > (2026x) Action scripts APIs > (2026x) Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

##### Getting the current State of a runtime object

You can use the following ALH API to get the current State of a specified object. If the object has more than one active State, it returns the first active state. The API returns the instance of the state.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get the current state of a specified object using ALH API:

```text

```

##### Checking the State of an object

You can use script to check state of an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.in("stateName")***.* For example:

- self.in("Operating");

Alternatively, you can check the state of an object using ALH API. The API returns the Boolean value.

```text

```

Unless the object is specified, it will use the current active object, for example:

```text

```

##### Checking a visited State

You can use following ALH API to check the visited state. You can check whether a specified State was visited by calling the following command.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to check whether a specified State of State Machine, e.g.,*State1*, was visited through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Checking if an activity is being simulated

You can use the script to check if an object performs a specific activity. You can use the fUML object syntax with **object.performs("activityName")**. For example:

- self.performs("Driving");

##### Checking if an object is busy

You can use the script to check if an object performing any behavior. You can use the fUML object syntax with **object.isBusy()**. For example:

- self.isBusy();

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bbc299a6-2be1-4931-b10f-148b22ed0984"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Getting the current State of a runtime object</h3><p>You can use the following ALH API to get the current State of a specified object. If the object has more than one active State, it returns the first active state. The API returns the instance of the state.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0993a5d4-0f9c-48ce-9d36-e1b2d5af0b19"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getState(object)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>The following code fragment shows how to get the current state of a specified object using ALH API:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e0dc7604-40cc-47ad-906e-7aa4166d0b78"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getState(self);
ALH.getState(self).getName()        //To get name of the state instance use .getName()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Checking the State of an object</h3><p>You can use script to check state of an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.in(&quot;stateName&quot;)</strong><em>.</em> For example:</p><ul><li>self.in(&quot;Operating&quot;);</li></ul><p>Alternatively, you can check the state of an object using ALH API. <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">The API returns the Boolean value.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d2a7e748-0ab1-47b5-aa35-71a847309a97"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.inState(object, "stateName")
ALH.inState("stateName")                // Returns true or false.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Unless the object is specified, it will use the current active object, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94bef7e0-c006-4015-bd41-5fe5b115b3b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[if (ALH.inState("Operating")) {
	force=ccForce
} else {
	force = acc*2
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Checking a visited State</h3><p>You can use following ALH API to check the visited state. You can check whether a specified State was visited by calling the following command.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="78465268-4a73-4879-a5dd-b908c37b2173"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.wasInState("stateName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to check whether a specified State of State Machine, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">State1</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, was visited through ALH API.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4b996387-85c6-4eb8-930c-8f16c5dc2c81"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.wasInState("State1");	// Returns true or false.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Checking if an activity is being simulated</h3><p>You can use the script to check if an object performs a specific activity. You can use the fUML object syntax with <strong>object.performs(&quot;activityName&quot;)</strong>. For example:</p><ul><li>self.performs(&quot;Driving&quot;);</li></ul><h3>Checking if an object is busy</h3><p>You can use the script to check if an object performing any behavior. You can use the fUML object syntax with <strong>object.isBusy()</strong>. For example:</p><ul><li>self.isBusy();</li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625452 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00296: (2026x) Checking values against feature types

- page_id: `255625452`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625452/2026x+Checking+values+against+feature+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:45.770+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['value-check', 'checking-runtime-values']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

44165603

44165605

44165604

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will automatically run a compatibility check every time you assign a value to a feature during simulation. It checks the value against the feature's type. The value you assign must correspond with the type of the feature, meaning you can assign only values that are compatible with the feature's type. If you assign an incompatible value to a feature, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will not add it to the feature. The following table lists all compatible values for each datatype

| Primitive datatype (Qualified name) | Compatible value |
| --- | --- |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::BooleanPrimitiveValueTypes::Boolean (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::boolean | Strings "true" or "false" (non-case sensitive). |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::int | Strings that can be cast to numbers (If the string is a real number, the floating point will be eliminated). Natural number datatypesUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip) Real numbers, including the following four datatypes (The floating point will be eliminated)UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float | Strings that can be cast to numbers. Integer numbers including the following five datatypesUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::intUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip) |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::StringPrimitiveValueTypes::String (SysML Profile.mdzip) | Every value is compatible. |
| UML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::UnlimitedNaturalPrimitiveValueTypes::UnlimitedNatural (SysML Profile.mdzip) | Strings that can be cast into positive numbers.Real and integer (positive) numbers including the following datatypesUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::IntegerPrimitiveValueTypes::Integer (SysML Pro- file.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Pro- file::datatypes::intUML Standard Profile::UML2 Metamodel::PrimitiveTypes::RealPrimitiveValueTypes::Real (SysML Profile.mdzip)UML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::doubleUML Standard Profile::MagicDraw Profile::datatypes::float |
| Enumeration | If a value equals to one of the Enumeration Literals, the value is compatible. |

44165602

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625843 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00297: (2026x) Class

- page_id: `255625843`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625843/2026x+Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:09.170+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Model simulation > (2026x) Elements simulation
- labels: ['class-simulation', 'simulating-classes']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1427265110

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1427265112

1428069550

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1427265111

You can run a Class element that is not a Behavior. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from the selected Class. The default value of its parts and properties will be used for initialization. If the selected Class has a defined Classifier Behavior which is Activity, State Machine or Interaction, it will also be simulated. For example, if you execute the [ATTACHMENT filename='Calculator.mdzip'] Class, the simulation will be performed on the Calculator State Machine as shown in the figure [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0007.png'] in the State Machine section.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### State Machine as the Classifier Behavior of the Calculator Class.

If the Class does not have a Classifier Behavior, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize the object from the selected Class and show its values and parts in the **Variables** pane. For a SysML model, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will update the values within the object with the constraint properties and the binding connectors. Therefore, you can manipulate the values of the object when working with the parametric model of the Class.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Running a SysML block that has no defined Classifier Behavior.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Animation of a SysML Parametric diagram.

You can simulate a system even though there is no Classifier Behavior in either the parent or its subsystems. When Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit runs the system, all Classifiers that have or do not have Classifier Behaviors will be simulated. The simulation will run the system and will stop only if you terminate it by clicking the**Stop**button.

#### Initialization of value properties using Arrays of default values

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports use of Arrays in Class/Block as a data type. A primitive value type, e.g., String, Boolean, Integer, and Real, as a value property with a default value using an Array can be initialized. The value property is shown in tree control structure on the **Variables** pane as shown in the figure below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Primitive value types are initialized as value properties with default values using Arrays.

Furthermore, the value property name as a variable can be used as a member of an Array. For example, *x* = 5 and *v* = [2, 3, 4, x] make the initialized *v* = [2, 3, 4, 5].

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- The initialization through an instance replaces the default value in the Array of the Block.
- Any missing values in Array slots are replaced by default values from the Array of the Block.

#### Automatic instantiation of the implementation Class

Simulation Toolkit supports automatic instantiation of an implementation Class, if the super Class is abstract (Is Abstract = true).

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If there are multiple implementation Classes, Simulation Toolkit will randomly choose one.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Automatic instantiation of the implementation Class if the super Class is abstract.

1427265109

**Related pages**

- Animation of a State Machine Simulation
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Classifier Behavior property' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624733 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00298: (2026x) Classifier Behavior property

- page_id: `255624733`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624733/2026x+Classifier+Behavior+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:57.851+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models > (2026x) Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['classifier-behavior-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A classifier Behavior property is a property to which the stereotype «ClassifierBehaviorProperty» is applied. The value of a classifier Behavior property is a Behavior simulation of the classifier Behavior of an object. Therefore, the value of the classifier Behavior property exists only after the Behavior of the object has been started (See [ATTACHMENT filename='execute_objectwithAdjunct.png'].). The block **Integrator** has a classifier Behavior property typed by the activity **Integrator**. You will see the Behavior simulation as the value of the classifier Behavior in the **Variables** pane.

#### NOTE: Note: Support multiple inherited Classifier Behaviors

false

Note: Support multiple inherited Classifier Behaviors

When a block inherits from multiple other blocks which have a classifier Behavior (CB) only one of them is run instead of all. In the figure below, block A inherits from block B and block C, however, when running the simulation, only block A Behavior is simulated. The Behaviors C and B will be not simulated.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Block A inheriting from multiple blocks, B and C.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Simulation console messages showing only Block A is run.

If the specialized block does not have a CB, all inherited should be run. In the figure below, System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 Behaviors are simulated as asynchronous sessions because the SubSystem block does not have a Classifier Behavior.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Block SubSystem without any inherited Classifier Behavior.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Simulation console messages showing Block System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 all running asynchronously.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A classifier Behavior property is a property to which the stereotype «ClassifierBehaviorProperty» is applied. The value of a classifier Behavior property is a Behavior simulation of the classifier Behavior of an object. Therefore, the value of the classifier Behavior property exists only after the Behavior of the object has been started (See <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="execute_objectwithAdjunct.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Adjunct property" /></ri:attachment><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Executing an Object with Adjunct and Classifier Behavior Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.). The block <strong>Integrator</strong> has a classifier Behavior property typed by the activity <strong>Integrator</strong>. You will see the Behavior simulation as the value of the classifier Behavior in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. </p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5bbc7b60-500d-439e-b736-ff623d26e3d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="icon">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note: Support multiple inherited Classifier Behaviors</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When a block inherits from multiple other blocks which have a classifier Behavior (CB) only one of them is run instead of all. In the figure below, block A inherits from block B and block C, however, when running the simulation, only block A Behavior is simulated. The Behaviors C and B will be not simulated.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Block A inheriting from multiple blocks, B and C.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-b.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Simulation console messages showing only Block A is run.</h6><p>If the specialized block does not have a CB, all inherited should be run. In the figure below, System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 Behaviors are simulated as asynchronous sessions because the SubSystem block does not have a Classifier Behavior.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-c.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Block SubSystem without any inherited Classifier Behavior.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-5874-d.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Classifier Behavior property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Simulation console messages showing Block System1, System2, Super1 and Super2 all running asynchronously.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p> </p>
````

- page_id: `255626269`

33

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1009470184

A Combined fragment is a combination (expression) of Interaction fragments defined by an Interaction operator and corresponding Interaction operands. A Combined fragment may have Interaction constraints, also called **guards**.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three major Interaction operators: **Alternative (alt)**,**Option (opt)**, and**Loop (loop)**. Combined fragments with various Interaction operators will also be supported (e.g., alt inside loop, opt inside loop, loop inside alt, etc.).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Alternative (alt), Option (opt), and Loop (loop) are supported in Interaction fragments.

##### Alternative (alt)

The Interaction operator **alt** signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior. Only one of the operands will be chosen. The chosen operand must have an explicit or implicit Guard expression evaluated as true at a particular point in the Interaction. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.

An operand guarded by **else** means a Guard that is the negation of the dis-junction of all other Guards. If none of the operands has a Guard evaluated true, none of the operands are executed, and the remainder of the enclosing Interaction fragment is executed.

##### Option (opt)

The Interaction operator **opt** signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior where either the (sole) operand happens or nothing happens. An option is semantically equivalent to an alternative combined fragment where there is one operand with non-empty content, and the second operand is empty. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.

##### Loop (loop)

The Interaction operator **loop** signifies that the combined fragment represents a loop. The loop operand will be repeated a number of times. Only an Interaction constraint (not iteration bounds), a Boolean expression shown in square brackets that guards an operand in a combined fragment, is supported.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Any Guard that is not Boolean expression evaluation will show a **Question** dialog box to set the evaluation result true or false. See also [using Guards on Transitions](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Using+Guards+on+Transitions).

382168754

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Using Guards on Transitions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Operators' space='']

A Combined fragment may have Interaction constraints, also called <strong>guards</strong>.</p><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three major Interaction operators: <strong>Alternative (alt)</strong>,<strong> Option (opt)</strong>, and<strong> Loop (loop)</strong>. Combined fragments with various Interaction operators will also be supported (e.g., alt inside loop, opt inside loop, loop inside alt, etc.).</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Combined Fragment.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Combined fragment" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Alternative (alt), Option (opt), and Loop (loop) are supported in Interaction fragments.</h6><h3 class="Textbody">Alternative (alt)</h3><p class="Textbody">The Interaction operator <strong>alt</strong> signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior. Only one of the operands will be chosen. The chosen operand must have an explicit or implicit Guard expression evaluated as true at a particular point in the Interaction. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.</p><p class="Textbody">An operand guarded by <strong>else</strong> means a Guard that is the negation of the dis-junction of all other Guards. If none of the operands has a Guard evaluated true, none of the operands are executed, and the remainder of the enclosing Interaction fragment is executed.</p><h3 class="Textbody">Option (opt)</h3><p class="Textbody">The Interaction operator <strong>opt</strong> signifies that the combined fragment represents a choice of Behavior where either the (sole) operand happens or nothing happens. An option is semantically equivalent to an alternative combined fragment where there is one operand with non-empty content, and the second operand is empty. An implicit ‘true’ Guard is applied if the operand has no Guard.</p><h3 class="Textbody">Loop (loop)</h3><p class="Textbody">The Interaction operator <strong>loop</strong> signifies that the combined fragment represents a loop. The loop operand will be repeated a number of times. Only an Interaction constraint (not iteration bounds), a Boolean expression shown in square brackets that guards an operand in a combined fragment, is supported.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7efb0106-a9af-4c09-82c0-ad1ae096e047"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Any Guard that is not Boolean expression evaluation will show a <strong>Question</strong> dialog box to set the evaluation result true or false. See also <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Using+Guards+on+Transitions">using Guards on Transitions</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="094456de-17cd-4df2-a13d-e1061251eaf4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">382168754</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Using Guards on Transitions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Operators" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626331 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00300: (2026x) Communicating with evaluators through simulation console

- page_id: `255626331`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626331/2026x+Communicating+with+evaluators+through+simulation+console
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:39.436+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['communicating-with-parametric-evaluator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

468495788

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

468495790

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

468495789

You can communicate with a parametric evaluator directly through the command prompt, which is located in the lower part of the **Simulation****Console** pane. You can click the arrow of the language selection drop-down list to the right of the command prompt and select a language you want. Once selected, the language will appear in the command prompt in the **Console** pane. For example, if you select **Matlab**, the language in the command prompt will change from "**»**" to "**matlab**»" (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selecting a Language for the Command Prompt.

You can enter an expression or a command, which is written in the syntax of the language you selected, in the command prompt and press the **Enter** key on your keyboard to simulate it (see the following figure).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Executing Expression in the Command Prompt.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can also use the command prompt in the **Simulation****Console** pane to communicate with the script engine by selecting a scripting language from the language selection drop-down list. For example, selecting **JavaScript** will cause the language to change to "js»".

468495787

Once selected, the language will appear in the command prompt in the <strong>Console</strong> pane. For example, if you select <strong>Matlab</strong>, the language in the command prompt will change from &quot;<strong>»</strong>&quot; to &quot;<strong>matlab</strong>»&quot; (see the following figure). </p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Matlab.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Communicating with evaluators through simulation console" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Selecting a Language for the Command Prompt.</h6><p>You can enter an expression or a command, which is written in the syntax of the language you selected, in the command prompt and press the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Enter</strong> key on your keyboard to simulate it (see the following figure). </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Executing Expression in the Command Prompt" ac:alt="Executing Expression in the Command Prompt"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110247_matlab_2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Communicating with evaluators through simulation console" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Executing Expression in the Command Prompt.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="30c33f8f-c02a-498f-b721-b701ff709d8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also use the command prompt in the <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Console</strong> pane to communicate with the script engine by selecting a scripting language from the language selection drop-down list. For example, selecting <strong>JavaScript</strong> will cause the language to change to &quot;js»&quot;. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c38a375f-080e-4bd3-a43e-e134ff5fef55"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">468495787</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specifying the language for the expression" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Value binding" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Evaluation with causality" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dynamic constraint" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Built-in Math" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Integration with external Evaluators" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Trade study analysis" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sample project" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625652 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00301: (2026x) Completion Events and Transitions

- page_id: `255625652`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625652/2026x+Completion+Events+and+Transitions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:53.967+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) State Machine simulation > (2026x) Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['completion-events', 'transitions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

122387409

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

122387411

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

122387410

Completion Events are standard UML Events which are fired during the execution of a State Machine diagram. For composite or submachine States, a completion Event will be generated when all internal Activities, e.g., **entry** and **doActivity** Behaviors, have completed execution under either of the following circumstances

- If the State is a composite State, all its orthogonal Regions have reached a FinalState.
- if the State is a submachine State, the submachine StateMachine execution has reached a FinalState.

#### TIP: TIP

TIP

You can enable/disable the **Completion Events and Transitions** option in the **SCXML Engine** group in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Project options' space='']** dialog for completion Events and Transitions support.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Completion Events and Transitions option in the SCXML Engine group in the Project Options dialog.

**Case 1**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Completion Events and Transitions in a State Machine diagram.

For example, in the above State Machine diagram, a Transition from the "on" State to the "off" State does not happen until a completion Event is generated. The completion Event is generated after the DoActivity Behavior completes and only then the State transits from "on" to "off".

**Case 2**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Completion Events and Transitions in a State Machine diagram.

In the above State Machine diagram, a completion Event is generated after the entry and sendOff Behaviors have completed.

**Case 3**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Completion Events and Transitions in a State Machine diagram.

In the above State Machine diagram, a completion Event will be generated after the entry and working Behaviors have completed.

#### INFO: Information

Information

Completion Events have dispatch priority over all other Events. They are put into the beginning of the Event queue.

122387408

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State Machine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MT']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625365 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00302: (2026x) Connecting to a running MATLAB session

- page_id: `255625365`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625365/2026x+Connecting+to+a+running+MATLAB+session
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:42.775+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator > (2026x) Integration with external Evaluators > (2026x) Integration with MATLAB
- labels: ['connecting-to-matlab-session', 'matlab-session']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

67934221

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

67934236

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

67934226

Simulation Toolkit can make connection to a running MATLAB session, thus all variables in the workspace can be used.

To connect Simulation Toolkit to a running MATLAB session through sharing

- Execute the matlab.engine.shareEngine command through the MATLAB Command window.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- before the connection is made.
- If the sharing command shown above is not executed, the other MATLAB session loaded by Simulation Toolkit will be used instead of the shared one.

After the session connection, Simulation Toolkit can access variables in the MATLAB shared workspace that are not in the SysML model. You can define some variables in MATLAB, e.g., *m1* and *m2* and solve parametric in Simulation Toolkit. These two groups of variables are then merged and can be accessed in MATLAB as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Variables in the shared MATLAB workspace can be used in either Simulation Toolkit or MATLAB.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e5c09506-5247-4fff-a7d1-4a63aa3bc831"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">67934221</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc95e30f-7a25-4997-96c9-2d8266d8f1d6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">67934236</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc35c9fb-7374-4a80-b011-c4cf9b4fe8b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">67934226</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Simulation Toolkit can make connection to a running MATLAB session, thus all variables in the workspace can be used.</p><p><br /></p><p>To connect Simulation Toolkit to a running MATLAB session through sharing</p><hr /><ul><li>Execute the <strong>matlab.engine.shareEngine</strong> command through the MATLAB Command window.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5593dd7f-5918-44cd-abcc-be3c85858b13"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="IntegratingMagicDraworCameoSystemsModelerwithMATLAB%C2%AE(onMicrosoftWindowsorMacOSX)"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[MATLAB must be integrated successfully]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> before the connection is made.</li><li>If the sharing command shown above is not executed, the other MATLAB session loaded by Simulation Toolkit will be used instead of the shared one.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">After the session connection, Simulation Toolkit can access variables in the MATLAB shared workspace that are not in the SysML model. You can define some variables in MATLAB, e.g., <em>m1</em> and <em>m2</em> and solve parametric in Simulation Toolkit. These two groups of variables are then merged and can be accessed in MATLAB as shown in the following figure.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Variables in the Shared MATLAB Workspace.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Connecting to a running MATLAB session" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Variables in the shared MATLAB workspace can be used in either Simulation Toolkit or MATLAB.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625231 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00303: (2026x) Console log's filter options

- page_id: `255625231`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625231/2026x+Console+log+s+filter+options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:36.400+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Simulation console
- labels: ['console-log-filter', 'log-filter-in-console']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

33294084

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

33294086

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

33294085

When running a model, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit always shows a log list in the **Console** pane that records each and every element that is being simulated such as the start and end elements. If you want to narrow down the log lists to display only specific messages, e.g., errors or warning messages, you can use the **Options** button in the **Console** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Console's Options button.

The **Options** button on the Console pane contains four options to filter log reports as follows

| Option | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Debug | Debug, by default, shows all messages. |
| Info | This option only shows information with a priority equivalent to INFO or higher (WARN and ERROR). |
| Warn | This is the default filter option. It only shows information with a priority equivalent to WARN or higher (ERROR). |
| Error | This option only displays information with a priority equivalent to ERROR. |

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- By default, the user print output, the start time, the end time, and the duration of the main Behavior simulation will always be printed.
- Duration Analysis information will be printed in the Debug filter option.

33294083

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Console pane' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

If you want to narrow down the log lists to display only specific messages, e.g., errors or warning messages, you can use the <strong>Options</strong> button in the <strong>Console</strong> pane. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="The Console's Options Button" ac:alt="The Console's Options Button"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110072_ConsoleFilterOptions_new.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Console log's filter options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Console's Options button.</h6><p>The <strong>Options</strong> button on the Console pane contains four options to filter log reports as follows</p><table class="wrapped relative-table" style="width: 60.0942%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 7.6719%;" /><col style="width: 92.3393%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Option</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><strong>Debug</strong> </td><td>Debug, by default, shows all messages.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Info</strong> </td><td>This option only shows information with a priority equivalent to INFO or higher (WARN and ERROR). </td></tr><tr><td><strong>Warn</strong> </td><td><p>This is the default filter option. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625190 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00304: (2026x) Console pane

- page_id: `255625190`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625190/2026x+Console+pane
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:31.803+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Simulation console
- labels: ['console-tab']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

32300809

32300811

32300810

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides the **Simulation** **Console** pane in the **Simulation** window. The **Console** pane displays simulation information during a model simulation including the date and time the simulation engine starts and the date and time the simulation runs and stops.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Runtime information of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit.

The **Console** pane may contain a hyperlink to a model element in a MagicDraw project. During a model simulation, scripts evaluation failures may happen and thus expression evaluation errors occur. If Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit cannot evaluate some scripts in an element, it will create a hyperlink in the **Console** pane to that element in the Containment tree. When you click the link, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will highlight the element in the Containment tree.

The following figure shows a hyperlink resulting from errors in evaluating scripts in the **Console** pane. The link points to the corresponding element in the Containment tree.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A hyperlink to the Element whose scripts cannot be simulated.

The table below shows the function of each button in the **Console** pane

| Button | Name | Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Clear Console | To remove all simulation information displayed in the Console pane. |
|  | Show Runtime Information | To display the runtime information of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit in the Console pane.The runtime information consists of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit version, registered simulation engines, and available scripting engines. |
|  | Options | To filter outputs in the Console pane. There are four filter options: Debug, Info, Warn, and Error (See for more details.). |

The following figure shows the simulation Console 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation console.

32300807

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title="(2026x) Console log's filter options" space='']

- page_id: `255626233`

- labels: ['constraints']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

| Constant | Value |
| --- | --- |
| E | A real value that is closer than any other to e, the base of natural logarithms. |
| PI | A real value that is closer than any other to pi, the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter. |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624749 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00306: (2026x) Constraint Block

- page_id: `255624749`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624749/2026x+Constraint+Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:59.599+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models > (2026x) Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['constraint-block']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A constraint Block is a subtype of a Block. It is a class stereotyped with «ConstraintBlock». It has a constraint with an expression to constrain the values of its constraint parameters. If an object initialized from a constraint Block and a value bound to a constraint parameter of that object is changed, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will evaluate the expression of the constraint. You can see more information about how Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit evaluates an expression in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Evaluating expressions' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A constraint Block is a subtype of a Block. It is a class stereotyped with «ConstraintBlock». It has a constraint with an expression to constrain the values of its constraint parameters. If an object initialized from a constraint Block and a value bound to a constraint parameter of that object is changed, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will evaluate the expression of the constraint. You can see more information about how Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit evaluates an expression in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link>. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626308 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00307: (2026x) Constraints on parts

- page_id: `255626308`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626308/2026x+Constraints+on+parts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:38.202+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['constraints-on-parts']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In a particular system or context where blocks are used, it is possible to add constraints on parts and constraint value ranges. To better explain how it works, see the following example of a car model.

A car model has *front:* *Wheel* and*rear: Wheel*. The diameter of the Wheel block is specified as *diameter: Real value property*. It is possible to add different constraints to different parts; e.g., the front wheel’s diameter is *{diameter < 19}* and rear wheel’s diameter is *{diameter < 20}*.

The following figure demonstrates a Car (block) along with the diameter of Wheel (constraints) applied to the rear, front, and spare wheel (part properties).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A sample model of constraints on part properties.

If the constraints of the block are applied to the part properties through another block with constraints inside, you will get the following results.

1. The value ranges will be evaluated through all of the applied constraints. The constraints may reside in the part properties, main block, or constraint blocks. For example, front wheel {diameter >= 15, diameter < 19} .
2. If a constraint fails during evaluation, a failure message will appear on the owned constraint; e.g., part properties, main block, or constraint blocks In the Simulation Console panel.
3. In the Variables pane, the constraints applied to the part properties appear together with the constraints of its own part properties in {...}. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit highlights failed constraints with a red background and opens the failure message in the tooltip of the applied constraint.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### This figure depicts the constraints failure message in the Console panes andthe details of failed constraints (in red background) that are applied to part properties front : Wheel in the Variables pane.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The diameter of the Wheel block is specified as <em>diameter: Real value property</em>. It is possible to add different constraints to different parts; <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5ee9814a-9b94-4782-9652-50c8cb08a72b">e.g., the front whe</ac:inline-comment-marker>el’s diameter is <em>{diameter &lt; 19}</em> and rear wheel’s <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="66357b79-e0ed-4a59-837e-67e30bf20b09">diameter</ac:inline-comment-marker> is <em>{diameter &lt; 20}</em>.</p><p class="Standard">The following figure demonstrates a Car (block) along with the diameter of Wheel (constraints) applied to the rear, front, and spare wheel (part properties).</p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="An example of constraints on the part properties" ac:alt="An example of constraints on the part properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraints_onParts1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Constraints on parts" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">A sample model of constraints on part properties.</h6><p class="Standard"> </p><p class="Standard">If the constraints of the block are applied to the part properties through another block with constraints inside, you will get the following results.</p><ol><li>The value ranges will be evaluated through all of the applied constraints. The constraints may reside in the part properties, main block, or constraint blocks. For example, front wheel <em>{diameter &gt;= 15, diameter &lt; 19}</em>.</li><li>If a constraint fails during evaluation, a failure message will appear on the owned constraint; e.g., part properties, main block, or constraint blocks In the Simulation Console panel.</li><li>In the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, the constraints applied to the part properties appear together with the constraints of its own part properties in {...}.
