<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625395 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00308: (2026x) Context, runtime object, and runtime value

- page_id: `255625395`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625395/2026x+Context+runtime+object+and+runtime+value
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:43.416+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['context', 'runtime-object', 'runtime-value', 'simulation-context']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Structural feature

35169272

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Structural feature

35169274

35169271

**On this page**

2

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Structural feature

35169273

When you are executing a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit creates the context, runtime objects, and runtime values to store the simulated values of the model.

#### **Context**

A simulation session is always associated with its context of simulation. The context of a simulation session is a Class or one of its subtypes. When a context element is simulated, a runtime object (of the context's type) will be created to store the runtime values. In the following figure, the context of the selected simulation session is the Calculator class.

#### **Runtime object**

A runtime object is the simulated value of a Class. In other words, it is a runtime instance of a Class and of the context as well. In the following figure, the runtime object of the simulation session context is the "Calculator@6e46ab00" instance. Since the runtime instance is the "Calculator" Class type, it can contain structural features (which correspond to the Class attributes), such as "display" and "operand1".

#### **Runtime value**

A runtime value refers to the value of the structural features mentioned in the Runtime object section, e.g., "200" and "120". However, if the type of a structural feature is a Classifier, its runtime value can also refer to another runtime object of a structural feature type.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Simulation console showing the context "Class, the runtime object of the simulation session context "Calculator@6e46ab00", and the runtime values of "120" and "200".

#### Glossary

#### EXPAND: Class

Class

Class is a Classifier that describes a set of objects that share the same features, constraints, and semantics (meaning). A class is the most widely used Classifier. It is shown as a solid-outline rectangle containing the Class name and with optional compartments separated by horizontal lines containing features or other members of the Classifier.

#### EXPAND: Classifier

Classifier

Classifier is a category of UML elements that have some common features, e.g., attributes and methods. It describes a set of instances that have common behavioral and structural features (operations and attributes respectively).

#### EXPAND: Element

Element

Element is the abstract root UML metaclass, it has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library.

#### EXPAND: Instance

Instance

Instance is a system's entity; it is a concrete manifestation (implementation) of an abstraction. Abstraction could be represented by one or more Classifiers or no Classifiers at all. When an instance manifests a Classifier, it is called instance of that Classifier. For example, Object is an instance of a Class, while the link is an instance of an association. An instance could have a name or be anonymous when the name is not important. The instance name allows you to distinguish it from the other instances within the same naming context (scope).

#### EXPAND: Object

Object

Object is an instance of a Class. It is an individual (thing) with a State and relationships to other objects. The State of an object identifies the values for that object of properties of the Classifier of the object.

#### EXPAND: Structural feature

Structural feature

Structural feature is a typed feature of a Classifier that specifies the structure of instances of the classifier. It specifies that instances of the featuring classifier have a slot whose value or values are of a specified type.

#### Sample model

[ATTACHMENT filename='Calculator.mdzip']250

35825649

**Related pages**

To learn more about working with context, runtime objects, and runtime values, you can visit the following pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating values and objects in the variables pane' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Classifiers' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Recording Verification status of runtime values' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72d05045-adbe-49ab-af30-52fb4609c235"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169272</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f525946f-98ef-43a3-8467-e1b0c0a53d91"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169274</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e75edad2-6584-4a5c-90e5-8eaef80c5b5f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169271</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c01a964c-5980-4d8c-8782-32395823ed02"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4fa895f0-2b9d-46f4-bcf9-2849374f82c4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35169273</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>When you are executing a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit creates the context, runtime objects, and runtime values to store the simulated values of the model. </p><h2><strong>Context</strong> </h2><p>A simulation session is always associated with its context of simulation. The context of a simulation session is a Class or one of its subtypes. When a context element is simulated, a runtime object (of the context's type) will be created to store the runtime values. In the following figure, the context of the selected simulation session is the Calculator class. </p><h2><strong>Runtime object</strong> </h2><p>A runtime object is the simulated value of a Class. In other words, it is a runtime instance of a Class and of the context as well. In the following figure, the runtime object of the simulation session context is the &quot;<span>Calculator@6e46ab00</span>&quot; instance. Since the runtime instance is the &quot;Calculator&quot; Class type, it can contain structural features (which correspond to the Class attributes), such as &quot;display&quot; and &quot;operand1&quot;. </p><h2><strong>Runtime value</strong> </h2><p>A runtime value refers to the value of the structural features mentioned in the Runtime object section, e.g., &quot;200&quot; and &quot;120&quot;. However, if the type of a structural feature is a Classifier, its runtime value can also refer to another runtime object of a structural feature type. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Simulation console" ac:alt="Simulation console"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Variable_panel.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Simulation console showing the context &quot;Class, the runtime object of the simulation session context &quot;Calculator@<span style="color: rgb(0,51,102);">6e46ab00</span>&quot;, and the runtime values of &quot;120&quot; and &quot;200&quot;.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h2>Glossary</h2><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68addeff-6845-48c9-adf9-660dd5124a75"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Class</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Class is a Classifier that describes a set of objects that share the same features, constraints, and semantics (meaning). A class is the most widely used Classifier. It is shown as a solid-outline rectangle containing the Class name and with optional compartments separated by horizontal lines containing features or other members of the Classifier.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa5e5f7e-7c99-4ce4-be31-1555c1218a51"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Classifier</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Classifier is a category of UML elements that have some common features, e.g., attributes and methods. It describes a set of instances that have common behavioral and structural features (operations and attributes respectively).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60e5d5fa-c507-48d7-889c-5a8d77526cc6"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Element</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Element is the abstract root UML metaclass, it has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="269574b7-688a-4b59-a254-030d72a81014"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Instance</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Instance is a system's entity; it is a concrete manifestation (implementation) of an abstraction. Abstraction could be represented by one or more Classifiers or no Classifiers at all. When an instance manifests a Classifier, it is called instance of that Classifier. For example, Object is an instance of a Class, while the link is an instance of an association. An instance could have a name or be anonymous when the name is not important. The instance name allows you to distinguish it from the other instances within the same naming context (scope).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="054be2d9-e123-43f4-8f12-5669c19e2340"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Object</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Object is an instance of a Class. It is an individual (thing) with a State and relationships to other objects. The State of an object identifies the values for that object of properties of the Classifier of the object.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0656aaec-9163-439e-b209-356ae3a177fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Structural feature</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Structural feature is a typed feature of a Classifier that specifies the structure of instances of the classifier. It specifies that instances of the featuring classifier have a slot whose value or values are of a specified type.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h2>Sample model</h2><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="view-file" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b66ade8a-1197-48fa-9140-1f2352da234b"><ac:parameter ac:name="name"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Calculator.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /></ri:attachment></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">250</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3eaaba6-2e83-48de-aaa9-3ca5ca1722e4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">35825649</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p>To learn more about working with context, runtime objects, and runtime values, you can visit the following pages</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating values and objects in the variables pane" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Automatic initialization of context and runtime objects" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Recording Verification status of runtime values" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625491 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00309: (2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity

- page_id: `255625491`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625491/2026x+Creating+increaseTime+Operation+and+increaseTime+Activity
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:47.509+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model > (2026x) Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An **increaseTime** operation is necessary for the StopWatch operation. It is used to increase the value of time by 1 whenever the operation is called. Therefore, we need to create an operation and Activity for the **StopWatch** Class to define this operation the same as we did for a resetTime operation and resetTime Activity.

To create an operation and an Activity for the StopWatch

1. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Operation .
2. Name the new operation "increaseTime" .
3. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Activity .
4. Name the new Activity "increaseTime" .
5. Right-click the increaseTime Activity in the containment browser and select New Diagram > Activity Diagram to add an Activity diagram to the increaseTime Activity. The new Activity diagram will be created under the increaseTime Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is "increaseTime" .

To increase the time values, the increaseTime Activity will do the following 
(ii) get the object of the StopWatch using a ReadSelfAction 
 (ii) obtain the current time value using the ReadStructuralFeatureAction 
 (iii) increment the time value by one using an OpaqueBehaviorAction 
 (iv) set the increased time value to the object of the StopWatch using AddStructuralFeatureValueAction 
 (v) set the specification of the IncreaseTime Activity to the IncreaseTime Operation 
 
**(i) Getting****the****object****of****the****StopWatch****using****a****ReadSelfAction**

The Object of the StopWatch, which contains the time value, must be obtained by using a ReadSelfAction. To create the ReadSelfAction, you can follow the same steps used to create the ReadSelfAction in the **resetTime** Activity. In this example, the Object, which is the value of the output pin of the ReadSelfAction, needs to be supplied to multiple Actions (ReadStructuralFeatureAction and AddStructuralFeatureValueAction that will be created later). Therefore, a Fork must be added to this Activity. 
 
To add a Fork to an Activity

1. Click Fork Vertical on the Activity diagram toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavf6bb2ee026c1f5da69ac93853fd47cae.png']
2. Click the increaseTime Activity diagram to create a Fork.
3. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the fork and the self output pin. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavd71668fa7e06ac9f3a537f68d7ef8f4a.png']

**(ii)****Obtaining****the****current****time****value u****sing****a****ReadStructuralFeatureAction**

Once you have obtained the object of the Stopwatch, you will need to get the time value.

To get the current time value using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction

1. Click Action > Any Action on the Activity diagram toolbar. The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open.
2. Select ReadStructuralFeatureAction and click OK .
3. Click the increaseTime Activity diagram. A new ReadStructuralFeatureAction will be created on the diagram.
4. Right-click the ReadStructuralFeatureAction symbol on the diagram and select Specification to open its Specification window. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action.png']
5. Select the Structural Feature row and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select Property dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-property-a.png']
6. Select the Attribute time:Integer[1] of the StopWatch Class and click the OK button.
7. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Fork and the self input pin of ReadStructuralFeatureAction. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavda542b6684ebbe2004005e0d7f47ee80.png']

**(iii)****Incrementing****the****time v****alue****by o****ne****using****an****Opaque Behavior Action**

We need an OpaqueBehaviorAction to increase the time value. An OpaqueBehaviorAction receives an integer value as its input parameter and returns the integer value, which is the value of the input parameter that has been incremented by one. 
 
To create an Opaque Behavior for the **increaseTime**Activity

1. Right-click the increaseTime Activity in the containment browser and select New Element > Opaque Behavior . A new Opaque Behavior will be created under the increaseTime Activity.
2. Name it "increase" .
3. Right-click the increase Opaque Behavior in the containment browser and select Specification to open its Specification window.
4. Select the Parameters node in the Specification window and click the Create button . A new parameter will be created for the increase Opaque Behavior, and the Parameter Specification window will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-opaque-behavior.png']
5. Name the parameter "ti" , select Integer as its type, and select in as its direction. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-parameter.png']
6. Click the Back button to return to the Specification window of the increase Opaque Behavior.
7. Repeat the same steps used to create the parameter ti to create another parameter, which will be a return parameter of the increase Opaque Behavior.
8. Name the return parameter "to" , select Integer as its type, and select return as its direction. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-parameter-2.png']
9. Click the Back button to return to the Specification window of the increase Opaque Behavior.

Once the parameters "ti" and "to" have been created, we will write a simple JavaScript that will be executed during the simulation. 
 
To create a simple JavaScript to be executed in the simulation

1. Open the increase Opaque Behavior's Specification window and select the increase Opaque Behavior node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
2. Type a JavaScript into the **Body** row, e.g., *"to=ti+1"*. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] NoteYou can use any scripting language that is supported by JSR-223. To specify the scripting language that you are going to use, type it directly in the **Language** row. JavaScript will be used by default if no scripting language is specified.
3. Click Close to close the Specification window.
4. Create an OpaqueBehaviorAction on the increaseTime Activity by dragging the created increase Opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the increaseTime Activity diagram. A new OpaqueBehaviorAction will be created with the pins that correspond to the defined parameters ti and to .
5. Name the created OpaqueBehaviorAction "inc".
6. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the output pin t of the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the input pin ti of the inc:increase OpaqueBehaviorAction . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddava7acc324d5817b8f773c8d3f58b0a8d1.png']

**(iv) Setting****the i****ncreased****time****value****to the****object****of****StopWatch****using****AddStructuralFeatureValueAction**

Once the time value has been increased using the **inc:increase** OpaqueBehaviorAction, you need to set it back to the Object of **StopWatch**. To do this, you can follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the **resetTime** Activity. 
 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To set the increased time value to the object of StopWatch

1. Follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the resetTime Activity diagram.
2. Get the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value to the time:Integer of StopWatch. Make sure that the Is Replace All of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction is set to true .
3. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect from the output pin to of inc:increase OpaqueBehaviorAction to the input pin t of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction (see the steps required for resetting the time value of the StopWatch object to zero with AddStructuralFeatureValueAction in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity' space=''] ).
4. Add an Initial Node and an Activity Final stage to the increaseTime Activity and click Control Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect between the Actions. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavff9d9f7f43a5f3ceeb2fce67dc48f4af.png']

**(v)****Setting****the****specification****of****the i****ncrease time****Activity****to the****IncreaseTime****Operation**

To set the specification of the increaseTime Activity to the increaseTime operation

1. Open the Specification window of the increaseTime Activity by right-clicking the increaseTime Activity in the containment browser and select Specification .
2. Select the Specification row and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select Element dialog will open.
3. Select the increaseTime operation of the StopWatch Class as the specification of the Activity.
4. Click OK to close the Select Element dialog.
5. Click Close to close the Specification window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An <strong>increaseTime</strong> operation is necessary for the StopWatch operation. It is used to increase the value of time by 1 whenever the operation is called. Therefore, we need to create an operation and Activity for the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class to define this operation the same as we did for a resetTime operation and resetTime Activity.</p><p>To create an operation and an Activity for the StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Operation</strong>.</li><li>Name the new operation <em>&quot;increaseTime&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">New Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Activity</strong>.</li><li>Name the new Activity <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;increaseTime&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">New Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Activity</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> to add an Activity diagram to the increaseTime Activity. The new Activity diagram will be created under the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;increaseTime&quot;</strong>.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To increase the time values, the increaseTime Activity will do the following <br class="atl-forced-newline" />(ii) get the object of the StopWatch using a ReadSelfAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (ii) obtain the current time value using the ReadStructuralFeatureAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (iii) increment the time value by one using an OpaqueBehaviorAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (iv) set the increased time value to the object of the StopWatch using AddStructuralFeatureValueAction <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (v) set the specification of the IncreaseTime Activity to the IncreaseTime Operation <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(i) Getting </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>object </strong><strong>of </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>StopWatch </strong><strong>using </strong><strong>a </strong><strong>ReadSelfAction</strong></p><p>The Object of the StopWatch, which contains the time value, must be obtained by using a ReadSelfAction. To create the ReadSelfAction, you can follow the same steps used to create the ReadSelfAction in the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. In this example, the Object, which is the value of the output pin of the ReadSelfAction, needs to be supplied to multiple Actions (ReadStructuralFeatureAction and AddStructuralFeatureValueAction that will be created later). Therefore, a Fork must be added to this Activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To add a Fork to an Activity</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Fork </strong><strong>Vertical</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Fork Vertical Button on the Activity Diagram Toolbar" ac:alt="The Fork Vertical Button on the Activity Diagram Toolbar"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavf6bb2ee026c1f5da69ac93853fd47cae.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the increaseTime Activity diagram to create a Fork.</li><li>Click <strong>Object</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the fork and the <strong>self</strong> output pin.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Connecting the Output Pin and Fork" ac:alt="Connecting the Output Pin and Fork"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavd71668fa7e06ac9f3a537f68d7ef8f4a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p><br /><strong>(ii) </strong><strong>Obtaining </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>current </strong><strong>time </strong><strong>value u</strong><strong>sing </strong><strong>a </strong><strong>ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong></p><p>Once you have obtained the object of the Stopwatch, you will need to get the time value.</p><p>To get the current time value using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong>MetaClass</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Property</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Selecting a Structural Feature in the Select Property Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting a Structural Feature in the Select Property Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-property-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the Attribute <strong>time:Integer[1]</strong> of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class and click the <strong>OK</strong> button.</li><li>Click <strong>Object</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Fork and the <strong>self</strong> input pin of ReadStructuralFeatureAction.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Connecting the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the Fork" ac:alt="Connecting the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the Fork"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavda542b6684ebbe2004005e0d7f47ee80.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(iii) </strong><strong>Incrementing </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>time v</strong><strong>alue </strong><strong>by o</strong><strong>ne </strong><strong>using </strong><strong>an </strong><strong>Opaque Behavior Action</strong></p><p>We need an OpaqueBehaviorAction to increase the time value. An OpaqueBehaviorAction receives an integer value as its input parameter and returns the integer value, which is the value of the input parameter that has been incremented by one. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create an Opaque Behavior for the <strong>increaseTime </strong>Activity</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Opaque </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Behavior</strong>. A new Opaque Behavior will be created under the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> Activity.</li><li>Name it <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;increase&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increase</strong> Opaque Behavior in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Parameters</strong> node in the Specification window and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create</strong> button . A new parameter will be created for the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increase</strong> Opaque Behavior, and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Parameter </strong>Specification window will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating Parameters for the Opaque Behavior" ac:alt="Creating Parameters for the Opaque Behavior"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Name the parameter <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;ti&quot;</em>, select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong> as its type, and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">in</strong> as its direction.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specification of parameter" ac:alt="Specification of parameter"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-parameter.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li> Click the <strong>Back</strong> button to return to the Specification window of the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior.</li><li>Repeat the same steps used to create the parameter <strong>ti</strong> to create another parameter, which will be a return parameter of the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior.</li><li>Name the return parameter <em>&quot;to&quot;</em>, select <strong>Integer</strong> as its type, and select <strong>return</strong> as its direction.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-parameter-2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Back</strong> button to return to the Specification window of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increase</strong> Opaque Behavior. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once the parameters &quot;ti&quot; and &quot;to&quot; have been created, we will write a simple JavaScript that will be executed during the simulation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a simple JavaScript to be executed in the simulation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior's Specification window and select the <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li><p>Type a JavaScript into the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Body</strong> row, e.g., <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;to=ti+1&quot;</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Typing a JavaScript for the Increase Opaque Behavior" ac:alt="Typing a JavaScript for the Increase Opaque Behavior"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior-2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfffd00c-b473-40d1-9048-d244bd880c3a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can use any scripting language that is supported by JSR-223. To specify the scripting language that you are going to use, type it directly in the <strong>Language</strong> row. JavaScript will be used by default if no scripting language is specified.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong> to close the Specification window. </li><li>Create an OpaqueBehaviorAction on the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity by dragging the created <strong>increase</strong> Opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram. A new OpaqueBehaviorAction will be created with the pins that correspond to the defined parameters <strong>ti</strong> and <strong>to</strong>.</li><li>Name the created OpaqueBehaviorAction &quot;inc&quot;.</li><li>Click <strong>Object</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the output pin <strong>t</strong> of the ReadStructuralFeatureAction and the input pin <strong>ti</strong> of the <strong>inc:increase</strong> OpaqueBehaviorAction .<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Adding the Increase Opaque Behavior to the IncreaseTime Activity" ac:alt="Adding the Increase Opaque Behavior to the IncreaseTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddava7acc324d5817b8f773c8d3f58b0a8d1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(iv) Setting </strong><strong>the i</strong><strong>ncreased </strong><strong>time </strong><strong>value </strong><strong>to the </strong><strong>object </strong><strong>of </strong><strong>StopWatch </strong><strong>using </strong><strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong></p><p>Once the time value has been increased using the <strong>inc:increase</strong> OpaqueBehaviorAction, you need to set it back to the Object of <strong>StopWatch</strong>. To do this, you can follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Adding an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to Set the Increased Time Value" ac:alt="Adding an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to Set the Increased Time Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav66cd1d3f270c7771a4959ddbeac8863f.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /></p><p>To set the increased time value to the object of StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Follow the same steps used to create the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram.</li><li>Get the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value to the time:Integer of StopWatch. Make sure that the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Is</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Replace</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">All</strong> of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction is set to <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">true</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Object</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect from the output pin <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">to</strong> of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">inc:increase</strong> OpaqueBehaviorAction to the input pin <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">t</strong> of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction (see the steps required for resetting the time value of the StopWatch object to zero with AddStructuralFeatureValueAction in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ac:link>).</li><li>Add an Initial Node and an Activity Final stage to the increaseTime Activity and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Control</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Flow </strong>on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect between the Actions. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="A Complete Model of the IncreaseTime Activity" ac:alt="A Complete Model of the IncreaseTime Activity" ac:height="430" ac:width="475"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavff9d9f7f43a5f3ceeb2fce67dc48f4af.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>(v)</strong><strong>Setting </strong><strong>the </strong><strong>specification </strong><strong>of </strong><strong>the i</strong><strong>ncrease time </strong><strong>Activity </strong><strong>to the </strong><strong>IncreaseTime </strong><strong>Operation</strong><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To set the specification of the increaseTime Activity to the increaseTime operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity by right-clicking the <strong>increaseTime </strong>Activity in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong>.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> row and click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">increaseTime</strong> operation of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class as the specification of the Activity.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong> to close the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> dialog.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> to close the Specification window.</li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624626 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00310: (2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation

- page_id: `255624626`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624626/2026x+Creating+a+model+for+Activity+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:51.618+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation
- labels: ['model-for-activity-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

142598154

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

142598157

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

142598156

You can simulate a UML Activity or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Activity. This section demonstrates how to create a simple but executable Activity model through the following steps

1. Create a Class containing two properties typed by Integers.
2. Create an Activity to print the summation value of the two properties.
3. Assign the Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the created Class.
4. Create an opaque Behavior to print the summation value of two input parameters of type Integer .
5. Write a script to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the two input parameters.
6. Complete the Activity diagram of the Class.
7. Create a ReadSelfAction to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of both the readX and readY Actions.
8. Create an InstanceSpecification and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the two created properties.

To create a Class containing two attributes typed by Integers

1. To create a new *UML* project, click **File** > **New Project** on the main menu. The **New Project**dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='cst-new-proj.png']
2. Select **Simulation****Project** from the **Simulation** group and specify the project's name, e.g., "SimpleActivitySimulation".
3. Specify the location where you want to save your project file, and then click **OK**.
4. Right-click the **Model** model in the containment browser and select **Create Element** > **Class**. A new Class element, which is the context of the Activity, will be created in the containment browser. Name the created Class, e.g., "SumPrinter".
5. Add two properties: **x** and **y** of type **Integer**. 
Right-click the **SumPrinter** Class and select **Create Element** > **Property**. Type 'x' to name the property (see the first figure below). Right-click **x** and select **Specification** to open its Specification window. Select **Integer** as the property type (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Creating a new Property x for the SumPrinter Class. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Specification of Property x. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The SumPrinter Class with the Properties X and Y of Integer Type.
6. Repeat Step 5 to create Property **y**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Once the Properties **x** and **y** have been created, define the Behavior of the created Class: Specify the Classifier Behavior of the **SumPrinter**Class with a UML Activity element.

To create an Activity to print the summation value of the two properties

1. Right-click the SumPrinter Class in the containment browser and select New Diagram > Activity Diagram to create a new Activity under it.
2. Name the diagram "PrintSum".

Now that the Activity has been created, you need to assign it as the Classifier Behavior of SumPrinter.

To assign the Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the created Class

1. Right-click the **SumPrinter** Class in the containment browser and select **Specification** to open its Specification window. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select All from the Properties drop-down menu to make sure that all of the properties are listed in the dialog.
3. Click Classifier Behavior and select the PrintSum Activity from the drop-down list on the right-hand side.

To create an opaque Behavior to print the summation value of the two input parameters of type **Integer**:

1. Right-click the Model model in the Containment tree and select Create Element > Opaque Behavior . A new opaque Behavior will be created under the Model model.
2. Name it "PrintSumOfIntegers". The following figure shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment browser. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Add two input parameters of type Integer : a and b .
4. Right-click the **PrintSumOfIntegers** opaque Behavior and select **Create****Element**> **Parameter**. Name the created parameter 'a' in the name field and select **Integer**as the type of Parameter **a** (see the following figure that shows the Specification of Parameter **a**). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat Steps 3-4 to create Parameter **b** as shown in the following figure that shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior containing Parameters **a** and **b** in the Containment browser. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To write a script to print the summation of the given integer values

- Open the Specification window of the **PrintSumOfIntegers** opaque Behavior and write a script in the **Body** field (You can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw's Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript will be used to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the parameters **a** and **b**; therefore, the script will be: "print(a+b)". [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If you want to use a scripting language other than JavaScript, you can specify it in the **Language**attribute of the Specification window.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specifying a Scripting Language in the Specification dialog.

The next step is to complete the **PrintSum** Activity diagram of the **SumPrinter** Class and add a **ReadStructuralFeatureAction** so that the values of properties **x** and **y**, which are owned by the **SumPrinter** Class, are readable. The values of **a** and **b** will later be passed on to the **PrintSumOfIntegers** opaque Behavior as the values of input parameters **a** and **b** respectively.

To complete the Activity diagram of the Class

1. Drag the PrintSumOfIntegers opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the PrintSum Activity diagram. A new Action of PrintSumOfIntegers will be created.
2. Name the Action "print". [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Add the Initial and Activity Final nodes to the Activity diagram and connect them to the print Action using a control flow. The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Initial and Final Activity nodes. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click **Action** and select **Any****Action** from the **Activity Diagram** toolbar on the **PrintSum** Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  1. Select **Read Structural Feature****Action** in the **Select Action MetaClass** dialog. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Click the **PrintSum** Activity diagram to create the Action and name it "readX" as shown in the following figure [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Open the Specification window of the Action **readX**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Click the **Structural Feature** and the**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** button to open the **Select Property** dialog to select **Structural Feature**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Select the Property **x** of the **SumPrinter** Class and click **OK**. The **Select Property** dialog will close.
9. Create an object flow from the pin **result** of the **readX** Action to pin **a** of the **print** Action. The following figure shows the Flow between readX and print Actions in the Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Repeat Steps 4 to 10 to create another Action named **readY**, which is the **ReadStructuralFeatureAction**, with the following arrangements **•** The name of the Action is "readY". **•** The structural feature is the attribute 'y' of the **SumPrinter** Class. **•** The name of the output pin of **readY** is 'b'. **•** The output pin **b** of **readY** connects to pin **b** of the **print** Action. The following figure shows the Activity diagram with readX and readY Actions [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a ReadSelfAction to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of **readX** and **readY** Actions

1. Click **Action** > **Any****Action** on the Activity Diagram toolbar. The **Select****Action****MetaClass**dialog will open (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select **Read Self****Action**.
3. Click the **PrintSum** Activity diagram to create an Action and name it, for example, **readSelf.**The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Action readSelf. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Create a **Fork****Horizontal** and use **Object****Flow** to connect it to the pins of the Actions **readX**, **readY**, and **readSelf** on the diagrams. The following figure shows a complete PrintSum Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The final step is to create an **InstanceSpecification** whose Classifier is the **SumPrinter** and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the properties **x** and **y**. These values will be used during the simulation. 
 
To create an **InstanceSpecification** whose Classifier is the **SumPrinter** and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the Properties **x** and **y**

1. Right-click the Model model and select Create Element > InstanceSpecification .
2. Name the created InstanceSpecification, e.g., **instance**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the created **Instance** and open the Specification window of **instance**.
4. Click the Classifier field and the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The **Select Elements** dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Select the **SumPrinter** Class to edit the Classifier and click **OK**.
6. Click **Slots** on the left-hand side pane of the Specification window and select **x:Integer**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Click the **Create****Value** button to create a new value of the slot. The **Value** box will open for you to assign the value to Property x slot. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Type a number, e.g., 2 as the value of the property **x** slot.
9. Repeat Steps 6 to 8 to assign "8" as the value of the property **y** slot as shown in the following figure and click **Close**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The model is now ready for you to simulate.

142598153

The following figure shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment browser.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SetNameOfPrintSumOfIntegers.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </h6></li><li>Add two input parameters of type <strong>Integer</strong>: <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong>.</li><li><p>Right-click the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior and select <strong>Create </strong><strong>Element </strong>&gt; <strong>Parameter</strong>. Name the created parameter 'a' in the name field and select <strong>Integer </strong>as the type of Parameter <strong>a</strong> (see the following figure that shows the Specification of Parameter <strong>a</strong>). </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specification Dialog of Parameter a" ac:alt="Specification Dialog of Parameter a"><ri:attachment ri:filename="zecification-of-parameter-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat Steps 3-4 to create Parameter <strong>b</strong> as shown in the following figure that shows the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior containing Parameters <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> in the Containment browser.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior Containing Parameters a and bin the Containment Browser" ac:alt="PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior Containing Parameters a and bin the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="OpaqueBehaviorWithAB.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p>To write a script to print the summation of the given integer values</p><hr /><ul><li><p>Open the Specification window of the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior and write a script in the <strong>Body</strong> field (You can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw's Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript will be used to print the summation of the given integer values that are referred to by the parameters <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong>; therefore, the script will be: &quot;print(a+b)&quot;. </p><h6><br /><ac:image ac:title="JavaScript forPrinting the Summation ofInteger Values" ac:alt="JavaScript forPrinting the Summation ofInteger Values"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc7b5dd2-f737-4141-89a2-84c4c8aa629a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want to use a scripting language other than JavaScript, you can specify it in the <strong>Language </strong>attribute of the Specification window.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specifying a Scripting Language" ac:alt="Specifying a Scripting Language"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-opaque-behavior-2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6>Specifying a Scripting Language in the Specification dialog.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The next step is to complete the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class and add a <strong>ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong> so that the values of properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong>, which are owned by the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class, are readable. The values of <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> will later be passed on to the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior as the values of input parameters <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> respectively. </p><p><br /></p><p>To complete the Activity diagram of the Class </p><hr /><ol><li>Drag the <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> opaque Behavior from the containment browser to the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram. A new Action of <strong>PrintSumOfIntegers</strong> will be created.</li><li><p>Name the Action &quot;print&quot;. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Dragging the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior totheActivityDiagram toCreate a PrintAction" ac:alt="Dragging the PrintSumOfIntegers Opaque Behavior totheActivityDiagram toCreate a PrintAction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DND_PrintSumOfIntegers_andNamedWithPrint.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Add the Initial and Activity Final nodes to the Activity diagram and connect them to the print Action using a control flow. The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Initial and Final Activity nodes.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with Initial and Final Activity Nodes" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with Initial and Final Activity Nodes"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddInitial_ActivityFinal_to_print.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Action</strong> and select <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action</strong> from the <strong>Activity Diagram</strong> toolbar on the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="SelectingAnyAction from theActivityDiagram Toolbar" ac:alt="SelectingAnyAction from theActivityDiagram Toolbar"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddAnyActionToolbarButton.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><ol><li><p>Select <strong>Read Structural Feature </strong><strong>Action</strong> in the <strong>Select Action MetaClass</strong> dialog. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting ReadStructuralFeatureAction in the SelectAction Metaclass Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting ReadStructuralFeatureAction in the SelectAction Metaclass Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="readStructFeatAction.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li></ol></li><li><p>Click the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram to create the Action and name it &quot;readX&quot; as shown in the following figure</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with Action readX" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with Action readX"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CreateReadXAction.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the Action <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">readX</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Dialog of ReadStructuralFeatureAction readX" ac:alt="The Specification Dialog of ReadStructuralFeatureAction readX"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-read-structural-feature.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Structural Feature</strong> and the<strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2015-12-02 15_31_56-Specification of State __.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> button to open the <strong>Select Property</strong> dialog to select <strong>Structural Feature</strong>.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Property x in the Select Property Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting the Property x in the Select Property Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-read-structural-feature-2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Select the Property <strong>x</strong> of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class and click <strong>OK</strong>. The <strong>Select Property</strong> dialog will close.</p></li><li><p>Create an object flow from the pin <strong>result</strong> of the <strong>readX</strong> Action to pin <strong>a</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> Action. The following figure shows the Flow between readX and print Actions in the Activity diagram.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram Showing the Flow between readX and print Actions" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram Showing the Flow between readX and print Actions"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ConnectReadXToPrint.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Repeat Steps 4 to 10 to create another Action named <strong>readY</strong>, which is the <strong>ReadStructuralFeatureAction</strong>, with the following arrangements</p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The name of the Action is &quot;readY&quot;. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The structural feature is the attribute 'y' of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The name of the output pin of <strong>readY</strong> is 'b'. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"><strong>•</strong></span> The output pin <strong>b</strong> of <strong>readY</strong> connects to pin <strong>b</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> Action.</p><p> The following figure shows the Activity diagram with readX and readY Actions</p><p><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with readX and readY Actions" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with readX and readY Actions"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddReadY.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create a ReadSelfAction to read a runtime object that will be supplied to the input pins of <strong>readX</strong> and <strong>readY</strong> Actions</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action</strong> on the Activity Diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong>MetaClass </strong>dialog will open (see the following figure). </p><p><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Read Self Action from the Select Action Metaclass Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting Read Self Action from the Select Action Metaclass Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="readselfaction.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Select <strong>Read Self</strong><strong>Action</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Click the <strong>PrintSum</strong> Activity diagram to create an Action and name it, for example, <strong>readSelf. </strong>The following figure shows the Activity diagram with Action readSelf.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Activity Diagram with Action readSelf" ac:alt="The Activity Diagram with Action readSelf"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddReadSelfAction.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Create a <strong>Fork </strong><strong>Horizontal</strong> and use <strong>Object </strong><strong>Flow</strong> to connect it to the pins of the Actions <strong>readX</strong>, <strong>readY</strong>, and <strong>readSelf</strong> on the diagrams. The following figure shows a complete PrintSum Activity diagram.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="A Complete PrintSum Activity Diagram" ac:alt="A Complete PrintSum Activity Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CompletePrintSumActivity.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p>The final step is to create an <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong> whose Classifier is the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong>. These values will be used during the simulation.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create an <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong> whose Classifier is the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> and assign the values to the slots that correspond to the Properties <strong>x</strong> and <strong>y</strong> </p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>Model</strong> model and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>InstanceSpecification</strong>.</li><li><p>Name the created InstanceSpecification, e.g., <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">instance</strong>.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="The Created InstanceSpecification in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="The Created InstanceSpecification in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NameInstanceSpecificationWithInstance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Right-click the created <strong>Instance</strong> and open the Specification window of <strong>instance</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Click the Classifier field and the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2015-12-02 15_31_56-Specification of State __.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The <strong>Select Elements</strong> dialog will open.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Select Elements Dialog of the Selected Classifier" ac:alt="The Select Elements Dialog of the Selected Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-elements-dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Select the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Class to edit the Classifier and click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Slots</strong> on the left-hand side pane of the Specification window and select <strong>x:Integer</strong>.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating a Slot Value of x" ac:alt="Creating a Slot Value of x"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-instance-slots.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Create </strong><strong>Value</strong> button to create a new value of the slot. The <strong>Value</strong> box will open for you to assign the value to Property x slot.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Assigning a Value to Property x Slot" ac:alt="Assigning a Value to Property x Slot"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-instance-slots-2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Type a number, e.g., 2 as the value of the property <strong>x</strong> slot.</p></li><li><p>Repeat Steps 6 to 8 to assign &quot;8&quot; as the value of the property <strong>y</strong> slot as shown in the following figure and click <strong>Close</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Created InstanceSpecification with Slot Values in the Containment Tree" ac:alt="The Created InstanceSpecification with Slot Values in the Containment Tree"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InstanceSpecificationOfSumPrinter.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The model is now ready for you to simulate. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c03e720-db13-4a6d-96c6-2f45192eabaa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">142598153</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Class" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Classifier Behavior property" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625007 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00311: (2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation

- page_id: `255625007`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625007/2026x+Creating+a+model+for+interaction+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:16.195+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Interaction simulation
- labels: ['model-for-interaction-simulation', 'interaction-simulation-model']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

384506323

384506325

384506324

You can simulate an Interaction element, a (UML or SysML) Sequence diagram, or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Interaction. This section demonstrates how to create a simple executable Interaction model through the following steps

- Create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes.
- Create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in (i) , for printing the summation of two Integer input parameters.
- Write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters.
- Create an operation, owned by the properties specified in (i) , and specify the opaque Behavior in (ii) as its method.
- Create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in (i) .
- Create call message to call operation specified in (iv) by another property.

classTo create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes

1. To create a new UML project, click **File** > **New Project** on the main menu. The **New Project**dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select UML Project from the General-Purpose Modeling group and specify the project's name, e.g., 'SimpleSequenceExecution'.
3. Specify the location where you want to save your project file and click OK .
4. Right-click the **Model** node in the Containment tree and select **Create** **Element** > **Class**. A new Class element will be created. Name the created Class, e.g., 'System'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Create two more Classes and name them, e.g., 'a' and 'b.
6. Right-click the System model in the Containment tree and select Create Element > Property to create property for the Class. Name the property, e.g., 'a1'. [ATTACHMENT filename='cst-containment-tree-b.png']
7. Right-click **a1** and select the Specification window. Select **A** as the property type. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Repeat step 6 to create property **b1** of type **B** (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']

opaqueTo create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in (), to print the summation of two Integer input parameters

1. Right-click the Class **B** and select **Create Element** > **Opaque** **Behavior**. Name the opaque Behavior, e.g., 'Add'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the opaque Behavior **Add** and select **Create Element** > **Parameter**to add a parameter element to the opaque Behavior. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the parameter **par1** and select the Specification window. Select **Integer** as the parameter type. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Repeat Step 2 to create another parameter and name it, e.g., 'par2' of type **Integer**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

scriptTo write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters

- Open the Specification window of the opaque Behavior **Add** and write a script in the **Body** field (you can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript is used: print(par1+par2); to print the summation of the two Integer parameters **par1** and **par2**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Specification window of an Opaque Behavior.

operationTo create an operation owned by the properties specified in , and to specify the opaque Behavior as in () as its method

1. Right-click **B** and select **Create** **Element** > **Operation**to add an operation element to the Class. Name the operation, e.g., 'Add'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the operation **Add** and select **Create Element** > **Parameter**to add a parameter to the operation element. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the parameter **par1** and select **Specification**. Select **Integer** as the parameter type and **in** as the parameter direction. The Specification window of parameter par1 will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Repeat Step 2 and 3 to create another parameter. Name it, e.g., **par2** of type **Integer**, having **in** as its direction. The parameters associated with the Operation will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Right-click the operation Add and select Specification . The Specification window will open.
6. Select the opaque Behavior **Add** as the **Method** of the operation. The Specification window of the Opaque Behavior will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The parameters associated with an Operation in the Containment tree will be as follows. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in ()

1. Right-click **System** and select **New** **Diagram** > **Sequence** **Diagram**. Select all properties in the **Display Lifelines** dialog and click **OK**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the Class System in the containment browser and select Specification to open its Specification window.
3. Make sure that the Interaction **System** is the Classifier Behavior of the Class **System**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a call message to call the operation specified in () by another property

1. Double-click the Interaction **System** to open the Sequence diagram containing two lifelines: **a1** and **b1**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select **Call** **Message** from the **Diagram Modeling** **Elements** toolbar and create a call message from **a1** to **b1**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']|
3. Double-click the call message created in Step 2 to open the Specification window and select the operation **Add** as the **Signature****(operation)** of the call message. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Select **Argument** on the left-hand side of the dialog to specify a value of the element. Type, e.g., **4** and **5** as the values of parameters **par1** and **par2** respectively. The Specification window of a call message showing its Arguments tags will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src=''] A Sequence diagram showing a call message between two lifelines will be as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']

384506322

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Class' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Supported elements in interaction simulation' space='']
