<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8c14047-83ba-4568-be08-678320259b52"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">384506323</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fde4b27e-0b7d-4ea7-8059-518a6ccb5542"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">384506325</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9cda626-9283-44de-8366-9124e747e921"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">384506324</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can simulate an Interaction element, a (UML or SysML) Sequence diagram, or a Classifier whose Classifier Behavior is defined by an Interaction. This section demonstrates how to create a simple executable Interaction model through the following steps</p><ul><li>Create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes.</li><li>Create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);">(i)</span>, for printing the summation of two Integer input parameters.</li><li>Write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters.</li><li>Create an operation, owned by the properties specified in <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);">(i)</span>, and specify the opaque Behavior in (ii) as its method.</li><li>Create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);">(i)</span>.</li><li>Create call message to call operation specified in (iv) by another property.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c059179-953a-43ed-989a-250a796cd57d"><ac:parameter ac:name="">class</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes </p><hr /><ol><li><p>To create a new UML project, click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>New Project </strong>dialog will open.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The New Project Dialog" ac:alt="The New Project Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ew-uml-project.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Select <strong>UML Project</strong> from the <strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> group and specify the project's name, e.g., 'SimpleSequenceExecution'.</li><li>Specify the location where you want to save your project file and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>Right-click the <strong>Model</strong> node in the Containment tree and select <strong>Create</strong> <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Class</strong>. A new Class element will be created. Name the created Class, e.g., 'System'. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating a New Class Named System" ac:alt="Creating a New Class Named System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Create two more Classes and name them, e.g., 'a' and 'b.</li><li>Right-click the <strong>System</strong> model in the Containment tree and select <strong>Create</strong> <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Property </strong>to create property for the Class. Name the property, e.g., 'a1'.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a New Property a1 for the System Class" ac:alt="Creating a New Property a1 for the System Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree-b.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p>Right-click <strong>a1</strong> and select the Specification window. Select <strong>A</strong> as the property type.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Class A as Property Type" ac:alt="Selecting Class A as Property Type"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-property-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat step 6 to create property <strong>b1</strong> of type <strong>B</strong> (see the following figure).</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="System Class with Properties a1of Type A and b1 of Type B" ac:alt="System Class with Properties a1of Type A and b1 of Type B"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree-c.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11a424ab-8ea0-46d1-84ef-d797ca270964"><ac:parameter ac:name="">opaque</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create an opaque Behavior, owned by one of the two properties of the Class specified in (<ac:link ac:anchor="class"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ a Class containing two properties typed by different Classes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>), to print the summation of two Integer input parameters</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click the Class <strong>B</strong> and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Opaque</strong> <strong>Behavior</strong>. Name the opaque Behavior, e.g., 'Add'.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Opaque BehaviorAdd Owned by Class Bunder the Data Model" ac:alt="Opaque BehaviorAdd Owned by Class Bunder the Data Model"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cst-containment-tree-d.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the opaque Behavior <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Add</strong> and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Parameter </strong>to add a parameter element to the opaque Behavior. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'. </p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the parameter <strong>par1</strong> and select the Specification window. Select <strong>Integer</strong> as the parameter type.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Integer as par1 Parameter Type" ac:alt="Selecting Integer as par1 Parameter Type"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-par1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat Step 2 to create another parameter and name it, e.g., 'par2' of type <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating Parameter par2 of Type Integer" ac:alt="Creating Parameter par2 of Type Integer"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation9.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="42ae2838-ee6b-4903-915e-540ab2a55dc9"><ac:parameter ac:name="">script</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To write a script to print the summation of the two input parameters</p><hr /><ul><li><p>Open the Specification window of the opaque Behavior <strong>Add</strong> and write a script in the <strong>Body</strong> field (you can use any scripting language that is supported by MagicDraw Macro Engine, e.g., BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, or Python). In this example, JavaScript is used: print(par1+par2); to print the summation of the two Integer parameters <strong>par1</strong> and <strong>par2</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="JavaScript for Printing the Summation of Integer Values" ac:alt="JavaScript for Printing the Summation of Integer Values"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-opaque-behavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6>The Specification window of an Opaque Behavior.</h6></li></ul><p><br class="atl-forced-newline _mce_tagged_br" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0eab106f-0407-4107-870d-77fe3abeef2b"><ac:parameter ac:name="">operation</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create an operation owned by the properties specified in <ac:link ac:anchor="class"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[(Class containing two properties typed by different Classes)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and to specify the opaque Behavior as in (<ac:link ac:anchor="scri"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[opaque Behavior owned by one of the two properties of the Class]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>) as its method<span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> </span></p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click <strong>B</strong> and select <strong>Create</strong> <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Operation </strong>to add an operation element to the Class. Name the operation, e.g., 'Add'. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating Operation Add Owned by Property B" ac:alt="Creating Operation Add Owned by Property B"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation11.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the operation <strong>Add</strong> and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Parameter </strong>to add a parameter to the operation element. Name the parameter, e.g., 'par1'.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating New Parameter 'par1' of the Operation Add" ac:alt="Creating New Parameter 'par1' of the Operation Add"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation12.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the parameter <strong>par1</strong> and select <strong>Specification</strong>. Select <strong>Integer</strong> as the parameter type and <strong>in</strong> as the parameter direction. The Specification window of parameter par1 will be as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Integer as par1 Parameter Type and in as par1 Parameter Direction" ac:alt="Selecting Integer as par1 Parameter Type and in as par1 Parameter Direction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-par1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat Step 2 and 3 to create another parameter. Name it, e.g., <strong>par2</strong> of type <strong>Integer</strong>, having <strong>in</strong> as its direction. The parameters associated with the Operation will be as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating Parameter par2 of Type Integer and of 'in' Direction" ac:alt="Creating Parameter par2 of Type Integer and of 'in' Direction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation14.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Right-click the operation <strong>Add</strong> and select <strong>Specification</strong>. The Specification window will open.</li><li><p>Select the opaque Behavior <strong>Add</strong> as the <strong>Method</strong> of the operation. The Specification window of the Opaque Behavior will be as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Opaque Behavior Add as the Operation Method" ac:alt="Selecting Opaque Behavior Add as the Operation Method"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-opaque-behavior-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>The parameters associated with an Operation in the Containment tree will be as follows.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="An Add Operation whose Method is an Opaque Behavior Add" ac:alt="An Add Operation whose Method is an Opaque Behavior Add"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation16.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create an Interaction as the Classifier Behavior of the Class specified in (<ac:link ac:anchor="class"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[the Class containing two properties typed by different Classes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>)</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click <strong>System</strong> and select <strong>New</strong> <strong>Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Sequence</strong> <strong>Diagram</strong>. Select all properties in the <strong>Display Lifelines</strong> dialog and click <strong>OK</strong>.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="SelectingAll ofthe Properties in the Display Lifelines Dialog" ac:alt="SelectingAll ofthe Properties in the Display Lifelines Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="display-lifelines-dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Right-click the Class <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong> in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li><p>Make sure that the Interaction <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong> is the Classifier Behavior of the Class <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Interaction Systemas the Classifier Behavior ofthe Class System" ac:alt="Interaction Systemas the Classifier Behavior ofthe Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-classifier-behavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create a call message to call the operation specified in (<ac:link ac:anchor="operation"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[operation owned by property]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>) by another property </p><hr /><ol><li><p>Double-click the Interaction <strong>System</strong> to open the Sequence diagram containing two lifelines: <strong>a1</strong> and <strong>b1</strong>. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Sequence Diagram Containing Two Lifelines Representing a1 and b1" ac:alt="Sequence Diagram Containing Two Lifelines Representing a1 and b1"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation19.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for interaction simulation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Select <strong>Call</strong> <strong>Message</strong> from the <strong>Diagram Modeling</strong> <strong>Elements</strong> toolbar and create a call created in Step 2 to open the Specification window and select the operation <strong>Add</strong> as the <strong>Signature</strong><strong>(operation)</strong> of the call message.</p><h6><ac:image <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624835 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00312: (2026x) Creating a new UML project

- page_id: `255624835`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624835/2026x+Creating+a+new+UML+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:07.106+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

First of all, we will need to create a new MagicDraw project. 
 
 To create a new MagicDraw project

1. Click File > _GoBack New Project on the main menu. The New Project dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='new-project-a.png']
2. Select UML Project from the General-Purpose Modeling group and enter the project name and location. The project name will be " StopWatch" in this example.
3. Click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>First of all, we will need to create a new MagicDraw project. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To create a new MagicDraw project</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt;<ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a73b1aa7-9b06-4c1d-a83a-c64841b95121"><ac:parameter ac:name="">_GoBack</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro> <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>New Project</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The New Project Dialog" ac:alt="The New Project Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new-project-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a new UML project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">UML</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Project</strong> from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">General-Purpose</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Modeling</strong> group and enter the project name and location. The project name will be &quot;<em style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch&quot;</em> in this example.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>. </li></ol>
````

## PAGE 00313: (2026x) Creating a runtime object

- page_id: `255625005`
- space_key: `MMA`

- version_number: 1

- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use the following APIs to create a runtime object. You can create the object by specifying its name, its qualified name, or you can pass the Class object.

```text

```

The following example shows how to create a runtime object (Object_), typed by a specified Classifier in ALH API. 
If a passing parameter is typed by a String, the API will find a Class in the model that has a matching name, and then create the runtime object typed by that Class.

```text

```

If an instance of the com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Class has been passed on to the parameter "a"; you can create an object through the specified Class directly as follows:

```text

```

Note that if a parameter is equal to null, execution will be thrown.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624789 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00314: (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model

- page_id: `255624789`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624789/2026x+Creating+an+executable+stopwatch+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:04.026+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

798415423

798415426

798415424

This section guides you through the steps required to create a stopwatch model in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. You will learn how to create a new UML project as the first step, followed by creating the stopwatch structure through defining the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features and adding a Time attribute. In the next step, you will define the stopwatch Classifier Behavior by using the State Machine Behavior (shown in this example). You will also be guided through creating an initial stage, the ready, running, paused, stopped and final States, transition, Package of Signal elements, and Signal Events for transitions for the stopwatch State Machine diagram. The last two sections explain new operations required for defining the elapsed time and adding Activities to the ready and running States, resetting the elapsed time to zero, and increasing the elapsed time by one.

798415422

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

You will learn how to create a new UML project as the first step, followed by creating the stopwatch structure through defining the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features and adding a Time attribute. In the next step, you will define the stopwatch Classifier Behavior by using the State Machine Behavior (shown in this example). You will also be guided through creating an initial stage, the ready, running, paused, stopped and final States, transition, Package of Signal elements, and Signal Events for transitions for the stopwatch State Machine diagram. The last two sections explain new operations required for defining the elapsed time and adding Activities to the ready and running States, resetting the elapsed time to zero, and increasing the elapsed time by one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0ce5a6e-1dca-4fdb-8735-76ba3ac9f1c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">798415422</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="8d22cdbe-c271-4829-815e-6566983feaaf" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625145 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00315: (2026x) Creating and sending signals

- page_id: `255625145`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625145/2026x+Creating+and+sending+signals
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:26.587+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Action languages > (2026x) Action scripts APIs > (2026x) Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

##### Creating a signal instance

You can create Signal Instances by using the following APIs. You can create the signal by specifying its name, its Qualified name, or you can pass the Signal object.

```text

```

The following example demonstrates how to create a Signal Instance:

```text

```

##### Sending a signal instance to a specific target object

You can send Signal Instances by using the following APIs. You must specify the Signal (either by specifying the Signal Name or the Signal Instance) and the Target (either by specifying the Target Name or the Target Object). Optionally, you can specify the Port Name from which the signal is sent. In this case, the target is the owner of the port.

```text

```

The conditions that apply when creating an Instance are as follows:

- If a signal name contains "::", it will find the signal from a qualified name. The signal will be found if its qualified name ends with signalName.
- If a target is an instance of an fUML Object, the signal is sent to it directly.
- If a target is an instance of a String, there are two possible cases as follows:
  - It will find the target object(s) from all waiting objects whose part/property names match the "targetName".
  - It will find the target object(s) through connectors, if the "targetName" is the name of the port.

The following example shows how to send a specific signal to a specific target object in ALH API

```text

```

All parameters must not be null. Otherwise, the ScriptEngine errors will be thrown.

##### Getting the last signal instance from a runtime object

You can use the following API to retrieve the last signal instance from a runtime object. It is equivalent to $signal$.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get the last signal instance in the event pool of a specified object using ALH API

```text

```

To access any property from the last received signal, you can use predefined variable "sig_ + property name". For example, you can use "sig_speed" to get the speed property of the last received signal.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e1523f38-099b-4bb6-a124-f061bb219daf"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Creating a signal instance</h3><p>You can create Signal Instances by using the following APIs. <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can create the signal by specifying its name, its Qualified name, or you can pass the Signal object.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="17357beb-c062-40d7-9e3e-7fdbfec8d9b2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.createSignal("name")
ALH.createSignal(signal)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following example demonstrates how to create a Signal Instance:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2eacd5cf-6926-4f2e-a9e5-a7eb764b3c45"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.createSignal("start");
ALH.createSignal("statemachine::signals::start");
ALH.createSignal(s);            // if 's' is an instance of a Signal.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Sending a signal instance to a specific target object</h3><p>You can send Signal Instances by using the following APIs. You must specify the Signal (either by specifying the Signal Name or the Signal Instance) and the Target (either by specifying the Target Name or the Target Object). Optionally, you can specify the Port Name from which the signal is sent. In this case, the target is the owner of the port. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="021ca246-41fd-4270-b574-c39e63e3dfc6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.sendSignal("name", target)
ALH.sendSignal("name", "targetName")
ALH.sendSignal(signal, target)
ALH.sendSignal(signal, "targetName")
ALH.sendSignal("name", target, "portName")
ALH.sendSignal(signal, target,  "portName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The conditions that apply when creating an Instance are as follows:</p><ul><li>If a signal name contains &quot;::&quot;, it will find the signal from a qualified name. The signal will be found if its qualified name ends with signalName.</li><li>If a target is an instance of an fUML Object, the signal is sent to it directly.</li><li>If a target is an instance of a String, there are two possible cases as follows:<br /><ul><li>It will find the target object(s) from all waiting objects whose part/property names match the &quot;targetName&quot;.</li><li>It will find the target object(s) through connectors, if the &quot;targetName&quot; is the name of the port.</li></ul></li></ul><p>The following example shows how to send a specific signal to a specific target object in ALH API</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71eca44d-e3ec-401d-a9dc-a9e951a496d8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.sendSignal("play", o);                          // "o" references to a target object.
ALH.sendSignal("system::play", o);                 // Find a signal using a qualified name.
ALH.sendSignal("play", "Speaker");                // Send signal to property with name "Speaker".
ALH.sendSignal("play", Player, "out2");        // "Player" is an object, and "out2" is a port name owned by that object.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All parameters must not be null. Otherwise, the ScriptEngine errors will be thrown.</p><h3>Getting the last signal instance from a runtime object</h3><p>You can use the following API to retrieve the last signal instance from a runtime object. It is equivalent to $signal$.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b04b24f-d1de-4636-b8d0-b3b57e70c679"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getLastSignal(Object)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>The following code fragment shows how to get the last signal instance in the event pool of a specified object using ALH API</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8651252f-9751-4de8-80ef-34c555587eaf"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getLastSignal(self);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="888995f8-8d17-4e5f-b455-6f1d1148e8cc"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To access any property from the last received signal, you can use predefined variable &quot;sig_ + property name&quot;. For example, you can use &quot;sig_speed&quot; to get the speed property of the last received signal.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625521 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00316: (2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity

- page_id: `255625521`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625521/2026x+Creating+resetTime+Operation+and+resetTime+Activity
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:48.407+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model > (2026x) Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a **resetTime** operation

1. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Operation .
2. Name the new operation "resetTime" . The resetTime operation will be created. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav33bc1cb1ce7b331271657a674d705aba.png']

Next, we will use an Activity to define the **resetTime** operation. The Activity will contain the Actions and the flows that will show the steps to set the **time** value to zero.

To create a **resetTime**Activity

1. Right-click the StopWatch Class in the containment browser and select New Element > Activity .
2. Name the created Activity "resetTime" .
3. Add an Activity diagram to the resetTime Activity by right-clicking the resetTime Activity in the containment browser and select New Diagram > Activity Diagram . The new Activity diagram will be created under the resetTime Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is "resetTime" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav4a4eacea1d68f44689eb60d76e0cb569.png']

On the **resetTime** Activity diagram, you will need an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value of **time:Integer[1]** to zero. The structural feature of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction must be set as the time attribute of the StopWatch Class. 
 
To reset the time value of the StopWatch Object to zero using an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction

1. Click Action > Any Action... on the Activity diagram toolbar (see the following figure). The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6e7022874460de01a81f4414ef90cadc.png']
2. Select AddStructuralFeatureValueAction and click OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Click the **resetTime** Activity diagram to place the position of the created **AddStructuralFeatureValueAction**.
4. Right-click the symbol of the **AddStructuralFeatureValueAction** on the **resetTime** Activity diagram and select **Specification** to open its Specification window. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action.png']
5. Scroll down to the Structural Feature row, select it, and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select Property dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-property-a.png']
6. The Attribute time:Integer[1] of the StopWatch Class will be selected as the structural feature of this Action in this example.
7. Click OK to close the Select Property dialog and return to the Specification window.
8. Click the Is Replace All row and select the check box. The AddStructuralFeatureValueAction will remove any existing value and assign a new value to the structural feature. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-b.png']

We need to specify input pins for both the Object and the value of the **AddStructuralFeatureValueAction** metaClass. The object of the Classifier that contains the structural feature and its value will be supplied through these input pins respectively. 
 
To create input pins

1. Click the Pins node on the left-hand side of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction Specification dialog. The pins that are related to the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction will appear.
2. Click the Value row and select Input Pin . [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-c.png']
3. Name the input pin "t" and specify its type as Integer . [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-input-pin.png']
4. Click the Close button. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav34ec79dfb0b9452bdf513c2af654c933.png']
5. Select AddStructuralFeatureValueAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and click the Display Pins icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. The Select Pins dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-input-pin.png']
6. Select all pins and click OK .

To allow the object of the StopWatch to supply the value to self input using a ReadSelfAction

1. Click Action > Any Action... on the Activity diagram toolbar. The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open.
2. Select ReadSelfAction and click OK .
3. Click the resetTime Activity diagram. A ReadSelfAction will be created on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavea520615a98996e6601cca689431039c.png']
4. Click Object Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar and click the self output pin of the ReadSelfAction and the self input pin of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. An object flow will be created to connect these two pins together. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavc15454b336b7513863415f2b71cfdf49.png']

Next, we will create a ValueSpecificationAction to supply a value to the input pin **t** of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. 
 
To create a LiteralInteger of zero value using a ValueSpecificationAction

1. Click Action > Any Action... on the Activity diagram toolbar. The Select Action MetaClass dialog will open.
2. Select ValueSpecificationAction and click OK to close the Select Action MetaClass dialog.
3. Click the resetTime Activity diagram to create a ValueSpecificationAction.
4. Right-click the ValueSpecificationAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and select Specification to open its Specification window.
5. Select the Value row and click the Show Shortcut Menu button, and select Value Specification > Literal Integer to create a Literal Integer. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-value-specification-action.png']
6. A new Literal Integer with a default value of 0 will be created as the value of the ValueSpecificationAction.
7. Right-click the pin result of the ValueSpecificationAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and select Specification to open its Specification window.
8. Rename the output pin ( result ) to " t " and specify its type as Integer . [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-output-pin.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav71821695a25971d7ea9242546e13e75b.png']
9. Add an Initial stage and an Activity Final stage to the resetTime Activity diagram.
10. Click Control Flow on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Initial Node to the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction and the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to the Final stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavccef70c22bee118881298b220e6db83b.png']

You have now created a complete resetTime Activity diagram. The next thing you will need to do is to set the**Specification** of the **resetTime** Activity to the **resetTime** operation. 
 
To set the **Specification**of the **resetTime**Activity to the **resetTime**operation

1. Right-click the resetTime Activity in the containment browser and select Specification to open its Specification window.
2. Select the Specification row and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button (see the following figure). The Select Element dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-activity-properties.png']
3. Select the resetTime operation of the StopWatch Class as the specification of the Activity and click OK to close the Select Element dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-element-dialog.png']
4. Click Close to close the Specification window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a <strong>resetTime</strong> operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Operation</strong>.</li><li>Name the new operation <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;resetTime&quot;</em>. The resetTime operation will be created.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating the ResetTime Operation" ac:alt="Creating the ResetTime Operation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav33bc1cb1ce7b331271657a674d705aba.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Next, we will use an Activity to define the <strong>resetTime</strong> operation. The Activity will contain the Actions and the flows that will show the steps to set the <strong>time</strong> value to zero. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To create a <strong>resetTime </strong>Activity</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Activity</strong>.</li><li>Name the created Activity <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;resetTime&quot;</em>.</li><li>Add an Activity diagram to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity by right-clicking the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">New Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Activity</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong>. The new Activity diagram will be created under the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity. We will use the default name of this diagram, which is <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;resetTime&quot;</em>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The ResetTime Activity and Its Activity Diagram" ac:alt="The ResetTime Activity and Its Activity Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4a4eacea1d68f44689eb60d76e0cb569.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /> On the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram, you will need an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to set the value of <strong>time:Integer[1]</strong> to zero. The structural feature of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction must be set as the time attribute of the StopWatch Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To reset the time value of the StopWatch Object to zero using an AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any </strong><strong>Action...</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar (see the following figure). The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">MetaClass</strong> dialog will open. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Selecting Any Action on the Activity Diagram Toolbar" ac:alt="Selecting Any Action on the Activity Diagram Toolbar" ac:height="162" ac:width="309"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav6e7022874460de01a81f4414ef90cadc.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><span style="line-height: 20.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-action-metaclass.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span><br /><span style="line-height: 20.0px;"><br /></span></li><li><span style="line-height: 20.0px;">Click the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram to place the position of the created <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong>.</span></li><li><span style="line-height: 20.0px;">Right-click the symbol of the <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> on the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</span><br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The AddStructuralFeatureValueAction Specification Dialog" ac:alt="The AddStructuralFeatureValueAction Specification Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-add-structural-feature-action.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Scroll down to the <strong>Structural </strong><strong>Feature</strong> row, select it, and click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Property</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Select property dialog" ac:alt="Select property dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-property-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>The Attribute <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time:Integer[1]</strong> of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class will be selected as the structural feature of this Action in this example.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong> to close the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Property</strong> dialog and return to the Specification window.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Is Replace</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">All</strong> row and select the check box. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> will remove any existing value and assign a new value to the structural feature.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Is Replace All Attribute Check Box" ac:alt="Selecting the Is Replace All Attribute Check Box"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-b.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /></li></ol><p>We need to specify input pins for both the Object and the value of the <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> metaClass. The object of the Classifier that contains the structural feature and its value will be supplied through these input pins respectively. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create input pins</p><hr /><ol><li>Click the <strong>Pins</strong> node on the left-hand side of the <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong><strong>Specification </strong>dialog. The pins that are related to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> will appear.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> row and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Input</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Pin</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Assigning a Value to the Input Pin" ac:alt="Assigning a Value to the Input Pin"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-add-structural-feature-action-c.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Name the input pin <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;t&quot;</em> and specify its type as <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specifying the Name of the Pin and Its Type for the Value" ac:alt="Specifying the Name of the Pin and Its Type for the Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-input-pin.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> button. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Display Pins Button on the Smart Manipulator Toolbar" ac:alt="The Display Pins Button on the Smart Manipulator Toolbar"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav34ec79dfb0b9452bdf513c2af654c933.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Select <strong>AddStructuralFeatureValueAction</strong> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity diagram and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Display </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Pins</strong> icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select Pins</strong> dialog will open. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Select Pins Dialog" ac:alt="The Select Pins Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-input-pin.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select all pins and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><span style="text-decoration: underline;"><br /></span></p><p>To allow the object of the StopWatch to supply the value to self input using a ReadSelfAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any</strong><strong>Action...</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select</strong><strong>Action</strong><strong>MetaClass</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ReadSelfAction</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resetTime</strong> Activity diagram. A ReadSelfAction will be created on the diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a ReadSelf Action" ac:alt="Creating a ReadSelf Action" ac:height="310" ac:width="386"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavea520615a98996e6601cca689431039c.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Object</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">self</strong> output pin of the ReadSelfAction and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">self</strong> input pin of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. An object flow will be created to connect these two pins together.<br /><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Connecting the Input and Output Pins with an Object Flow" ac:alt="Connecting the Input and Output Pins with an Object Flow" ac:height="311" ac:width="386"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavc15454b336b7513863415f2b71cfdf49.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Next, we will create a ValueSpecificationAction to supply a value to the input pin <strong>t</strong> of the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a LiteralInteger of zero value using a ValueSpecificationAction</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Action</strong> &gt; <strong>Any</strong><strong>Action...</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar. The <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action </strong><strong>MetaClass</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong>ValueSpecificationAction</strong> and click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Select </strong><strong>Action MetaClass</strong> dialog.</li><li>Click the resetTime Activity diagram to create a ValueSpecificationAction.</li><li>Right-click the ValueSpecificationAction on the resetTime Activity diagram and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification </strong>to open its Specification window.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> row and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Show</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Shortcut</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Menu</strong> button, and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Literal Integer</strong> to create a Literal Integer. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a Literal Integer Value for the Value of Action" ac:alt="Creating a Literal Integer Value for the Value of Action"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-value-specification-action.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>A new Literal Integer with a default value of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">0</strong> will be created as the value of the ValueSpecificationAction.</li><li>Right-click the pin <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">result</strong> of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ValueSpecificationAction</strong> on the resetTime Activity diagram and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Rename the output pin (<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">result</strong>) to &quot;<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">t</strong>&quot; and specify its type as <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Output Pin with Its Type Assigned to the Result" ac:alt="The Output Pin with Its Type Assigned to the Result"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-output-pin.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Connecting ValueSpecificationAction and addStructuralFeatureValueAction with an Object Flow" ac:alt="Connecting ValueSpecificationAction and addStructuralFeatureValueAction with an Object Flow"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav71821695a25971d7ea9242546e13e75b.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Add an Initial stage and an Activity Final stage to the resetTime Activity diagram.</li><li>Click <strong>Control</strong><strong>Flow</strong> on the Activity diagram toolbar to connect the Initial Node to the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction and the AddStructuralFeatureValueAction to the Final stage.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavccef70c22bee118881298b220e6db83b.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> You have now created a complete resetTime Activity diagram. The next thing you will need to do is to set the<strong> Specification</strong> of the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity to the <strong>resetTime</strong> operation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To set the <strong>Specification </strong>of the <strong>resetTime </strong>Activity to the <strong>resetTime </strong>operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity in the containment browser and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> row and click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button (see the following figure). The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Attribute of the ResetTime Activity" ac:alt="The Specification Attribute of the ResetTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-activity-properties.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong>resetTime</strong> operation of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class as the specification of the Activity and click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Select Element</strong> dialog.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Select element dialog" ac:alt="Select element dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-element-dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong> to close the Specification window. <br /><br /></li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625430 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00317: (2026x) Creating runtime objects from Classifiers

- page_id: `255625430`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625430/2026x+Creating+runtime+objects+from+Classifiers
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:44.952+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['runtime-objects-from-classifiers']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

39858299

39858302

39858301

With Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit, you can use a Classifier with nested parts as the simulation context without the need to create Instance Specifications for those nested parts. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create runtime objects for those parts automatically. If the type of a property is Data Type, the default value of the runtime value of that property will also be created, depending on the default value of the property's type. In addition, if the type of the part contains a specified Classifier Behavior, and the type itself is set as active, the Behavior will run (the autoStartActiveObjects option in the SimulationConfig must be set as true).

The first figure shows a Class diagram located in the *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample, demonstrating property light, button and timer as parts of the System Class.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Class System and its parts.

You may replace the SimulationTarget of the SimulationConfig named FlashLight with the System Class. You can see the result of running this SimulationConfig. The result shows that Runtime Objects are automatically created for the parts of the System Class, and the Behavior of each part also automatically starts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Sessions and Variables Panes showing runtime objects with a Classifier.

39858298

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Classifier Behavior property' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create runtime objects for those parts automatically. If the type of a property is Data Type, the default value of the runtime value of that property will also be created, depending on the default value of the property's type. In addition, if the type of the part contains a specified Classifier Behavior, and the type itself is set as active, the Behavior will run (the autoStartActiveObjects option in the SimulationConfig must be set as true). </p><p>The first figure shows a Class diagram located in the <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample, demonstrating property light, button and timer as parts of the System Class. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Class System and Its Parts" ac:alt="Class System and Its Parts"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ClassSystemAndItsParts.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Class System and its parts.</h6><p>You may replace the SimulationTarget of the SimulationConfig named FlashLight with the System Class. You can see the result of running this SimulationConfig. The result shows that Runtime Objects are automatically created for the parts of the System Class, and the Behavior of each part also automatically starts. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight" ac:alt="Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ModifiedExecutionConfigFlashLight.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Modified SimulationConfig FlashLight.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Sessions and Variables Panes Showing Runtime Objects with a Classifier" ac:alt="Sessions and Variables Panes Showing Runtime Objects with a Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sim-console-e.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating runtime objects from Classifiers" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Sessions and Variables Panes showing runtime objects with a Classifier.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4cc2a2b-03e7-4285-8e51-bb5ecc0e948d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39858298</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Classifier Behavior property" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625416 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00318: (2026x) Creating runtime objects from Instance Specifications

- page_id: `255625416`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625416/2026x+Creating+runtime+objects+from+Instance+Specifications
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:44.370+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['runtime-objects-from-instance-specifications']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

39929354

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

39929356

39929353

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

39929355

At the beginning of a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create a runtime object to store runtime values. If the element to be simulated is an InstanceSpecification or an SimulationConfig whose SimulationTarget is InstanceSpecification, the runtime values will be generated from the slot values. These runtime values will later be assigned to the runtime object's structural features that are equivalent to the defined feature of the slots.

If the slot of the InstanceSpecification is empty, and the defined feature of the slots has a defined default value, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will generate a runtime value from the default value and assign it to the structural feature of the runtime object.

The following figure shows the example of a runtime object that is created for executing the pipe InstanceSpecification. The length slot of the InstanceSpecification contains only one value, which is 1.0. The runtime value, which is produced for the length structural feature of the runtime object, will equal this slot value (1.0), while the runtime values of radius and thickness will equal the default values of the radius and thickness property of the Pipe class (0.05 and 0.002 respectively).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Variables pane showing the runtime object.

#### Representation of a Runtime object

The **Variables** pane in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit displays **Name** and **Value** information of a runtime object that you are executing. If a runtime object is created by an Instance Specification, the name of the Instance Specification will appear in the **Value** column as shown in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A representation of the runtime object in the Variables pane.

You can also see a representation of the element's name in tooltip as in the **Value** column if you point over the element being activated on the diagram pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A representation of element being activated on the tooltip.

You can open a **Message** dialog that contains the runtime object being activated by clicking the element and the tooltip icon [IMAGE alt='' src='']on the tooltip menu.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A representation of the Element dialog.

#### Locating Instance Specification in the Containment tree

If you used an Instance Specification to create a runtime object and you want to find that particular Instance Specification in the Containment tree among the other items, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit can locate and highlight it for you. This is particularly useful if you have a long list of items in the Containment tree, and it takes some time to locate a specific item. All you need to do is select the **Go****To** command from the context menu of that runtime object in the **Variables** pane.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If a runtime object is not created from an instance specification, the **Go****To** command will be disabled on the context menu.

To locate and highlight an Instance Specification in the Containment tree through the **Variables** pane

1. Right-click a runtime object in the **Variables** pane and select **Go****To**. The name of the Instance Specification that you used so as to create the runtime object will appear on the submenu. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Click the Instance Specification's name. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will locate and highlight it in the Containment tree. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

40952040

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Instance Specification' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment Tree' space='MT']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Variables pane' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624827 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00319: (2026x) Creating the stopwatch structure

- page_id: `255624827`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624827/2026x+Creating+the+stopwatch+structure
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:06.521+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Next, we will define the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features by using a Class diagram. We will start by creating a new package to store the stopwatch model. 
 
 To create a new package to store the stopwatch model

1. Right-click Model in the containment browser and select New Element > Package . A new package will be created under the Model node. Name the created package "system" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavcbf13a2eed10e9f0ad36880954bb8398.png']
2. Create a new Class diagram in the system package by clicking the Class Diagram button [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav58e81b0fa02881f9f435fa92dbdccb79.png'] on the Diagrams toolbar. The Create Diagram dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='new-diagram-menu.png']
3. Name the Class diagram "System" and select the system package as the diagram owner.
4. Click **OK**. NoteYou can also use the context menu of the system package to create a new Class diagram by right-clicking the system package and select **New Diagram** > **Class Diagram**.
5. Use the diagram toolbar of Class Diagram to create a new Class Element and name the created Class "StopWatch" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav9d2a257d15dee27f02355f217932ae9e.png']

We will then add a Time Attribute to the **StopWatch** Class. Since this Attribute represents the elapsed time in seconds, it will be typed by an **Integer** data type. 
 
To add a Time Attribute to the **StopWatch**Class

1. Click the manipulated button (the small orange button) in the StopWatch Class. A new Attribute will be created in the StopWatch Class.
2. Name the Attribute "time" and type it with an Integer by directly typing it into the Attribute compartment of the StopWatch Class. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav96fbc3722aee48918b184b38ee181f39.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Next, we will define the stopwatch Classifier and its structural features by using a Class diagram. We will start by creating a new package to store the stopwatch model. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To create a new package to store the stopwatch model</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click <strong>Model</strong> in the containment browser and select <strong>New Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Package</strong>. A new package will be created under the <strong>Model</strong> node. Name the created package <em>&quot;system&quot;</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating a Package Named System" ac:alt="Creating a Package Named System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavcbf13a2eed10e9f0ad36880954bb8398.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a new Class diagram in the system package by clicking the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> button <ac:image ac:height="15" ac:width="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav58e81b0fa02881f9f435fa92dbdccb79.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the Diagrams toolbar. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> dialog will open. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Create Diagram Dialog" ac:alt="The Create Diagram Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new-diagram-menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Name the Class diagram <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;System&quot;</em> and select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">system</strong> package as the diagram owner.</li><li><p>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72fef5ad-47e0-4575-b419-3ab435f114cd"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also use the context menu of the system package to create a new Class diagram by right-clicking the system package and select <strong>New Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Class Diagram</strong>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Use the diagram toolbar of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Diagram</strong> to create a new <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class</strong> Element and name the created Class <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;StopWatch&quot;</em>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Creating the StopWatch Class" ac:alt="Creating the StopWatch Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav9d2a257d15dee27f02355f217932ae9e.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> We will then add a Time Attribute to the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. Since this Attribute represents the elapsed time in seconds, it will be typed by an <strong>Integer</strong> data type. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To add a Time Attribute to the <strong>StopWatch </strong>Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Click the manipulated button (the small orange button) in the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. A new Attribute will be created in the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class.</li><li>Name the Attribute <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;time&quot;</em> and type it with an <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Integer</strong> by directly typing it into the Attribute compartment of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Adding a Time Attribute to the StopWatch Class" ac:alt="Adding a Time Attribute to the StopWatch Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav96fbc3722aee48918b184b38ee181f39.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating the stopwatch structure" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p> </p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624970 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00320: (2026x) Creating User Interface mockups for the stopwatch model

- page_id: `255624970`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624970/2026x+Creating+User+Interface+mockups+for+the+stopwatch+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:13.709+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to use the MagicDraw's User Interface (UI) Modeler to create user interface mockups that will be displayed during model execution. This session shows you how to create a user interface for the stopwatch model. The user interface will be in the form of a panel with buttons to send signals to the runtime object of the stopwatch during model execution. A label will be used to show the runtime time value. 
 
 To create user a interface mockup

1. Right-click the Model node and select New Element > Package to create a new package for the UI Elements.
2. Name the created package " ui ". [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-containment.png']
3. Click the button on the Custom Diagram toolbar to create a new User Interface Modeling Diagram . The Create Diagram dialog will open.
4. Name the diagram " StopWatchUI " and select the ui package as the owner of the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav99741dc64147b53d16c89670321df3a3.png']
5. Click OK .
6. Create a new frame for the UI mockup by clicking Frame on the User Interface Modeling Diagram toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='package-ui.png']
7. Create a label to show the runtime time value by clicking Label on the diagram toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='package-ui-2.png']
8. Create five buttons to send the signals to the runtime object of the stopwatch model by clicking Button on the diagram toolbar.
9. Name the buttons: Start , stop , split , unsplit , and reset . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav15cc35ab7ecdbd6e363cc911d64736af.png']
10. Create a UI Prototype by dragging the StopWatch Class from the containment browser to the created UI frame on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-b.png']
11. Create a Runtime Value Prototype by dragging the time property of the StopWatch Class from the containment browser to the created label on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-c.png']
12. Create a Signal Instance Prototype by dragging the Signal from the containment browser to each of the buttons. The buttons will send the dragged signals to the runtime object when you click them during the execution. Therefore, each signal in the stopwatch model will be dragged to each button that has a corresponding name. For example, the start signal will be dragged to the start button, and the stop signal will be dragged to the stop button. [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-d.png']
13. Open the Specification window of the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration to add the ready user interface mockup for the stopwatch to the SimulationConfig for the StopWatch.
14. Edit the UI field by clicking the [ATTACHMENT filename='3-dot-btn.png'] button. The Select «UI» dialog will open.
15. Select the created Frame in the ui package and click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='select-ui-dialog.png']

The modified SilentStopwatch is now ready to run the stopwatch with the UI mockup. Next, you need to run the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration. 
 
 To run the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1. Right-click the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration and select Simulation > Run . A simulation session will be created to execute the StopWatch class.
2. Click the Run Simulation button on the Simulation window toolbar. The execution of the StopWatch class will start with the designed UI mockup. The time value displayed in the label is now zero (0). [ATTACHMENT filename='stopwatch-ui-e.png']

You can click the **start** button to start the stopwatch. You can use the buttons on the UI mockup to send the signals to the runtime object of the **StopWatch** class. The time value displayed on the label of the UI mockup will increment by one every second. 
 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Click the **stop** button to stop the execution and click it again to go to the final stage, which is the termination of the execution.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625487 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00321: (2026x) Creating values and objects in the variables pane

- page_id: `255625487`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625487/2026x+Creating+values+and+objects+in+the+variables+pane
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:47.304+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['creating-values-in-variables-tab', 'creating-objects-in-variables-tab']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

39503046

39503048

39503047

You can create a new value or object through the shortcut menu **Add** **Value** of a property typed by a primitive type, a Class, or a block in the **Variables** pane. The shortcut menu will be available only when the number of existing values or objects of the selected property is less than the upper multiplicity of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using the Add Value shortcut menu to add a new value.

If a property is typed by a primitive type (and SysML primitive value types), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will add a new value, which is a default value of the primitive type, as the value of that property.

If a property is typed by a class or a Block, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show a list of Classes or Blocks containing the Class or the Block that types the property and all of its subtypes, which are not abstract Classes or abstract Blocks. When you select a Class or a Block from the list, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from that Class or Block and set the object as the value of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Selecting a Class or a Block to initialize an object.

39503045

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Variables pane' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The shortcut menu will be available only when the number of existing values or objects of the selected property is less than the upper multiplicity of the property. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Context Menu to Add a New Value" ac:alt="Context Menu to Add a New Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="addValue_menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating values and objects in the variables pane" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Using the Add Value shortcut menu to add a new value.</h6><p>If a property is typed by a primitive type (and SysML primitive value types), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will add a new value, which is a default value of the primitive type, as the value of that property. </p><p>If a property is typed by a class or a Block, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show a list of Classes or Blocks containing the Class or the Block that types the property and all of its subtypes, which are not abstract Classes or abstract Blocks. When you select a Class or a Block from the list, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from that Class or Block and set the object as the value of the property. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Selecting a Class or a Block to Initialize an Object" ac:alt="Selecting a Class or a Block to Initialize an Object"><ri:attachment ri:filename="classblocks_to_initObj.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating values and objects in the variables pane" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Selecting a Class or a Block to initialize an object.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c60a695-f941-474f-a75b-d7b4dbf912db"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503045</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
