<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625747 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00322: (2026x) CSV export

- page_id: `255625747`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625747/2026x+CSV+export
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:01.856+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['csv-export', 'exporting-simulation-results-to-csv']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

33

CSV Export configuration allows you to export simulation results to a CSV file.

To export simulation results to a CSV file

1. Open a project. Here the <modeling_tool_install_root>\samples\simulation\HingeMonteCarloAnalysis sample project is used.
2. In the Simulation Configuration diagram, create the CSV Export configuration.
3. Open the Specification window of the CSV Export configuration and specify the following properties:
  - Record Time - if set to true , time will be recorded in the first column of the exported CSV file.
  - Write At The End (optional) - if set to true , values are recorded only once at the end of execution before termination.
  - File Name - the name of the file where the exported results are written. By default, the file is saved in the project directory.
  - **Represents** - the Classifier represented by the CSV Export configuration.
  - **Values** - the values which will be written to the exported CSV file.
  - **Export States**-**** If set to *true*, active State names are exported for the selected part. If set to *false*, the instance name of the part is exported.
  - **Load in Player** - if set to *true*, the exported CSV file is automatically loaded to the Results Player after the simulation terminates. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specification of CSVExport clearance.png']
4. Set the created CSV Export as the value of the Execution Listeners property of the Simulation Configuration. This step is important for the results to be written to file. See the CSV export configuration below: [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Run the simulation and then stop it. The results will be written to the file specified in Step 3. See the parameters of CSV Export configuration below: [ATTACHMENT filename='CSV Export Result.png']

##### Support of Part properties

Not only can CSV export the values of properties with primitive types, e.g., Real, Integer, etc., but it can also export the types or active states of the properties that are not primitive. Selected Part names in the **value** tag will be used as headers, and type names will be used as values of exported CSVs as shown in the figure below. You can also export the active State names for the selected part, if you set the Export States property to *true.*If you set the Export States property to *false*, the instance name is exported.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selected Part names used as headers and type names used as values of exported CSVs.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9c9ea061-7618-4f2f-b94f-40c0652d99e2"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>CSV Export configuration allows you to export simulation results to a CSV file.</p><p><br /></p><p>To export simulation results to a CSV file</p><hr /><ol><li class="confluence-link">Open a project. Here the <em>&lt;modeling_tool_install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\HingeMonteCarloAnalysis</em> sample project is used.</li><li>In the Simulation Configuration diagram, create the CSV Export configuration.</li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the CSV Export configuration and specify the following properties:</p><ul><li><strong>Record Time</strong> - if set to <em>true</em>, time will be recorded in the first column of the exported CSV file.</li><li><strong>Write At The End</strong> (optional) - if set to <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">true</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, values are recorded</span> only once at the end of execution before termination.</li><li><strong>File Name</strong> - the name of the file where the exported results are written. By default, the file is saved in the project directory.</li><li><p><strong>Represents</strong> - the Classifier represented by the CSV Export configuration.</p></li><li><p><strong>Values</strong> - the values which will be written to the exported CSV file.</p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><strong>Export States </strong>-<strong> </strong> If set to <em>true</em>, active State names are exported for the selected part. If set to <em>false</em>, the instance name of the part is exported.</span></li><li><p><strong>Load in Player</strong> - if set to <em>true</em>, the exported CSV file is automatically loaded to the Results Player after the simulation terminates.<br /><br /></p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification of CSVExport clearance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) CSV export" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><p><br /></p></li></ul></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Set the created CSV Export as the value of the Execution Listeners property of the Simulation Configuration.</span> This step is important for the results to be written to file. See the CSV export configuration below:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="CSV Export Simulation Config.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) CSV export" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li>Run the simulation and then stop it. The results will be written to the file specified in Step 3. See the parameters of CSV Export configuration below:<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="CSV Export Result.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) CSV export" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><h3>Support of Part properties</h3><p>Not only can CSV export the values of properties with primitive types, e.g., Real, Integer, etc., but it can also export the types or active states of the properties that are not primitive. Selected Part names in the <strong>value</strong> tag will be used as headers, and type names will be used as values of exported CSVs as shown in the figure below. You can also export the active State names for the selected part, if you set the Export States property to <em>true. </em>If you set the Export States property to <em>false</em>, the instance name is exported.<span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><br /><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Part Properties Supported.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) CSV export" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Selected Part names used as headers and type names used as values of exported CSVs.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Exporting plots data to a CSV file" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626073 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00323: (2026x) Customizing animation

- page_id: `255626073`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626073/2026x+Customizing+animation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:29.864+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Animation
- labels: ['customizing-animation', 'animation-customization']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

1441516382

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

1441516385

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

1441516384

There are four kinds of annotated elements in a model simulation: (i) Active, (ii) Visited, (iii) Breakpoint, and (iv) Last Visited elements. By default, active elements will be annotated with red, visited elements with green, last visited elements with orange, and breakpoints with yellow. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize the colors of these annotated elements through the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Project options' space='']** dialog in the **Animation** group.

The **Auto** **Open** **Diagrams** option in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Project options' space='']** dialog and Simulation window allows Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to open a tab where you can see a diagram that contains a currently active Element in a non-silent simulation (animation is enabled). If you want to enable this option, you need to select **true** as its value. This is useful when you want to track the simulation current status. Select **false** as the value of **Auto****Open** **Diagrams** when you would like to focus on just one diagram and avoid automatic switching or opening of diagrams.

If the value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is **false** and you run a silent-mode simulation of an activity diagram that has a decision node without any decision input, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight all elements including the decision node and open a **Question** dialog to ask for a decision when the simulation reaches the decision node. However, if the value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is false, and you are running a non-silent simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight only the decision node.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The default value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is **false**.

To select the **Auto****Open****Diagrams** option in the **Simulation** window

- Click the Auto Open Diagrams toggle button in the Simulation window as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### INFO: Information

Information

When animation with selected **Auto Open Diagrams** is used, it must open animated diagrams in the same tab and must not open new diagram windows as new tabs. If a diagram is already open in a tab other than the active tab, the other tab will close, and the diagram animation will be shown in the active tab.

1441516381

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Project options' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize the colors of these annotated elements through the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Project Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog in the <strong>Animation</strong> group.</p><p>The <strong>Auto</strong> <strong>Open</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> option in the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Project Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog and Simulation window allows Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to open a tab where you can see a diagram that contains a currently active Element in a non-silent simulation (animation is enabled). Select <strong>false</strong> as the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> when you would like to focus on just one diagram and avoid automatic switching or opening of diagrams. </p><p>If the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is <strong>false</strong> and you run a silent-mode simulation of an activity diagram that has a decision node without any decision input, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight all elements including the decision node and open a <strong>Question</strong> dialog to ask for a decision when the simulation reaches the decision node. However, if the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is false, and you are running a non-silent simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight only the decision node. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dfbd5162-7a95-4d00-b8d5-f2e9cd7dbb1a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The default value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is <strong>false</strong>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To select the <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> option in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window </p><hr /><ul><li>Click the <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> toggle button in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window as follows</li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Auto Open Diagrams button" ac:alt="Auto Open Diagrams button"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110041_2016-06-24+15_23_53-Photos.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Customizing animation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ec1cbc1c-e000-41dd-8703-db447586369a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When animation with selected <strong>Auto Open Diagrams</strong> is used, it must open animated diagrams in the same tab and must not open new diagram windows as new tabs. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626120 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00324: (2026x) Debug process simulation

- page_id: `255626120`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626120/2026x+Debug+process+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:32.168+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging
- labels: ['debug-buttons']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

30123672

30123675

30123674

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to control a model simulation using the debug buttons such as **Suspend**, **Resume**, **Step** **into**, and **Step** **over**. The table below explains the functions of all of the debug buttons.

| Button | Name | Shortcut key | Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Start Simulation | F8 | To start simulating an object of the initialized session shown in the Sessions pane. |
|  | Restart | F8 | To restart the last simulated element. |
|  | Suspend | F8 | To pause a running simulation session in the Sessions. |
|  | Resume | F8 | To resume a simulation session. |
|  | Step into | F5 | To simulate and animate a currently active element in a selected simulation session in the Sessions pane. |
|  | Step over | F6 | To simulate a currently active element in a selected simulation session and run animation in the background. |
|  | Terminate | None | To stop a session in the Sessions pane. If the selected session contains sub-sessions, all of the sub-sessions will also be terminated. |

You can examine and edit variables in the **Variables** pane (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Variables pane' space='']), pause the a model simulation at predefined breakpoints (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Breakpoints' space='']), or simulate one element at a time using the **Step** **into** or **Step** **over** button.

The **Debugger** pane includes a player-like control panel for a step-by-step simulation (see the table above), threads or Behaviors with an expandable stack trace (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Understanding simulation sessions' space='']), input/output console for custom commands or expressions evaluation (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation console' space='']), **Variables** pane/runtime structure (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring' space='']), and **Breakpoints** pane.

30123671

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Variables pane' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Breakpoints' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Understanding simulation sessions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625591 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00325: (2026x) Deferred Events

- page_id: `255625591`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625591/2026x+Deferred+Events
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:50.842+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) State Machine simulation > (2026x) Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['event-deffering', 'deffering-events']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Important

122554799

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Important

122554801

122554798

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Important

122554800

UML State Machines provide a special mechanism for deferring Events in States. In every State, you can include a clause [Event list]/defer. If an Event in the current State’s deferred Event list occurs, the Event will be saved (deferred) for future processing until a State is entered that does not list the Event in its deferred Event list. Upon entry to such a State, the UML State Machine will automatically recall any saved Event(s) that are no longer deferred and will then either consume or discard these Events.

- A deferred Event is activated as soon as it enters a State where it is not deferred.
- A deferred Event can only be added to a State Machine diagram at this time.

#### Deferrable Trigger

A list of Triggers that are candidates to be retained by the State Machine if they trigger no transitions out of the State (not consumed). A deferred Trigger is retained until the State Machine reaches a State configuration where it is no longer deferred.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Specification window for Event State moving - showing the editable Deferrable Trigger tag.

As in the State Machine diagram in the following figure, the Elevator**'open'** Event is deferred as long as the Elevator is moving. Only when the State transitions to **'stopped'** State again, the **'open'** Event will be executed because it is no longer deferred at this point.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 A deferred clause added to Event moving which causes the Event to delay until it is no longer deferred.

#### WARNING: Important

Important

This version of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit does not support **Deferred Events** for Activity diagrams. They can now only be added to a State in a State Machine diagram.

122943877

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State Machine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MT']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61558ab5-bfc1-4cde-9ead-c4ea664e519f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554799</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="73b04c3b-e4ed-4fa5-8be1-1da11bbdf0da"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554801</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="13ca05ee-7924-408a-bd47-32c99feb82f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554798</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b384a4d1-f744-4524-aedc-45f7f60c0e0d"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">2</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e42a51a-3848-436e-8d1c-3da033a70b43"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554800</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>UML State Machines provide a special mechanism for deferring Events in States. In every State, you can include a clause [Event list]/defer. If an Event in the current State’s deferred Event list occurs, the Event will be saved (deferred) for future processing until a State is entered that does not list the Event in its deferred Event list. Upon entry to such a State, the UML State Machine will automatically recall any saved Event(s) that are no longer deferred and will then either consume or discard these Events.</p><ul><li>A deferred Event is activated as soon as it enters a State where it is not deferred. </li><li>A deferred Event can only be added to a State Machine diagram at this time. </li></ul><h2>Deferrable Trigger</h2><p>A list of Triggers that are candidates to be retained by the State Machine if they trigger no transitions out of the State (not consumed). A deferred Trigger is retained until the State Machine reaches a State configuration where it is no longer deferred.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Deferable Trigger" ac:alt="Deferable Trigger"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-state-moving.png" /></ac:image><br />The Specification window for Event State moving - showing the editable Deferrable Trigger tag.</h6><p>As in the State Machine diagram in the following figure, the Elevator<strong> 'open'</strong> Event is deferred as long as the Elevator is moving. Only when the State transitions to <strong>'stopped'</strong> State again, the <strong>'open'</strong> Event will be executed because it is no longer deferred at this point.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Simulating deferred events" ac:alt="Simulating deferred events"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deferred-events-b.png" /></ac:image><br />      A deferred clause added to Event moving which causes the Event to delay until it is no longer deferred. </h6><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7629a836-d769-4cdc-a90e-83952b10907f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This version of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit does not support <strong>Deferred Events</strong> for Activity diagrams. They can now only be added to a State in a State Machine diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="691d9796-6ca0-48fc-82b8-6d01a42304ff"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122943877</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) State Machine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624793 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00326: (2026x) Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior

- page_id: `255624793`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624793/2026x+Defining+the+stopwatch+Classifier+Behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:04.489+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Class Element, which can be executed with Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit, must have its Classifier Behavior defined. The Classifier Behavior can be any kind of Behavior such as Activity, State Machine, and Interaction. This tutorial uses the State Machine Behavior to define the Behavior of the stopwatch.

To define the Classifier Behavior for the **StopWatch** Class, you have to create the Behavior on the StopWatch, which will be assigned later as the Classifier Behavior of the **StopWatch** automatically. 
 
 To define the Classifier Behavior for the **StopWatch** Class

1. Right-click the StopWatch symbol on the Class diagram and select New Diagram > State-Machine Diagram... . A new State Machine Element will be created under the StopWatch Class with a new State Machine diagram.
2. Name the State Machine diagram "StopWatch".
3. Make sure that the StopWatch State machine is the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class. To see the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class, right-click the StopWatch symbol on the Class diagram and select Specification from the context menu. The Specification window will open.
4. Select All from the Properties drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-class-stopwatch.png']
5. Scroll down to see the Classifier Behavior row, and you will see that the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class is defined by the StopWatch State Machine. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-class-stopwatch-c.png']

Next, we are going to create the StopWatch initial and final stages, and the four States (**Ready**, **Running**, **Paused**, and **Stopped**) in the initial stage as well.

To add an **Initial**stage to the **StopWatch**State Machine

- Click Initial on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the State Machine diagram to place the Initial stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavfc807e5dad35a6dc8eb8a2e2334bb90d.png']

To add the ready, running, paused, and stopped States

1. Click State on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the diagram to place a State. Name the created State "ready" .
2. Repeat Step 1 to create the " running" , " paused" , and " stopped" States. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavdb2618531755fa8d4b674c2de40b0379.png']

To add a final stage to the State machine diagram

- Click Final State on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the diagram to place the final stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav7c8723378243ad324a3743c62dca401d.png']

Now that the initial and final stages have been created, you will need to add the transitions between them and among the four States in the initial stage. 
 
To add a transition from the **Initial**stage to the **ready**State

1. Click a stage (the Initial stage in this example) that will be the source of the transition to be created. The smart manipulator toolbar will open.
2. Click the Transition icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddava072afa3d5c17146c05715796f526339.png']
3. Click a State (the ready State in this example) that will be the target of the transition. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav60294b81f5996dcd6bfa1060c5cb598a.png']
4. Repeat Steps 1 to 3 to create more transitions between the following States: from the ready to running State from the running to paused State from the running to stopped State from the paused to running State from the paused to stopped State from the stopped to ready State from the stopped State to the Final stage [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav0b3243bea9e2b36c84b6875f3524ffdf.png']
5. Create a looping transition to the running State by clicking the Transition to self icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav43be35b8d4a2413da8149a015837abdb.png']
6. Now your StopWatch State machine will look similar to the one shown in the following figure [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavb2013b68b56b9e5b343057299cb23010.png']

You will see that some of the created transitions have the SignalEvent triggers. These triggers need signal Elements and a package that is required to store those signal Elements. You can add a package and create the signal Elements by using the browser context menu. 
 
To add a new package for the signal Elements

1. Right-click the Model node in the containment browser and select New Element > Package .
2. Name the created package "signals" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav02f2e9636d8edccd1414fa29212758ed.png']

To create signal Elements

1. Right-click the signals package and select New Element > Signal .
2. Name the created signal "reset" . The reset signal will be created and stored in the signals package.
3. Repeat Steps 1 to 2 to create the split , start , stop , and unsplit signal Elements. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavbb4a178543c51f8a6410935f98cbc0ab.png']

To create signal Events for the transitions on the StopWatch State Machine diagram

- Drag a signal Element from the containment browser to a transition on the State Machine diagram. The signal Event will later be specified as the trigger of the transition. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav98b0652dae8cdc9b9239a0b08b2abf68.png']

To create a signal Event for each transition

1. Drag the start signal to the transition between the ready and running States.
2. Drag the split signal to the transition between the running and paused States.
3. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the running and stopped States.
4. Drag the unsplit signal to the transition between the paused and running States.
5. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the paused and stopped States.
6. Drag the reset signal to the transition between the stopped and ready States.
7. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the stopped and Final States. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6e722749fb60fdc6e89a10de0981a858.png']

Now each and every transition between the States has its own signal Event. Next, you will also need to create a signal Event for the **transition****to****self** of the running State. The signal Event that will be created as the trigger for the **transition****to****self** is called **TimeEvent**. 
 
To create a signal Event for the **transition****to****self**of the running State

1. Right-click the Transition to self of the running State and select Specification to open its Specification window.
2. Click the Categorized View button to open the properties of the transition in the categorized view.
3. Click the Event Type row in the Trigger category and select TimeEvent from the drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event.png']
4. Click the When row in the Trigger category and type "1s" to specify that the trigger will send the time Event every second. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event-b.png']
5. Click Close . You will see all of the created stages, States, transitions, and signal Events are shown on the StopWatch State Machine diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav1cfe524dfbea755379071dbc78160048.png']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624791 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00327: (2026x) Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities

- page_id: `255624791`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624791/2026x+Defining+the+stopwatch+operations+by+using+Activities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:04.239+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

984249231

984249234

984249232

This section defines the values of the elapsed time among the States in the StopWatch State Machine. When the stopwatch enters the **ready** State, the elapsed time defined by the Attribute **time:Integer[1]** should be reset to zero. In addition, the elapsed time should increment by 1 every second while the StopWatch is at the **running** State. Therefore, we need to add two new operations: (i) ResetTime and (ii) increaseTime to the StopWatch Class to define the elapsed time at the ready and running States. The **resetTime** operation will reset the elapsed time to zero, and the **increaseTime** operation will increment the elapsed time by one.

984249230

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="39f5646d-0bc3-47dd-901b-a69f559a0b60"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249231</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c14916f-a570-4a4c-b221-f076761ccb8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249234</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="459be496-a0fc-4b5d-bc77-6dae7f097baf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249232</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This section defines the values of the elapsed time among the States in the StopWatch State Machine. When the stopwatch enters the <strong>ready</strong> State, the elapsed time defined by the Attribute <strong>time:Integer[1]</strong> should be reset to zero. In addition, the elapsed time should increment by 1 every second while the StopWatch is at the <strong>running</strong> State. Therefore, we need to add two new operations: (i) ResetTime and (ii) increaseTime to the StopWatch Class to define the elapsed time at the ready and running States. The <strong>resetTime</strong> operation will reset the elapsed time to zero, and the <strong>increaseTime</strong> operation will increment the elapsed time by one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a4acebe4-25bd-4647-af0f-63230fba9da9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249230</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625688 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00328: (2026x) Defining Triggers on Transitions

- page_id: `255625688`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625688/2026x+Defining+Triggers+on+Transitions
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:29.292+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) State Machine simulation > (2026x) Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['triggers-on-transitions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Warning

1061801889

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Warning

1061801891

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Warning

1061801890

If you want a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Context, runtime object, and runtime value' space=''] to change its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MT'] on a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MT'], you need to define a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram elements' space='MT'] on the Transition by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assigning Event Type' space='MT']. A runtime object will change its State when it receives a Trigger on the Transition. A Trigger can be a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MT'], a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MT'], or a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MT'].

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The StopWatch's State Machine diagram with defined Triggers on the Transitions.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

Optionally, you can also use a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Send Signal Action' space='MT'] of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity' space=''] as a Signal Event of Transition. As shown in the following figure, the *stopped* State can wait for the *off* Send Signal Action of the *do*Activity to proceed to the final State.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The off Send Signal Action of the do Activity as a Signal Event of Transition.

#### WARNING: Warning

Warning

- According to UML Version 2.5.1, the initial Transition must not have Triggers or Guards. If the Initial Transition violates the rule, Simulation will print a warning message in the Console pane .
- Simulation also provides the validation rule to prevent the model error by displaying a warning in the Validation Results panel . You can either select the Check Model Before Execution option through validation and verification before running the simulation or select Pre-execution constraints from the [Validation Suite](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+dialog#Validationdialog-descrDescriptionofValidationdialogoptions) drop-down list in the **Validation** dialog and click Validate .

1061801888

**Related page**

- Signal properties mapping to behavior parameters

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3b503811-4d15-4904-afc5-4f24115da8ea"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801889</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bcfc06ba-69d7-48fc-9868-1fc1181d7ad2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801891</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9caa312-1679-4b43-8b97-72cb2e414058"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801890</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want a <ac:link ac:anchor="Runtimeobject"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[runtime object]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to change its <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="State" /></ac:link> on a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link>, you need to define a <ac:link ac:anchor="Trigger"><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Trigger]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> on the Transition by <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Assigning Event Type" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[assigning an Event Type to a Transition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. A runtime object will change its State when it receives a Trigger on the Transition. A Trigger can be a <ac:link ac:anchor="SignalEvent"><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Signal Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, a <ac:link ac:anchor="TimeEvent"><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Time Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, or a <ac:link ac:anchor="ChangeEvent"><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Change Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="StopWatch State Machine.png" /></ac:image>The StopWatch's State Machine diagram with defined Triggers on the Transitions.</h6><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ef68b471-2890-49cd-b761-4690446070d2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Optionally, you can also use a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Send Signal Action" /></ac:link> of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity" /></ac:link> as a Signal Event of Transition. As shown in the following figure, the <em>stopped</em> State can wait for the <em>off</em> Send Signal Action of the <em>do </em>Activity to proceed to the final State.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Send Signal Action.PNG" /></ac:image>The off Send Signal Action of the do Activity as a Signal Event of Transition.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="85d9561c-851f-4963-9b5b-d06c07870df8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Warning</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>According to UML Version 2.5.1, the initial Transition must not have Triggers or Guards. If the Initial Transition violates the rule, Simulation will print a warning message in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Console+pane">Console pane</a>.</li><li>Simulation also provides the validation rule to prevent the model error by displaying a warning in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+Results+panel">Validation Results panel</a>. You can either select the <strong>Check Model Before Execution</strong> option through <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Validation+and+verification">validation and verification</a> before running the simulation or select <strong>Pre-execution constraints</strong> from the <strong><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+dialog#Validationdialog-descrDescriptionofValidationdialogoptions">Validation Suite</a></strong> drop-down list in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+dialog"><strong>Validation</strong> dialog</a> and click <strong>Validate</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d29cb9b-7b89-4f4f-b721-fa0d66c1d605"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1061801888</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Signal+properties+mapping+to+behavior+parameters">Signal properties mapping to behavior parameters</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625923 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00329: (2026x) Diagram

- page_id: `255625923`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625923/2026x+Diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:14.373+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Model simulation > (2026x) Elements simulation
- labels: ['diagram-simulation', 'simulating-diagrams']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use the shortcut menu or the diagram toolbar to simulate a diagram. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will simulate the element, which is the context of the selected diagram, the same way it simulates a Behavior or a Class depending on the element which is the context of diagram. You can also specify the diagram as a target of a Simulation Configuration (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration' space=''] for more details).

To simulate a diagram do one of the following

- Right-click a diagram and select either Simulation > Run or Simulation > Run with Context ( if the context of the diagram is a Behavior). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- Click the **Run** button in the diagram toolbar and select either **Run** or **Run with Context** (if the context of the diagram is a Behavior). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To terminate diagram simulation do one of the following

- Click the **Terminate** button in the diagram toolbar. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- Click the **Terminate** button in the **Simulation** pane. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

You can also specify the diagram as a target of a Simulation Configuration (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link> for more details).</p><p><br /></p><p>To simulate a diagram do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a diagram and select either <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255629798 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00330: (2026x) Diagram animation on HTML UI

- page_id: `255629798`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255629798/2026x+Diagram+animation+on+HTML+UI
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-13T11:11:39.348+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) HTML UI
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Behavioral diagrams (Activity diagrams, State Machine diagrams, and Sequence Diagrams) and Composite Structure diagrams (Internal Block diagrams) can be embedded into HTML-generated user interfaces and animated.

Composite Structure diagrams only display activations and not run-time values.

##### Creating a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram

The Joystick.mdzip sample model is used to demonstrate how to create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram.

To create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram

1. Open the <modeling_tool_install_dir>\samples\simulation\Joystick.mdzip sample model.
2. Create a User Interface Modeling Diagram.
3. In the diagram, create a Frame and create a Panel inside the Frame. To learn more about the components of a User Interface Modeling Diagram, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''] .
4. Drag a State Machine Diagram to the Panel using a long drop and select the Create Diagram Knob Symbol command. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop State Machine Diagram.png']
5. Click the diagram symbol in the Panel to open the smart manipulator toolbar and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Eye Symbol.png'] to view the diagram in the Panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='Show Diagram Overview Content.png']
6. Drag the Classifier from the Containment tree to the Frame. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop Classifier.png']
7. Drag the Signals from the Containment tree to the Frame to create buttons. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop Signals.png'] After completing the steps above, the diagram should look like the one below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Model.png']
8. Drag the Frame from the Containment Tree to a Simulation Configuration. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop Frame on SimConfig.png']
9. In the Specification window of the Simulation Configuration, set the Show UI As HTML property to true .
10. Run the Simulation Configuration to simulate the created UI Mockup in the embedded browser. [ATTACHMENT filename='Embedded Browser.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Behavioral diagrams (Activity diagrams, State Machine diagrams, and Sequence Diagrams) and Composite Structure diagrams (Internal Block diagrams) can be embedded into HTML-generated user interfaces and animated.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3eda1cc-d707-49f0-9011-f68b11c52b7a"><ac:rich-text-body>Composite Structure diagrams only display activations and not run-time values.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Creating a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram</h3><p>The Joystick.mdzip sample model is used to demonstrate how to create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="bac564f3-dcdb-4683-91bf-5f42ab7c60fb">Open the <em>&lt;modeling_tool_install_dir&gt;\samples\simulation\Joystick.mdzip</em> sample model.</li><li data-uuid="75b506a8-e31f-4f97-8543-48c6eba8c913">Create a User Interface Modeling Diagram.</li><li data-uuid="91a62db8-1dcb-4552-b3fe-517046266895">In the diagram, create a Frame and create a Panel inside the Frame. To learn more about the components of a User Interface Modeling Diagram, refer to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UI modeling diagram simulation" /></ac:link>.</li><li data-uuid="464c39b4-5a44-4dd9-895b-1cc9d0b11e06">Drag a State Machine Diagram to the Panel using a long drop and select the <strong>Create Diagram Knob Symbol</strong> command.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="157"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop State Machine Diagram.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="fc05716e-cdec-45aa-b146-c50b0298f228">Click the diagram symbol in the Panel to open the smart manipulator toolbar and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Eye Symbol.png" /></ac:image>to view the diagram in the Panel.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="142"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Show Diagram Overview Content.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="ec202050-fdd4-4807-a7ba-c206a5f59370">Drag the Classifier from the Containment tree to the Frame.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop Classifier.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="01310b0a-9e30-4dc1-91e9-e19dd9d23fd5">Drag the Signals from the Containment tree to the Frame to create buttons.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop Signals.png" /></ac:image><br /><br />After completing the steps above, the diagram should look like the one below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Model.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="bfc1e979-0c78-44e8-9a54-00456a8cf186">Drag the Frame from the Containment Tree to a Simulation Configuration.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop Frame on SimConfig.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="f655047d-6623-410c-a5b4-57aaf5f81202">In the Specification window of the Simulation Configuration, set the <strong>Show UI As HTML</strong> property to <em>true</em>.</li><li data-uuid="dab9b7ad-72d5-46b3-b813-746b48aadcfa">Run the Simulation Configuration to simulate the created UI Mockup in the embedded browser.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Embedded Browser.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626106 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00331: (2026x) Disabling updates in Simulation panes

- page_id: `255626106`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626106/2026x+Disabling+updates+in+Simulation+panes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:31.947+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging
- labels: ['turning-off-updates-in-simulation-pane', 'disabling-updates-in-simulation-pane']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can turn off automatic updates of panes in the **Simulation** window by clicking [IMAGE alt='' src='']. Turning off auto-updates of panes will cause all of the panes (Sessions, Console, Variables, and Breakpoints) in the Simulation window to be disabled and the simulation speed to increase. This can be seen in the comparison of the Simulation window before turning off the auto-update option and after turning off the auto-update option in the following figures respectively.

The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is enabled

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is enabled.

The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is disabled

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is disabled.

After the auto-update of panes option is disabled, you can work on the model, make changes, or run it, but the actions that you are performing will not be displayed in any of the panes in the Simulation window. If you enable the option, the Actions that you have performed will appear in the panes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can turn off automatic updates of panes in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window by clicking <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Disable_autoupdate_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Disabling updates in Simulation panes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span>. Turning off auto-updates of panes will cause all of the panes (Sessions, Console, Variables, and Breakpoints) in the Simulation window to be disabled and the simulation speed to increase. This can be seen in the comparison of the Simulation window before turning off the auto-update option and after turning off the auto-update option in the following figures respectively.</p><p>The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is enabled</p><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="LockUpdate_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Disabling updates in Simulation panes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is enabled. </h6><p>The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is disabled</p><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DisabledUpdates.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Disabling updates in Simulation panes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is disabled.</h6><p>After the auto-update of panes option is disabled, you can work on the model, make changes, or run it, but the actions that you are performing will not be displayed in any of the panes in the Simulation window. If you enable the option, the Actions that you have performed will appear in the panes.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625296 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00332: (2026x) Displaying simulation information

- page_id: `255625296`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625296/2026x+Displaying+simulation+information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:41.289+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Animation > (2026x) Simulation information in diagrams
- labels: ['active-states-in-diagrams', 'active-state-images', 'runtime-values-in-diagrms', 'flowing-information-in-diagrams', 'animation-options']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

When simulating a model, you can choose to display simulation information not only in the Simulation window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can see runtime values directly on Part shapes or a flowing item with its name on a path.

#### INFO: Types of diagrams

Types of diagrams

You can display simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram. However, the flowing information on paths can also be shown in an Activity Diagram.

##### Displaying active States

In diagrams, active States can be displayed on the shapes ofall initialized Parts a similar way they are displayed on the Variables pane of the Simulation window (in square brackets).

#### NOTE: Exception

Exception

Active States are not displayed for Parts with the multiplicity of more than one.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The diagram illustrates how active States are displayed on Part shapes.

If you do not want to see active States in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Active States on Part Shapes**animation option. For more information, see .

##### Displaying active State images

You can display active State images on Part shapes by applying an image to a State or by assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration from which you execute your model. When States change during simulation, State images change as well.

#### NOTE: Important

Important

If you specify State images both by applying them to States and assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration, the images of the Image Switcher will be used when running the Simulation Configuration.

To display active State images when executing a model

1. Open the Specification window of the State you want to create an image for.
2. Click the specification cell of the Image property and select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='image_library.png'] to select an image from the default image library, click [ATTACHMENT filename='custom_image.png'] to select an image from your file system, or click [ATTACHMENT filename='image_URL.png'] to specify an image URL.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Here you can see how active State images are displayed when simulating a model.

If you run a Simulation Configuration, use an Image Switcher to display active State images as described below.

To display active State images when running a Simulation Configuration

1. Open the Simulation Configuration Diagram in which the Simulation Configuration is displayed and create an Image Switcher.
2. In the Specification window of the Image Switcher, click the Represents property specification cell and select the element for the states of which you want to create images.
3. For every active State image you want to create, create an Active Image element as the attribute of the Image Switcher. Creating Active Image attributesTo create an Active Image attribute for an Image Switcher, click **Active Image** in the diagram palette, then click the Image Switcher shape.
4. In the Specification window of every Active Image specify the following properties:
  - **Image** property - select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to select an image from the default image library, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to select an image from your file system, or click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to specify an image URL.
  - **Active Element** property - select the State which the Active Image represents.
5. Drag the Image Switcher created in step 1 to the Simulation Configuration shape. This way the Image Switcher is specified as the value of the UI property of the Simulation Configuration.

##### Displaying runtime values

In diagrams, you can display the runtime values of Part properties and Port labels the same way they are displayed in the Variables pane of the Simulation window. Runtime values are displayed next to property names on Part shapes and Port labels, instead of the default property values. Note that when you run a simulation, property types are not displayed.

Runtime values can be displayed only for the following types of properties:

- Primitive
- ValueType
- Enumeration
- Custom DataType

#### INFO: Automated Requirement verification

Automated Requirement verification

Runtime values on Part shapes are highlighted in green (the Requirement is satisfied) or red (the Requirement is not satisfied) when the automated Requirement verification is performed.

In addition, a tooltip with the text of unsatisfied Requirements or constraints appears when you hover the mouse pointer over Part or Value Property shapes. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Part shapes during model simulation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Port labels during model simulation.

#### TIP: Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

If a runtime value does not fit in a shape and is cut off, resize the shape as described below.

1. Select the shape that you want to resize.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Click the Resize to Fit Runtime Values icon on the shape (displayed below). [ATTACHMENT filename='resizing_shapes.png']
  2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='resize_to_fit_runtme_values.png'] in the diagram toolbar.

In addition, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Manipulating simulation information' space=''] directly in diagrams and change simulation results in real-time. If you do not want to see runtime values in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the animation options. For more information, see .

##### Displaying flowing information

You can choose to display flowing information on animated paths when executing a model. In diagrams based on a Composite Structure Diagram, the flowing item is displayed as a moving triangle with a label showing the item name (see the image below). In Activity Diagrams, the flowing item is displayed as a bubble.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this Internal Block Definition Diagram, you can see the *neutral* signal flowing from the *GearShift* to the *TCU*.

You can also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Manipulating simulation information' space=''] directly from a Part shape in a diagram and see the flowing item on a path. If you do not want to see flowing information in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Flowing Information** animation option. For more information, see .

##### Enabling and disabling animation options

You can disable or enable animation options, such as displaying active States and runtime values on Part shapes. The steps below explain how to change option values for the whole project and a specific Simulation Configuration. Note that by default all simulation animation options are enabled.

****

To disable or enable animation options for a project

1. In the main menu, go to Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, expand the General option group and select Simulation . [ATTACHMENT filename='Project Options.png']
3. In the option specification area on the right side of the dialog, disable or enable the following animation options:
  - Show Annotations- if the option set to true , the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled . Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Active States on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active States are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Runtime Values on Port Labels**- if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Flowing Information - if the option set to true , the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to false , paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.
  - Show Active State Images on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active State images are displayed on Part shapes.
4. Click OK to save the changes.

To disable or enable animation options for a Simulation Configuration

1. Open the Specification window of a Simulation Configuration element. [ATTACHMENT filename='Simulation Configuration.png']
2. Disable or enable the following animation options located in the Animation category:
  - Show Annotations- if the option set to true , the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled . Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Active States on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active States are displayed on Part Property shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Active State Images**- Select**On Part shapes**to show active State images on Part shapes. Select**In the Simulation UI**to show active State images in the simulation UI dialog. To show images both on Part shapes and in the simulation UI dialog, select**Both**.
  - Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Runtime Values on Port Labels**- if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Flowing Information - if the option set to true , the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to false , paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.
3. Close the Specification window.

Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><strong>Show Runtime Values on Port Labels</strong>- </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to <em>false</em>, paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.</li><li><strong>Show Active State Images on Part Shapes </strong>- if the option set to <em>true</em>, active State images are displayed on Part shapes.</li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to save the changes.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To disable or enable animation options for a Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of a Simulation Configuration element.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulation Configuration.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying simulation information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Disable or enable the following animation options located in the <strong>Animation</strong> category:<br /><ul><li><strong>Show Annotations-</strong><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled</span>. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, active States are displayed on Part Property shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><p><strong>Show Active State Images</strong><span> - Select </span><strong>On Part shapes</strong><span> to show active State images on Part shapes. Select </span><strong>In the Simulation UI</strong><span> to show active State images in the simulation UI dialog. To show images both on Part shapes and in the simulation UI dialog, select </span><strong>Both</strong><span>.</span></p></li><li><strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><strong>Show Runtime Values on Port Labels</strong>- </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to <em>false</em>, paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.</li></ul></li><li>Close the Specification window.</li></ol><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626390 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00333: (2026x) Distribution Extensions

- page_id: `255626390`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626390/2026x+Distribution+Extensions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:42.410+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation of SysML models
- labels: ['non-normative-extensions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Distributed Property is a property of a Block or a Value Type used to apply a probability distribution to the values of the property. Specific distributions can be defined by applying a Subclass of the «DistributedProperty» stereotype to a property according to[OMG SysML 1.4](http://sysml.org/docs/specs/OMGSysML-v1.4-15-06-03.pdf), E.7 Distribution Extensions. The Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three types of distributions:Uniform, Normal, and Triangular.

To set a distributed property

1. Select a property and double-click it to open the Specification window.
2. Select Applied Stereotype and click [...] to include a distribution property.
3. Specify the required properties. These include **Mean**, **Standard Deviation**, etc. for «normal» and **Min**, **Max**, etc. for other distributed properties. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click Close . The distributed property will be applied.
5. Run the simulation model. Depending on the applied stereotype, the distributed properties will be initialized with a random value, such as normal distribution, constant distribution between min & max values. You can review sampling results by running the model with association end multiplicity (e.g., 100), and keep the result with «CSVExport» for analysis.
6. Results of the distributed property «normal» (with 100000 samples) with a Mean value of 10 and a Standard Deviation value of 2. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. You can apply a «uniform» distributed property stereotype with Min and Max properties (e.g., 0 and 100) to plot a uniform distribution chart. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. You can apply a «triangular» distributed property stereotype with Min, Max, and Peak properties to plot a triangular distribution chart. 
The sample project used is *<modeling_tool_installation_directory>\samples\simulation\AircraftProjectAnalysis.mdzip*. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Supported SysML elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Requirements traceability from the Variables pane' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports t</span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">hree types of distributions: </span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Uniform, Normal, and Triangular.</span></p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard">To set a distributed property</p><hr /><ol><li>Select a property and double-click it to open the Specification window.</li><li>Select <strong>Applied Stereotype</strong> and click [...] to include a distribution property.</li><li><p>Specify the required properties. These include <strong>Mean</strong>, <strong>Standard Deviation</strong>, etc. for «normal» and <strong>Min</strong>, <strong>Max</strong>, etc. for other distributed properties.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="non-normativeExt.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Distribution Extensions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>. The distributed property will be applied.</li><li>Run the simulation model. Depending on the applied stereotype, the distributed properties will be initialized with a random value, such as normal distribution, constant distribution between min &amp; max values. You can review sampling results by running the model with association end multiplicity (e.g., 100), and keep the result with «CSVExport» for analysis.</li><li><p style="text-align: left;">Results of the distributed property «normal» (with 100000 samples) with a Mean value of 10 and a Standard Deviation value of 2.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Distribution.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Distribution Extensions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can apply a «uniform» distributed property stereotype with Min and Max properties (e.g., 0 and 100) to plot a uniform distribution chart.</span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Unifrom_Distribution.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Distribution Extensions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can apply a «triangular» distributed property stereotype with Min, Max, and Peak properties to plot a triangular distribution chart.<br />The sample project used is <em style="text-align: left;">&lt;modeling_tool_installation_directory&gt;\samples\simulation\AircraftProjectAnalysis.mdzip</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Triangular_Distribution.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Distribution Extensions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Supported SysML elements" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Requirements traceability from the Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626166 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00334: (2026x) Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins

- page_id: `255626166`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626166/2026x+Dummy+tokens+of+Actions+through+Output+Pins
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:33.092+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation > (2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models
- labels: ['dummy-tokens']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1918270852

1918270855

1918270854

Simulation Toolkit has an option to support dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior. Business-level abstract diagrams can be executed and animated because Simulation will automatically create new objects according to type of those Output Pins if unspecified, even though this action will break fUML semantics. You can set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option (true by default) in the **Project Options Simulation** dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Objects are automatically created according to type of unspecified Output Pins, e.g., *order*.

From the figure above, the Output Pin returns an object (*ro* is *order@203c1eec*). If the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option is set false, the Output Pin will have no value.

To set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option

1. From the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. On the left pane, click **Simulation**.
3. Under the **fUML Engine** group, set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option to **true**.
4. Select **OK**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin.png'] The Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin option in the Project Options Simulation dialog.

1918270850

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity simulation' space='']

Business-level abstract diagrams can be executed and animated because Simulation will automatically create new objects according to type of those Output Pins if unspecified, even though this action will break fUML semantics. You can set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option (true by default) in the <strong>Project Options Simulation</strong> dialog.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Dummy Token of Action through Output Pin.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Objects are automatically created according to type of unspecified Output Pins, e.g., <em>order</em>.</h6><p>From the figure above, the Output Pin returns an object (<em>ro</em> is <em>order@203c1eec</em>). If the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option is set false, the Output Pin will have no value.</p><p><br /></p><p>To set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option</p><hr /><ol><li>From the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt;<strong> Project</strong>. The <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog opens.</li><li><p>On the left pane, click <strong>Simulation</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Under the <strong>fUML Engine</strong> group, set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option to <strong>true</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Select <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><h6>The Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin option in the Project Options Simulation dialog.</h6></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ece79763-d0bd-4ecd-ab7f-ce9f27dabee7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270850</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624729 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00335: (2026x) Duration analysis

- page_id: `255624729`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624729/2026x+Duration+analysis
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:56.830+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation
- labels: ['duration-analysis']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of calculating the minimum, maximum, and average duration of any Activity. It uses the values taken from duration constraints applied to each element in an Activity as the basis to calculate the Activity's duration.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit can perform an analysis of activity duration in the two following scenarios

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
