<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626172 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00336: (2026x) Duration analysis on executed traces

- page_id: `255626172`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626172/2026x+Duration+analysis+on+executed+traces
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:33.232+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation > (2026x) Duration analysis
- labels: ['executed-trace-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

144626827

144626829

144626828

You can also perform an analysis of Activity duration after an Activity has been simulated. Prior to the analysis, be sure that the simulation log has been configured and the value of a Record Activation tag is **true** (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation log' space=''] for more information about the simulation log).

To see the duration of a visited element

1. Right-click the simulation log and select **Simulation** > **Analyze Duration** **of** **Executed Trace**. The **Select Execution** **Session** **and** **Activity** dialog will open (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Analyze Duration of Executed Trace Menu on the Simulation Log Context Menu. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Select Execution Session and Activity dialog.
2. Select a session and an Activity and click the **Calculate Duration** button. The result will appear in the Simulation **Console** pane. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Duration of executed trace in the Simulation Console pane.

144626826

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Duration analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Duration analysis on visited elements' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626155 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00337: (2026x) Duration analysis on visited elements

- page_id: `255626155`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626155/2026x+Duration+analysis+on+visited+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:32.795+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation > (2026x) Duration analysis
- labels: ['visited-element-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

144449098

144449101

144449099

The duration analysis on visited elements feature is not enabled by default. To enable it, you need a TimeHandler and register it in the execution listener of the <<SimulationConfig>>. You can create the TimeHandler by creating a new Class and apply the <<TimeHandler>> stereotype.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Enabling duration analysis on visited elements in the <<SimulationConfig>>.

You can view the duration of a running Activity by using the diagram's context menu **Analyze Duration** **of** **Visited** **Elements**.

To see the duration of visited elements

1. Pause a currently running Activity by either adding a breakpoint or clicking the Pause button.
2. Right-click the diagram and select **Simulation** > **Analyze Duration****of****Visited****Elements**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Analyze duration on visited elements menu on the diagram context menu.
3. The minimum, maximum, and average duration of the visited elements together with the simulated trace listing all of the visited elements will appear in the Simulation **Console** pane. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Duration of visited elements in the Simulation Console.

144449097

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Duration analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Duration analysis on executed traces' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626129 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00338: (2026x) Duration constraint

- page_id: `255626129`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626129/2026x+Duration+constraint
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:24.894+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Interaction simulation > (2026x) Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['duration-constraint']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

380966344

380966346

380966345

Simulation allows you to evaluate duration constraints between Call Messages and Reply Messages, e.g., you can specify that a Call Message must get a Reply Message in 500 ms, so the actual duration of operation method execution must be compared with the duration constraint of 500 ms. The comparison is done as an evaluation of the constraint and will generate a fail value for *testcases* or stop at breakpoints on the [Constraint Failure As Breakpoint option](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) SimulationConfig+stereotype). However, it does not support the delay time of a message.

The default time unit of the duration constraint is millisecond (ms), but you can also use other time units by specifying them after the duration value, e.g., you can use 10s to denote 10 seconds. If you specify both the minimum and maximum values, Simulation will use the maximum value of the duration constraint to delay the delivery of a Message.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Sequence diagram showing how a Duration Constraint {500} can be used to evaluate between the Call Message and Reply Message.

The figure above shows that *cons* sends a Call Message to *bank* to verify an account (12345678) with an expected duration constraint of 500 ms from the Reply Message. The evaluation in the first run is failed because the actual duration (431 ms) is less than 500 ms, while another run passes because the actual duration (710 ms) is greater than 500 ms.

380966342

**Related pages**

- Message
- Internal simulation clock
- Specification window

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f1e1dd4a-9f5c-4bad-9746-2d91c71c9e14"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">380966344</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72cbdc05-6107-4139-9a65-0e063812c7e5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">380966346</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="73a2512a-a8a3-4e1f-8ef8-896d3f2f9d14"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">380966345</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Simulation allows you to evaluate duration constraints between Call Messages and Reply Messages, e.g., you can specify that a Call Message must get a Reply Message in 500 ms, so the actual duration of operation method execution must be compared with the duration constraint of 500 ms. The comparison is done as an evaluation of the constraint and will generate a fail value for <em>testcases</em> or stop at breakpoints on the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) SimulationConfig+stereotype">Constraint Failure As Breakpoint option</a>. However, it does not support the delay time of a message.</p><p>The default time unit of the duration constraint is millisecond (ms), but you can also use other time units by specifying them after the duration value, e.g., you can use 10s to denote 10 seconds. If you specify both the minimum and maximum values, Simulation will use the maximum value of the duration constraint to delay the delivery of a Message.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Duration constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Sequence diagram showing how a Duration Constraint {500} can be used to evaluate between the Call Message and Reply Message.</h6><p>The figure above shows that <em>cons</em> sends a Call Message to <em>bank</em> to verify an account (12345678) with an expected duration constraint of 500 ms from the Reply Message. The evaluation in the first run is failed because the actual duration (431 ms) is less than 500 ms, while another run passes because the actual duration (710 ms) is greater than 500 ms.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="034ebc7d-ba8c-46c8-ba73-bfcfcceaf3ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">380966342</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Message">Message</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Internal+simulation+clock">Internal simulation clock</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Specification+window">Specification window</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626312 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00339: (2026x) Dynamic constraint

- page_id: `255626312`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626312/2026x+Dynamic+constraint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:38.400+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['dynamic-constraint']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The concept of dynamic constraint is to allow parametric calculation of a Constraint Property with another constraint rather than one from its type (Constraint Block). Such constraint can be dynamically obtained during the simulation from typing Constraint Block or one of its subtypes. This section uses the *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip* sample to demonstrate this concept.

To dynamically apply different constraints to a Constraint Property

1. Create a Constraint Block with all needed Constraint Parameter(s).
2. Create Constraint Blocks which inherit the Constraint Block created in step 1.
3. Specify the constraint expression of each Constraint Blocks created in step 2.
4. Type the Constraint Property with the Constraint Block created in step 1.
5. Create a behavior, for example State Machine, that will assign the runtime object of different Constraint Blocks created in step 2 to the Constraint Property.

In *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip*, we will dynamically assign a constraint to the **Valuation_Rule** Constraint Property, which is typed by the **Valuation** Constraint Block (having two subtypes: **Long** **Valuation** and **Short** **Valuation** Constraint Blocks with two different constraint expressions) as shown in the following figure. The context of this simulation will be the **System** Block.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property.

In the following figure, the state machine is used as the classifier behavior of the **System** block. The **Calculate Contract Value** **for Long** **Position** and **Calculate Contract Value** **for Short** **Position** states apply different valuations to the **System** block via its entry activity.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
System Classifier Behavior State Machine.

The **Calculate Contract Value** **for Long** **Position**'s entry activity assigns the runtime value of the **Long** **Valuation** Constraint Block to the **Valuation_Rule** Constraint Property (see the first figure below). Consequently, the values of the constraint parameters will be calculated with the constraint expression specified in **Long** **Valuation**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State.

Similarly, the second figure below demonstrates the **Short** **Valuation** dynamic assignment. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the **ForwardContractValuation.mdzip** sample model that comes with the modeling tool.

To open this sample, do one of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='ForwardContractValuation.mdzip'] **
- Find in the modeling tool <*install_root*>\samples\simulation\Parametrics\ForwardContractValuation.mdzip.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specifying the language for the expression' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Value binding' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Evaluation with causality' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Communicating with evaluators through simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Built-in Math' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Integration with external Evaluators' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Trade study analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Sample project' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>The concept of dynamic constraint is to allow parametric calculation of a Constraint Property with another constraint rather than one from its type (Constraint Block). Such constraint can be dynamically obtained during the simulation from typing Constraint Block or one of its subtypes. This section uses the <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em> sample to demonstrate this concept. </p><p>To dynamically apply different constraints to a Constraint Property</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Constraint Block with all needed Constraint Parameter(s).</li><li>Create Constraint Blocks which inherit the Constraint Block created in step 1.</li><li>Specify the constraint expression of each Constraint Blocks created in step 2.</li><li>Type the Constraint Property with the Constraint Block created in step 1.</li><li>Create a behavior, for example State Machine, that will assign the runtime object of different Constraint Blocks created in step 2 to the Constraint Property. </li></ol><p>In <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em>, we will dynamically assign a constraint to the <strong>Valuation_Rule</strong> Constraint Property, which is typed by the <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Block (having two subtypes: <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> and <strong>Short</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Blocks with two different constraint expressions) as shown in the following figure. The context of this simulation will be the <strong>System</strong> Block. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property" ac:alt="A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint01.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dynamic constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property.</h6><p>In the following figure, the state machine is used as the classifier behavior of the <strong>System</strong> block. The <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Long</strong> <strong>Position</strong> and <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Short</strong> <strong>Position</strong> states apply different valuations to the <strong>System</strong> block via its entry activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="System Classifier Behavior State Machine" ac:alt="System Classifier Behavior State Machine"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint02.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dynamic constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />System Classifier Behavior State Machine.</h6><p>The <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Long</strong> <strong>Position</strong>'s entry activity assigns the runtime value of the <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Block to the <strong>Valuation_Rule</strong> Constraint Property (see the first figure below). Consequently, the values of the constraint parameters will be calculated with the constraint expression specified in <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State" ac:alt="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint03.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dynamic constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State.</h6><p>Similarly, the second figure below demonstrates the <strong>Short</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> dynamic assignment. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State" ac:alt="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint04.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dynamic constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State.</h6><h2>Sample model</h2><p>The model used in the figures on this page is the <strong>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</strong> sample model that comes with the modeling tool.</p><p>To open this sample, do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Download <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="ForwardContractValuation.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Dynamic constraint" /></ri:attachment></ac:link><span class="confluence-link"><em><br /></em></span></li><li>Find in the modeling tool <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation\Parametrics\ForwardContractValuation.mdzip.</em></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specifying the language for the expression" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625204 space=MMA version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00340: (2026x) Elements simulation

- page_id: `255625204`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625204/2026x+Elements+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:33.131+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Model simulation
- labels: ['element-simulation', 'simulating-elements', 'unrestored-unknown-attachment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1331316376

1331316378

1331316375

**On this page**

1331316377

You can run a model simulation through the shortcut menu and the diagram toolbar.

To run a model simulation through the shortcut menu

1. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click an element shape on a diagram and select Simulation > Run . [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0001.png']
  - Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select Simulation > Run. [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0002.png']
2. In the toolbar of the open Simulation window, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to run the model. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To run a model through the diagram toolbar

1. Do any of the following:
  - In the diagram toolbar, click the Run or Run with Context button to run a diagram whose context is Class or Behavior (Composite Structure diagram, Activity diagram, State-Machine diagram, Use Case diagram, or Sequence diagram). Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the class or the behavior which is the context of the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_from_diagram_toolbar.png']
  - In a Class or a Package diagram, select the element you want to run and click the Run or Run with Context button on the diagram toolbar. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the selected element on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_from_diagram_toolbar2.png']
2. In the toolbar of the open Simulation window, click the Start button to run the model.

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use the following API to evaluate an expression

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to evaluate expressions in ALH API:

```text

```

If no language is defined, the default language will be used (JavaScript Rhino).

The following scripting languages are available by default:

- BeanShell Engine [beanshell, bsh]
- Built-in Math [Built-in Math, math, Math Engine, math engine, built-in]
- Groovy Scripting Engine [groovy, Groovy]
- JEXL Engine [JEXL, Jexl, jexl, JEXL2, Jexl2, jexl2]
- JavaScript Rhino [rhino, rhino-nonjdk, js, JavaScript, javascript, ECMAScript, ecmascript, JavaScript Rhino]
- jython [python, jython]

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can use the following API to evaluate an expression</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ce49c377-e823-4b35-a913-3e735913e826"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.evaluate("expression")
ALH.evaluate("language", "expression")
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following code fragment shows how to evaluate expressions in ALH API:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98841b13-e01d-4feb-a58b-fc2d375b13ca"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = ALH.evaluate("a+b");
result = ALH.evaluate("Python", "a+b")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If no language is defined, the default language will be used (JavaScript Rhino).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00d7b7a5-90f8-45e3-b8ca-09d42aebd349"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following scripting languages are available by default:</p><ul><li>BeanShell Engine [beanshell, bsh]</li><li>Built-in Math [Built-in Math, math, Math Engine, math engine, built-in]</li><li>Groovy Scripting Engine [groovy, Groovy]</li><li>JEXL Engine [JEXL, Jexl, jexl, JEXL2, Jexl2, jexl2]</li><li>JavaScript Rhino [rhino, rhino-nonjdk, js, JavaScript, javascript, ECMAScript, ecmascript, JavaScript Rhino]</li><li>jython [python, jython]</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626260 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00342: (2026x) Evaluating expressions

- page_id: `255626260`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626260/2026x+Evaluating+expressions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:36.208+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['expression-evaluation', 'evaluating-expressions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

402791423

402791426

1240126456

**On this page**

4

402791425

This section explains how to evaluate the mathematical equations and logical expressions in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides constraint parameters substitution in the expressions to improve the human readability of the constraint parameters. All user interface objects (including tooltips, Console panel, and Variables panel related to a constraint property connected to a value property) will display the value property name (actual binding) instead of the constraint parameter name. This feature is applicable at the user interface level only, not at the original constraint property. For example, “Width” and “Height” (value properties) are used instead of “w” and “h” (parameter properties) in constraints, as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Constraint parameters substitution in expressions.

##### Parametric updates when States change

Simulation can check the object State during the evaluation. For example, if an elevator is moving, the door must be closed, or if the car is moving, the speed must be more than 0.

- Y ou can use [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Action scripts APIs' space=''] (or _*context*_ ) in the Specification of a constraint in order to access the runtime object of the owner of the Constraint property.
- If the State check, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Checking Behaviors' space=''] , is used in the Specification of a constraint, the constraint will be re-evaluated on a State change with the parametric context and any objects passed as parameters into the constraint. The State change supported on simple, composite, orthogonal, and submachine.
- The Parametric update occurs after the State activation, so it depends on the selected [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State activation semantics' space=''] option in the Project Option dialog:
  - 
    - If Before entry (by default) is set, the following sequence applies: the State will be activated, the Parametric will be updated, and the Entry behavior will be simulated.
    - If After entry is set, the following sequence applies: the Entry behavior will be simulated, the state will be activated, and the Parametric will be updated.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The constraint re-evaluated on a State change in the Parametric of the context object

##### Parametric updates when Activities start or end

Simulation can check whether there is a specified Activity being executed at that time in Object_ by using the **performs(activity String)** function, e.g., *self.performs("Activity_1")* will return **true** when *Activity_1* is being executed under itself and return **false** if it is not executed under other Object_ or anywhere else. When the condition expression is evaluated at the first time, a listener for Activity execution will register the specified Activity in the *performs("Activity_1")* function. The listener will re-evaluate the condition expression when the context object starts or ends executing Activities.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4f42111-687a-4748-b5d3-81a587afae04"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791423</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c45b9e53-7c68-40ef-9eaf-7b925e0725fe"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791426</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c24489a2-4d22-42bb-9442-a563aa2cc2ce"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1240126456</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8cc367ee-3cd0-4caf-91db-cd6a844fef3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="66bb8bf7-3b02-4c09-b2aa-d8e6dc365783"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791425</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="Textbody">This section explains how to evaluate the mathematical equations and logical expressions in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit.</p><p class="Textbody">Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides constraint parameters substitution in the expressions to improve the human readability of the constraint parameters. All user interface objects (including tooltips, Console panel, and Variables panel related to a constraint property connected to a value property) will display the value property name (actual binding) instead of the constraint parameter name. This feature is applicable at the user interface level only, not at the original constraint property. ac:ref="380768b9-4762-48ca-9fa4-f9a31ee56770">The constraint re-evaluated on a State change in the Parametric of the contex</ac:inline-comment-marker>t object</h6><h3 style="text-align: String)</strong> function, e.g., <em>self.performs(&quot;Activity_1&quot;)</em> will return <strong>true</strong> when <em>Activity_1</em> is being executed under itself and return <strong>false</strong> if it is not executed under other Object_ or anywhere else. When the condition expression is evaluated at the first time, a listener for Activity execution will register the specified Activity in the <em>performs(&quot;Activity_1&quot;)</em> function. The listener will re-evaluate the condition expression when the context object starts or ends executing Activities.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626272 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00343: (2026x) Evaluating strings from command input

- page_id: `255626272`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626272/2026x+Evaluating+strings+from+command+input
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:36.788+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator > (2026x) Built-in Math
- labels: ['evaluating-strings']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can type generic mathematical equations directly in command input of the Simulation Console when it prompts for the built-in math. The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10;
- math» y = 20;
- math» z = x + y

z = 30.0000 (the calculation result) will be displayed on the simulation console.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The result of a calculation expression that ends with a semicolon (;) in the built-in Math will be assigned to the corresponding variable in the selected built-in math environment. It will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

Or, if you type, e.g., in command input of the Simulation Console, the examples are as follows

- math» a = true;
- math» b = false;
- math» c = a & b;

If **false** is the result of a calculation, it will be assigned to the variable c, but it will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

If an expression does not contain any assignment operators, the result will be assigned to the variable 'ans'. The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10;
- math» 20 + x
- ans = 30.0000 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

You can calculate multiple expressions at the same time by ending each expression with a semicolon (;). The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10; y = 20; z = x + y; a = z / x
- a = 3 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can type generic mathematical equations directly in command input of the Simulation Console when it prompts for the built-in math. The examples are as follows </p><ul><li>math» x = 10;</li><li>math» y = 20;</li><li>math» z = x + y </li></ul><p style="margin-left: 90.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline _mce_tagged_br" />z = 30.0000 (the calculation result) will be displayed on the simulation console. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7dc5773-083b-42e2-adb0-4f38479bcf86"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The result of a calculation expression that ends with a semicolon (;) in the built-in Math will be assigned to the corresponding variable in the selected built-in math environment. It will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Or, if you type, e.g., in command input of the Simulation Console, the examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» a = true;</li><li>math» b = false;</li><li>math» c = a &amp; b; </li></ul><p>If <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">false</strong> is the result of a calculation, it will be assigned to the variable c, but it will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </p><p>If an expression does not contain any assignment operators, the result will be assigned to the variable 'ans'. The examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» x = 10;</li><li>math» 20 + x</li><li>ans = 30.0000 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </li></ul><p>You can calculate multiple expressions at the same time by ending each expression with a semicolon (;). The examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» x = 10; y = 20; z = x + y; a = z / x </li><li>a = 3 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </li></ul><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626300 space=MMA version=4 -->
## PAGE 00344: (2026x) Evaluation with causality

- page_id: `255626300`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626300/2026x+Evaluation+with+causality
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-04-13T12:57:59.340+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['evaluation-with-causality']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

As described in the section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Mathematical equation' space=''], the parametric evaluator is capable of solving expressions in the mathematical equation to find the value of the target from the value of the given. We could say that the target is an unknown value that you want to find and the given is a known input value. Normally, a target is a variable on the left-hand side of an equation and a given is a variable on the right-hand side.

Sometimes, however, you know the value of the variable on the left-hand side of an equation and need to find the value of the variable on the right-hand side. You can use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to obtain the given variable if you integrate an external evaluator, which supports solving symbolic equations, to Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. MATLAB with [Symbolic Math Toolbox](https://www.mathworks.com/products/symbolic.html) is one of the external evaluators you can use.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

As a prerequisite, you must integrate MATLAB with [Symbolic Math Toolbox](https://www.mathworks.com/products/symbolic.html) successfully. See also [Integration with MATLAB](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Integration+with+MATLAB).

If the language that defines an expression of a constraint block needs to be solved by an external evaluator that is capable of solving symbolic equations, you can specify what property is the target and the given through the **Causality** column in the **Variables** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using MATLAB to evaluate mathematical equations.

The preceding figure shows the block object **Circle** in the **Variables** pane. It has a constraint property typed by the constraint block **Circle** **Area**. The expression of the constraint block is **{area** **=** **3.14159** *** **(radius** **^** **2)}**. The default causality of **area** and **radius** will be **target** and **given**respectively. However, you can click the drop-down list box in the **Causality** column to change the causality of **area** and **radius** to be **given** and **target**. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then evaluate the value of **radius** from the given value of **area**.

The expression of the constraint block **Circle** **Area** in the figure shows two roots: (i) a positive value 2.8209 and (ii) a negative value -2.8209. The parametric evaluator needs only one root to evaluate the value of a radius from the given value of an area. Therefore, the **Roots** **selection**dialog will open for you to select which root you want as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Roots Selection dialog.

The number of roots varies according to the expression and the multiplicity of a property, which is the target. Therefore, it is possible to select more than one root. The following scenarios show you how to work with multiple roots. 
 
**Scenario 1** 
The following figure shows the constraint **Test** **Multiple** **Root** **1** is applied to block **A**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Constraint Test Multiple Root 1 is Applied to Block A

**Scenario 2** 
The given types of causality of y and x are given and target respectively. Once Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit finished executing block **A** and performing a parametric simulation to satisfy the constraint **Test** **Multiple** **Root** **1**, the equation would result in three values of x and all are the possible roots for the equation. Therefore, the **Roots selection** dialog would open, allowing you to select one of these three values as the root (see the following figure).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Roots Selection Dialog.

**Scenario 3** 
However, you may select more than one value as the root. But, the number of values you can select cannot be more than the upper multiplicity. Otherwise, an error will occur and an **Error** message dialog will open. The following figure shows that *two* values were selected as the roots when in fact, the upper multiplicity of x value property was *one*. Therefore, an error occurred and the **Error** dialog would open.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
An error occurs when the Number of Roots Selected is Greater than the Upper Multiplicity.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specifying the language for the expression' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Value binding' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Dynamic constraint' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Communicating with evaluators through simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Built-in Math' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Integration with external Evaluators' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Trade study analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Sample project' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>As described in the section <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Mathematical equation" /></ac:link>, the parametric evaluator is capable of solving expressions in the mathematical equation to find the value of the target from the value of the given. We could say that the target is an unknown value that you want to find and the given is a known input value. Normally, a target is a variable on the left-hand side of an equation and a given is a variable on the right-hand side. </p><p>Sometimes, however, you know the value of the variable on the left-hand side of an equation and need to find the value of the variable on the right-hand side. You can use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to obtain the given variable if you integrate an external evaluator, which supports solving symbolic equations, to Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. MATLAB with <a class="external-link" href="https://www.mathworks.com/products/symbolic.html">Symbolic Math Toolbox</a> is one of the external evaluators you can use.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="96aa5f0a-4b22-4b19-9f32-ba42ff805ea1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>As a prerequisite, you must integrate MATLAB with <a class="external-link" href="https://www.mathworks.com/products/symbolic.html" title="Follow link">Symbolic Math Toolbox</a> successfully. See also <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Integration+with+MATLAB">Integration with MATLAB</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If the language that defines an expression of a constraint block needs to be solved by an external evaluator that is capable of solving symbolic equations, you can specify what property is the target and the given through the <strong>Causality</strong> column in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:alt="Using MATLAB to evaluate mathematical equations "><ri:attachment ri:filename="variables-pane.png" /></ac:image><br />Using MATLAB to evaluate mathematical equations.</h6><p>The preceding figure shows the block object <strong>Circle</strong> in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. It has a constraint property typed by the constraint block <strong>Circle</strong> <strong>Area</strong>. The expression of the constraint block is <strong>{area</strong> <strong>=</strong> <strong>3.14159</strong> *** <strong>(radius</strong> <strong>^</strong> <strong>2)}</strong>. The default causality of <strong>area</strong> and <strong>radius</strong> will be <strong>target</strong> and <strong>given </strong>respectively. However, you can click the drop-down list box in the <strong>Causality</strong> column to change the causality of <strong>area</strong> and <strong>radius</strong> to be <strong>given</strong> and <strong>target</strong>. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will then evaluate the value of <strong>radius</strong> from the given value of <strong>area</strong>. </p><p>The expression of the constraint block <strong>Circle</strong> <strong>Area</strong> in the figure shows two roots: (i) a positive value 2.8209 and (ii) a negative value -2.8209. The parametric evaluator needs only one root to evaluate the value of a radius from the given value of an area. Therefore, the <strong>Roots</strong> <strong>selection </strong>dialog will open for you to select which root you want as shown in the following figure. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:alt="Roots Selection Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="roots_selectdialog.png" /></ac:image><br />Roots Selection dialog.</h6><p>The number of roots varies according to the expression and the multiplicity of a property, which is the target. Therefore, it is possible to select more than one root. The following scenarios show you how to work with multiple roots. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><strong>Scenario 1</strong> <br class="atl-forced-newline" />The following figure shows the constraint <strong>Test</strong> <strong>Multiple</strong> <strong>Root</strong> <strong>1</strong> is applied to block <strong>A</strong>. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:alt="The Constraint Test Multiple Root 1 is Applied to Block A"><ri:attachment ri:filename="testmultiplicity.png" /></ac:image><br />The Constraint Test Multiple Root 1 is Applied to Block A</h6><p><strong>Scenario 2</strong> <br class="atl-forced-newline" />The given types of causality of y and x are given and target respectively. Once Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit finished executing block <strong>A</strong> and performing a parametric simulation to satisfy the constraint <strong>Test</strong> <strong>Multiple</strong> <strong>Root</strong> <strong>1</strong>, the equation would result in three values of x and all are the possible roots for the equation. Therefore, the <strong>Roots selection</strong> dialog would open, allowing you to select one of these three values as the root (see the following figure). </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:alt="The Roots Selection Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="rootsSelection.png" /></ac:image> <br />The Roots Selection Dialog.</h6><p><strong>Scenario 3</strong> <br class="atl-forced-newline" />However, you may select more than one value as the root. But, the number of values you can select cannot be more than the upper multiplicity. Otherwise, an error will occur and an <strong>Error</strong> message dialog will open. The following figure shows that <em>two</em> values were selected as the roots when in fact, the upper multiplicity of x value property was <em>one</em>. Therefore, an error occurred and the <strong>Error</strong> dialog would open. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:alt="Error Occurs when the Number of Roots Selected is Greater than the Upper Multiplicity" ac:width="947"><ri:attachment ri:filename="multiplicityerror.png" /></ac:image><br />An error occurs when the Number of Roots Selected is Greater than the Upper Multiplicity.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="1f6bbf78-ab80-4208-a64d-5e5d7543c8ab"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specifying the language for <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625154 space=MMA version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00345: (2026x) Excel Helper APIs

- page_id: `255625154`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625154/2026x+Excel+Helper+APIs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:27.443+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Action languages > (2026x) Action scripts APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides APIs to import data from an Excel file into runtime values, and export the runtime values to an Excel file. Either a «DiagramTableMapToDataSource», an Instance Table linked to an Excel file, or an[«ImportMap»](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map)can be used as the*mappingElement*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The provided APIs in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are given in the following pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides APIs to import data from an Excel file into runtime values, and export the runtime values to an Excel file. <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Either a «DiagramTableMapToDataSource», an Instance Table linked to an Excel file, or an </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map">«ImportMap»</a><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> can be used as the<span> </span></span><em style="text-align: left;">mappingElement</em><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:height="178"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Excel Helper APIs.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Excel Helper APIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>The provided APIs in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are given in the following pages</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7ca20eb3-a3ed-4464-a16f-0c04fff1269b" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624668 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00346: (2026x) Executing Activities

- page_id: `255624668`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624668/2026x+Executing+Activities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:52.912+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation
- labels: ['activity-execution', 'executing-activities']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

143637310

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

143637312

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

143637311

You can add some breakpoints to the model created when [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation' space=''] before executing it. This section demonstrates how to suspend the model simulation at some specific points with breakpoints. You can use either the diagram or browser context menu to add a breakpoint to an element.

The following example shows you how to add breakpoints to pins **a** and **b** of the action **print**. Once the model simulation has reached these pins, the simulation will be suspended.

To add a breakpoint to an element and simulate the model

1. Right-click an element and select **Simulation** > **Add****Breakpoint(s)**. The breakpoints will be shown in the **Breakpoints** pane of the Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Breakpoints pane in the Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Clicking Window > Simulation on the main menu to open the Simulation window.

2. Right-click **instance** in the containment browser and select **Simulation** > **Run** to simulate the model from **instance**, which is the InstanceSpecification of the **SumPrinter** Classifier.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. A new simulation session will be created and displayed in the **Sessions** pane of the Simulation window. The symbol of the elements with breakpoints attached will be highlighted in yellow by default. Click the **Start** button on the **Simulation** window toolbar to animate the simulation on the **Print-Sum** Activity diagram.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

4. The simulation will be suspended when pin **a** or **b** of the **print** action is activated. You can hover your mouse pointer over the active element to see its runtime value.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

5. Click the **Resume** button on the **Simulation** window toolbar to continue the simulation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

6. The simulation will be suspended again when pin **b** is activated. Click the **Resume** button to continue the simulation. In the **Console** pane of the **Simulation** window, you can see the printed value of 10, which is the summation between 2 and 8.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If you do not want to display animation (silent simulation), you can create **Simulation****Configuration** to customize the simulation, select **instance** as the **simulationTarget**, and set **silent** to **true**. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space=''] for more information.

143637308

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2978dbf0-a19a-4100-9f4c-1fd625785c46"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637310</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a5339d0d-54f6-4c90-a2c9-66c3c558d6b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637312</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0d81100-9c3e-4890-8d05-f2acadcfa12d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637311</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can add some breakpoints to the model created when <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ac:link> before executing it. This section demonstrates how to suspend the model simulation at some specific points with breakpoints. You can use either the diagram or browser context menu to add a breakpoint to an element. </p><p>The following example shows you how to add breakpoints to pins <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> of the action <strong>print</strong>. Once the model simulation has reached these pins, the simulation will be suspended. </p><p><br /></p><p>To add a breakpoint to an element and simulate the model</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click an element and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Add</strong><strong>Breakpoint(s)</strong>. The breakpoints will be shown in the <strong>Breakpoints</strong> pane of the Simulation window.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Adding Breakpoints to Pin a of print Action" ac:alt="Adding Breakpoints to Pin a of print Action"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddBreakPointAtPinA.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Breakpoints Pane in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="The Breakpoints Pane in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddBreakpointsToAandB.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6>The Breakpoints pane in the Simulation window.</h6><h6><ac:image ac:title="Opening the Simulation Window from the Main Menu" ac:alt="Opening the Simulation Window from the Main Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="WindowSimulationContextMenu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Clicking Window &gt; Simulation on the main menu to open the Simulation window.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">2. Right-click <strong>instance</strong> in the containment browser and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong> to simulate the model from <strong>instance</strong>, which is the InstanceSpecification of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Classifier.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Executing the InstanceSpecification of the SumPrinter Classifier" ac:alt="Executing the InstanceSpecification of the SumPrinter Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInstanceSpecification.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">3. A new simulation session will be created and displayed in the <strong>Sessions</strong> pane of the Simulation window. The symbol of the elements with breakpoints attached will be highlighted in yellow by default. Click the <strong>Start</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to animate the simulation on the <strong>Print-Sum</strong> Activity diagram.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="A Simulation Session in the Sessions Pane of the Simulation Window" ac:alt="A Simulation Session in the Sessions Pane of the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0079.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">4.  The simulation will be suspended when pin <strong>a</strong> or <strong>b</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> action is activated. You can hover your mouse pointer over the active element to see its runtime value.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Is Suspended When Pin a Is Activated" ac:alt="The Simulation Is Suspended When Pin a Is Activated"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SuspendExecutionAtA.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">5. Click the <strong>Resume</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to continue the simulation.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Resume Button in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="The Resume Button in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ResumeToolbarButton.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">6. The simulation will be suspended again when pin <strong>b</strong> is activated. Click the <strong>Resume</strong> button to continue the simulation. In the <strong>Console</strong> pane of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window, you can see the printed value of 10, which is the summation between 2 and 8. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Console Pane of the Simulation Window Showing the Printed Result of the Summation" ac:alt="The Console Pane of the Simulation Window Showing the Printed Result of the Summation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110195_PrintSum.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing Activities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="655cdf81-caa8-4949-af0d-1e20a9ec966e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not want to display animation (silent simulation), you can create <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Configuration</strong> to customize the simulation, select <strong>instance</strong> as the <strong>simulationTarget</strong>, and set <strong>silent</strong> to <strong>true</strong>. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling" /></ac:link> for more information.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="491b3a64-616a-4592-8c9d-fce1054abbc5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637308</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625070 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00347: (2026x) Executing an interaction model

- page_id: `255625070`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625070/2026x+Executing+an+interaction+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:19.498+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Interaction simulation
- labels: ['interaction-model-execution']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section illustrates the steps to simulate the interaction model mentioned in section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='']. 
 
To simulate the interaction model in section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='']

1. Right-click the package **Model** in the containment browser and select **Create Element** > **Package**. Name the package, for example, InstancePackage. The following figure shows the Containment tree containing the objects within its hierarchy. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the **InstancePackage** and select **Create** **Element** > **Instance** **Specification**. Name the instance specification, for example, Sys. The following figure shows the creation of an InstancePackage element from its shortcut menu.. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the instance specification Sys and select Specification to open the specification dialog.
4. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] browse button in the **Classifier** row to open the **Select Elements** dialog (see the first figure below) and select class **System** as the classifier of the instance specification **Sys** (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure shows the elements that can be selected in the **Select Elements** dialog. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat steps 2 and 3 above to create the other instance specifications named **aa1** and **bb1** of the classifiers **A** and **B** respectively. The following figure shows the Containment tree depicting objects within its hierarchy. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Right-click the instance specification Sys and select Simulation > Run (see the first figure below). The Simulation window will appear. [ATTACHMENT filename='InteractionSimulation29.png']
7. Click the **Start** button in the **Simulation** window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Simulation **Console** shows the results of a simulation and the constraints which have passed or failed. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
