The <strong>Simulation</strong> window will appear.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Executing the Instance Specification Sys Simulation through the Context Menu" ac:alt="Executing the Instance Specification Sys Simulation through the Context Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation29.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing an interaction model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Start</strong> button in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Running the Simulation of the Instance Specification Sys in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="Running the Simulation of the Instance Specification Sys in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInteraction_1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing an interaction model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>The Simulation <strong>Console</strong> shows the results of a simulation and the constraints which have passed or failed.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInteraction_2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing an interaction model" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><p> </p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624857 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00348: (2026x) Executing the StopWatch class

- page_id: `255624857`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624857/2026x+Executing+the+StopWatch+class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:08.716+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The StopWatch Class is now ready to be executed. You can execute the StopWatch Class either from the containment browser or from the diagram.

To execute the StopWatch Class

1. Either (i) select the StopWatch class in the containment tree and click the Run button on the Simulation Control main toolbar or (ii) right-click the StopWatch class and select Simulation > Run . [ATTACHMENT filename='shortcutmenu_run.png']
2. The Simulation window will open. It contains four tabs: (i) Sessions , (ii) Console , (iii) Variables , and (iv) Breakpoints tabs. At this point, a simulation session to execute the StopWatch Class will be created. You can see this session in the Sessions tab of the Simulation window. [ATTACHMENT filename='simulation_window.png']
3. Click the **Start** button [ATTACHMENT filename='start_button.png'] on the Simulation window toolbar.

Once you have clicked the **Run** **Execution** button, the Object of the **StopWatch** Class will be created and shown in the **Variables** tab. The Classifier Behavior of the **StopWatch** class and the **StopWatch** State Machine will be executed respectively. A new session to execute the **StopWatch** State Machine will be created under the simulation session of the **StopWatch** class, and the **StopWatch** State Machine diagram will be open and ready for the simulation.

The **StopWatch** State Machine execution will start from the initial stage and automatically move to the **ready** state because the transition which connects between the initial stage and **ready** state does not have a defined trigger. When the **ready** State is entered, the **reset** Activity, which is assigned as the entry to the **ready** State, will be invoked. This Activity also calls the **resetTime** activity of the StopWatch Class for setting the time value to be zero. At this point, a new simulation session will be created to execute the **resetTime** Activity, and the **resetTime** Activity diagram will be open and animated.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 The execution of the **resetTime** Activity will be terminated once it has reached the final stage. When the simulation stops, the session executing the **resetTime** Activity will be closed, the StopWatch State Machine diagram will be reactivated, and the simulation will wait for an Event signal at the ready State. You can browse the **StopWatch** object in the **Variables** tab to see the time value.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 Now we want to trigger the StopWatch object from the ready State to the running State. To do this, we need to send a start signal to the StopWatch object. 
 
To send an event (start) signal to the object of the **StopWatch**Class

1. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab. The signals that can be sent to the StopWatch will be listed in the Trigger drop-down menu.
2. Select the start signal from the Triggers drop-down menu (see the following figure).

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can click the arrow button on the left or right-hand side of the **Simulation** window toolbar to scroll the toolbar to the left or to the right.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Once the start Signal has been sent, the **StopWatch** will go to the **running** State. At the entry to the **running** State, the **increase** Activity will be defined; therefore, it will be invoked. The **increase** Activity will then call the **increaseTime** Activity of the **StopWatch** Class to increment the time value by one second. A new simulation session will be created to execute the **increaseTime** Activity, and the **increaseTime** Activity diagram will be open and animated. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 The **increaseTime** Activity will be completely executed when its Final stage has been reached. Once the execution is complete, the simulation session of the **increaseTime** Activity will be closed and the **StopWatch** State Machine will be activated again. Now you can see in the **Variables** tab that the time value of the StopWatch object has been incremented by one.

The StopWatch object will still be at the **running** State and the time Event will be triggered at this State every second. Therefore, a new simulation session will be created to execute the **increaseTime** activity every second, and the time value will increment by one every time the increaseTime Activity is executed.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 While the StopWatch object is at the **running** State, we can send a **split** signal to trigger the object to the **paused** State or send a **stop** signal to trigger the object to the **stopped** State. 
 
 To send a split signal to the StopWatch object

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the split signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the paused State.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 You can change the paused StopWatch object to the **running** State by sending an **unsplit** signal to the object. 
 
To change the StopWatch object from a paused state to a running State

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the unsplit signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the running State, and the time value will continuously increase. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6240bec3246339c7c6e2a46d39e55614.png']

To send a stop Signal to the StopWatch object

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the stop signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The State of the StopWatch object will be changed to the stopped State. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavaf4fd7ef425f080b2957e3bda2118678.png']

At the **stopped** State, if the **reset** signal is sent to the StopWatch object, the object will go to the **ready** State, and the time value will be reset to zero at the entry to the **ready** state by calling the **resetTime** Activity. If the **stop** signal is sent to the object again, the object will go to the final stage, and then the simulation will stop and all of the simulation sessions will be closed.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The StopWatch Class is now ready to be executed. You can execute the StopWatch Class either from the containment browser or from the diagram. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To execute the StopWatch Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Either (i) select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class in the containment tree and click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Control</strong> main toolbar or (ii) right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Shortcut Menu Run" ac:alt="Shortcut Menu Run"><ri:attachment ri:filename="shortcutmenu_run.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window will open. It contains four tabs: (i) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sessions</strong>, (ii) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Console</strong>, (iii) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong>, and (iv) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Breakpoints</strong> tabs. At this point, a simulation session to execute the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class will be created. You can see this session in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sessions</strong> tab of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Simulation Console" ac:alt="Simulation Console"><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation_window.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <span style="line-height: 20.0px;"><strong>Start</strong></span> button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="start_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once you have clicked the <strong>Run</strong> <strong>Execution</strong> button, the Object of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class will be created and shown in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab. The Classifier Behavior of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be executed respectively. A new session to execute the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be created under the simulation session of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class, and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine diagram will be open and ready for the simulation.</p><p>The <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine execution will start from the initial stage and automatically move to the <strong>ready</strong> state because the transition which connects between the initial stage and <strong>ready</strong> state does not have a defined trigger. When the <strong>ready</strong> State is entered, the <strong>reset</strong> Activity, which is assigned as the entry to the <strong>ready</strong> State, will be invoked. This Activity also calls the <strong>resetTime</strong> activity of the StopWatch Class for setting the time value to be zero. At this point, a new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity, and the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram will be open and animated.</p><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Executing the ResetTime Activity" ac:alt="Executing the ResetTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0d2d0cab3d8ad736e9f36d62ab703bb2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The execution of the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity will be terminated once it has reached the final stage. When the simulation stops, the session executing the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity will be closed, the StopWatch State Machine diagram will be reactivated, and the simulation will wait for an Event signal at the ready State. You can browse the <strong>StopWatch</strong> object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab to see the time value.</p><p><br /><ac:image ac:title="(Upper) The Reset Activity Invoked at the Entry of the Ready State and (Lower) Browsing the StopWatch Object in the Variables Tab for the Time Value" ac:alt="(Upper) The Reset Activity Invoked at the Entry of the Ready State and (Lower) Browsing the StopWatch Object in the Variables Tab for the Time Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav710b7b9c304fbabadfdd01d17a76b53d.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Now we want to trigger the StopWatch object from the ready State to the running State. To do this, we need to send a start signal to the StopWatch object. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To send an event (start) signal to the object of the <strong>StopWatch </strong>Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab. The signals that can be sent to the <strong>StopWatch</strong> will be listed in the <strong>Trigger</strong> drop-down menu.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">start</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu (see the following figure). </li></ol><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="356a0572-f189-4c33-9671-47aa551d6212"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can click the arrow button on the left or right-hand side of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to scroll the toolbar to the left or to the right.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Start Signal from the Triggers Drop-down Menu" ac:alt="Selecting the Start Signal from the Triggers Drop-down Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav26b7e0c79977913f0326c7502218e610.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once the start Signal has been sent, the <strong>StopWatch</strong> will go to the <strong>running</strong> State. At the entry to the <strong>running</strong> State, the <strong>increase</strong> Activity will be defined; therefore, it will be invoked. The <strong>increase</strong> Activity will then call the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class to increment the time value by one second. A new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity, and the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram will be open and animated.<br /> <ac:image ac:title="The Execution of the IncreaseTime Activity (Upper) and the Time Value of the StopWatch Object Incrementing by One (Lower) " ac:alt="The Execution of the IncreaseTime Activity (Upper) and the Time Value of the StopWatch Object Incrementing by One (Lower) "><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav01fbe03f0d8da98602e00cbb751b5fb1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity will be completely executed when its Final stage has been reached. Once the execution is complete, the simulation session of the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity will be closed and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be activated again. Now you can see in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab that the time value of the StopWatch object has been incremented by one.</p><p>The StopWatch object will still be at the <strong>running</strong> State and the time Event will be triggered at this State every second. Therefore, a new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>increaseTime</strong> activity every second, and the time value will increment by one every time the increaseTime Activity is executed.</p><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Activating the Transition to Self Every Second" ac:alt="Activating the Transition to Self Every Second"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4170bb121ff21494de21372aff8918de.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />  <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> While the StopWatch object is at the <strong>running</strong> State, we can send a <strong>split</strong> signal to trigger the object to the <strong>paused</strong> State or send a <strong>stop</strong> signal to trigger the object to the <strong>stopped</strong> State. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To send a split signal to the StopWatch object</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">split</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paused</strong> State. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Paused State" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Paused State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaf5f9748fcb0d4a3e1491cfa121f2468.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /> You can change the paused StopWatch object to the <strong>running</strong> State by sending an <strong>unsplit</strong> signal to the object. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To change the StopWatch object from a paused state to a running State</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">unsplit</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">running</strong> State, and the time value will continuously increase. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object from the Paused State to the Running State with Unsplit Signal" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object from the Paused State to the Running State with Unsplit Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav6240bec3246339c7c6e2a46d39e55614.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To send a stop Signal to the StopWatch object</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The State of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> State.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Stopped State by Sending the Stop Signal" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Stopped State by Sending the Stop Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaf4fd7ef425f080b2957e3bda2118678.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> At the <strong>stopped</strong> State, if the <strong>reset</strong> signal is sent to the StopWatch object, the object will go to the <strong>ready</strong> State, and the time value will be reset to zero at the entry to the <strong>ready</strong> state by calling the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. If the <strong>stop</strong> signal is sent to the object again, the object will go to the final stage, and then the simulation will stop and all of the simulation sessions will be closed. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624846 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00349: (2026x) Executing the StopWatch instance specification

- page_id: `255624846`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624846/2026x+Executing+the+StopWatch+instance+specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:08.075+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the previous section, we have shown you how to execute the StopWatch model from the StopWatch Class. You can also use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to execute the model from an InstanceSpecification. With this feature, you can specify values to the slots of an InstanceSpecification. These values will be used as the input values for the execution such as using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction to read the values.

In this tutorial, our StopWatch contains only one value property, which is time. The time value will always reset to zero whenever the system enters the ready State. If we create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch and use it for model execution, the result will be the same as the model execution of the StopWatch Class.

To create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch

1. Open a System Class diagram.
2. Click Instance on the Class Diagram toolbar and click the System Class diagram. A new InstanceSpecification will be created and the Select Classifier dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-classifier-a.png']
3. Select the StopWatch Class and click OK .
4. On the Class diagram, name the created InstanceSpecification "stopwatch" . Now we will need to create values and assign them to the slots.

To create values and assign them to the slots

1. Right-click the symbol of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification and select Specification to open the Specification window of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification.
2. Select the Slots node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
3. Select the Structural Feature of the slot to which the values will be applied. In this case, select time:Integer[1] . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav9a7cf25ce112886b657a3d1ed123cab8.png']
4. Click the Create Value button. A new integer value will be created for the slot. If an Integer value is selected, a zero will be created by default. You can modify this value by typing a new value into the Value box. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav26add523b109fefb885b3d0398fc018f.png']
5. Click Close to close the Specification window.

To start executing the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, right-click it either on the **System** Class diagram or in the containment browser and click **Execute**. A simulation session will be created in the **Sessions** tab of the Simulation window to execute this instance. The steps to run the execution are the same as those to execute the **StopWatch** Class.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In the previous section, we have shown you how to execute the StopWatch model from the StopWatch Class. You can also use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to execute the model from an InstanceSpecification. With this feature, you can specify values to the slots of an InstanceSpecification. These values will be used as the input values for the execution such as using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction to read the values.</p><p>In this tutorial, our StopWatch contains only one value property, which is time. The time value will always reset to zero whenever the system enters the ready State. If we create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch and use it for model execution, the result will be the same as the model execution of the StopWatch Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a <strong>System</strong> Class diagram.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Instance</strong> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class Diagram</strong> toolbar and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong> Class diagram. A new InstanceSpecification will be created and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Classifier</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Select Classifier Dialog" ac:alt="The Select Classifier Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-classifier-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>On the Class diagram, name the created InstanceSpecification <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;stopwatch&quot;</em>. Now we will need to create values and assign them to the slots.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To create values and assign them to the slots</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the symbol of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open the Specification window of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopwatch</strong> InstanceSpecification.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Slots</strong> node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li>Select the Structural Feature of the slot to which the values will be applied. In this case, select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time:Integer[1]</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Dialog of the StopWatch InstanceSpecification" ac:alt="The Specification Dialog of the StopWatch InstanceSpecification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav9a7cf25ce112886b657a3d1ed123cab8.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> button. A new integer value will be created for the slot. If an Integer value is selected, a zero will be created by default. You can modify this value by typing a new value into the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> box.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Setting a Slot Value in the Specification Dialog" ac:alt="Setting a Slot Value in the Specification Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav26add523b109fefb885b3d0398fc018f.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> to close the Specification window. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To start executing the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, right-click it either on the <strong>System</strong> Class diagram or in the containment browser and click <strong>Execute</strong>. A simulation session will be created in the <strong>Sessions</strong> tab of the Simulation window to execute this instance. The steps to run the execution are the same as those to execute the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Window during the Execution of StopWatch Model from InstanceSpecification" ac:alt="The Simulation Window during the Execution of StopWatch Model from InstanceSpecification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav1ed8cb9f4b7f147c9cceb298df636342.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch instance specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><p> </p>
````

1119953376

This section shows you how to execute the StopWatch model with Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. The StopWatch Class will be selected to execute the model.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624943 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00351: (2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration

- page_id: `255624943`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624943/2026x+Executing+the+StopWatch+using+Simulation+Configuration
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:12.466+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) Tutorial > (2026x) Stopwatch model sample > (2026x) Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize model execution by using an Simulation Configuration. The Simulation Configuration is a stereotype that can be applied to a Class Element. This stereotype contains tag definitions for model execution customization such as enable or disable animation and UI mockups. To be able to use the Simulation Configuration, we need to add the *SimulationProfile.mdzip* file to our project. 
 
 To add the *SimulationProfile.mdzip* file to a project

1. Click File > Use Project on the main menu. The Use Project wizard dialog opens.
2. Select the From predefined location option.
3. Select <install.root>\profiles in Project modules paths , and then select SimulationProfile.mdzip . [ATTACHMENT filename='Use Project Dialog.png']
4. Click Next to go to the second step, Module Settings . [ATTACHMENT filename='use-project-dialog-2.png']
5. Use the default settings and click Finish . The simulation profile will be added to the project. You can see it in the containment browser. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavd4057523d3d2f3e88e8ca3eb06273df0.png']

An Simulation Configuration is a Class Element, which is applied with the «SimulationConfig» stereotype (see the following figure). This stereotype contains tag definitions to customize model execution (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space='']).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Next, we will create the Simulation Configuration to execute our StopWatch model without using animation. 
 
 To execute our StopWatch model without using animation

1. Open a System Class diagram.
2. Create a new Class Element by clicking the Class button on the Class diagram toolbar.
3. Click the diagram to create the Class Element.
4. Name the created Class Element "SilentStopwatch" .
5. Right-click the SilentStopwatch Class and select Stereotype to apply the «SimulationConfig» stereotype to the created Class. The Stereotype context menu group will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype.png']
6. Select SimulationConfig from the list of available stereotypes for the Class Element.
7. Click Apply . The SilentStopwatch Class will be with the applied SimulationConfig stereotype. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype Completed.png']

Next, we will create a SimulationLog to record the runtime events occurrence and assign this SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig.

To record all runtime events occurrence and assign a SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig

1. Create a new Class Element by clicking the Class button on the Class diagram toolbar.
2. Click the diagram to create the Class Element.
3. Name the created Class "SimulationLogs" .
4. Right-click the SimulationLogs Class and select Stereotype to apply the «SimulationLog» stereotype to the created Class. The Stereotype context menu will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying SimulationLog Stereotype.png']
5. Select SimulationLog and click Apply . The SimulationLogs Class will be with the applied «SimulationLog» stereotype. [ATTACHMENT filename='SimulationLog Completed.png']

Now we need to specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype that is applied to the SilentStopwatch Class. 
 
To specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype

1. Open the Specification dialog of the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration by right-clicking the SilentStopwatch symbol and select Specification .
2. Select the Tags node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
3. Select SimulationProfile from the Profile drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specification Dialog Selecting SimulationProfile.png']
4. Specify the tagged value of the execution target to the StopWatch Class by selecting executionTarget and click Create Value . The Select Elements dialog will open for you to select the Element that will be the execution target. [ATTACHMENT filename='Select Elements Dialog for executionTarget.png']
5. Select the StopWatch Class (you can also select the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, which has been created in the previous section) as the executionTarget .
6. Click OK to close the Select Elements dialog.
7. Follow the same steps used to set the tagged value of executionTarget to the StopWatch Class and specify the tagged values of the following tag definitions i. log to SimulationLogs . ii. resultLocation to the Stopwatch folder. iii. silent to true .
8. Click Close to close the Specification dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Complete SimulationConfig.png']

To execute the Simulation Configuration

1. Right-click the SilentStopwatch symbol on the diagram and select Simulation > Execute .
2. Click the Run Simulation button on the Simulation window toolbar to start the simulation. The StopWatch Class will then be executed without animation. You will need to wait until the runtime object of StopWatch is at the ready State. You can see the State of the runtime object at the Variables tab. The State of the object will be displayed in square brackets.
3. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab.
4. Select the start signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar.

Now, you can see that the value of **time:Integer**, which is displayed in the **Variables** tab, will increase by one every second. 
 
To stop the execution

1. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab.
2. Select the stop signal from the Triggers drop-down menu. The StopWatch object will go to the stopped State.
3. Select the stop signal again to move the object to the final stage to close the simulation session. The latest value of time:Integer will be saved into the slot value of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize model execution by using an Simulation Configuration. The Simulation Configuration is a stereotype that can be applied to a Class Element. This stereotype contains tag definitions for model execution customization such as enable or disable animation and UI mockups. To be able to use the Simulation Configuration, we need to add the <em>SimulationProfile.mdzip</em> file to our project. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To add the <em>SimulationProfile.mdzip</em> file to a project</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Use</strong> <strong>Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>Use</strong> <strong>Project</strong> wizard dialog opens.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">From predefined </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">location</strong> option.</li><li>Select <em>&lt;install.root&gt;\profiles</em> in <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Project </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">modules </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paths</strong>, and then select <em>SimulationProfile.mdzip</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Use Project Dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Next</strong> to go to the second step, <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Module </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Settings</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Module Settings Pane in the Use Module Dialog" ac:alt="The Module Settings Pane in the Use Module Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="use-project-dialog-2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Use the default settings and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Finish</strong>. The simulation profile will be added to the project. You can see it in the containment browser. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Profile in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="The Simulation Profile in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavd4057523d3d2f3e88e8ca3eb06273df0.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> An Simulation Configuration is a Class Element, which is applied with the «SimulationConfig» stereotype (see the following figure). This stereotype contains tag definitions to customize model execution (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype" /></ac:link>).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="SimulationConfig.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>Next, we will create the Simulation Configuration to execute our StopWatch model without using animation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To execute our StopWatch model without using animation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a <strong>System</strong> Class diagram.</li><li>Create a new Class Element by clicking the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class</strong> button on the Class diagram toolbar.</li><li>Click the diagram to create the Class Element.</li><li>Name the created Class Element <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;SilentStopwatch&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SilentStopwatch</strong> Class and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> to apply the «SimulationConfig» stereotype to the created Class. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> context menu group will open.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationConfig</strong> from the list of available stereotypes for the Class Element.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Apply</strong>. The SilentStopwatch Class will be with the applied SimulationConfig stereotype.<br /><br />  <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype Completed.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Next, we will create a SimulationLog to record the runtime events occurrence and assign this SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To record all runtime events occurrence and assign a SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a new Class Element by clicking the <strong>Class</strong> button on the Class diagram toolbar.</li><li>Click the diagram to create the Class Element.</li><li>Name the created Class <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;SimulationLogs&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationLogs</strong> Class and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> to apply the «SimulationLog» stereotype to the created Class. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> context menu will open.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Applying SimulationLog Stereotype.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationLog</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Apply</strong>. The <strong>SimulationLogs</strong> Class will be with the applied «SimulationLog» stereotype.<br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="SimulationLog Completed.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p>Now we need to specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype that is applied to the SilentStopwatch Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <strong>Specification</strong> dialog of the <strong>SilentStopwatch</strong> Simulation Configuration by right-clicking the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SilentStopwatch</strong> symbol and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong>.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Tags</strong> node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationProfile</strong> from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Profile</strong> drop-down menu. <br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification Dialog Selecting SimulationProfile.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Specify the tagged value of the execution target to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class by selecting <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">executionTarget</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong>. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Elements</strong> dialog will open for you to select the Element that will be the execution target.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Elements Dialog for executionTarget.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class (you can also select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopwatch</strong> InstanceSpecification, which has been created in the previous section) as the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">executionTarget</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong> to close the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select Elements</strong> dialog.</li><li>Follow the same steps used to set the tagged value of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">executionTarget</strong> to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class and specify the tagged values of the following tag definitions <br />i. <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">log</strong> to <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationLogs</strong>. <br />ii. <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">resultLocation</strong> to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stopwatch</strong> folder.<br />iii. <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">silent</strong> to <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">true</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> to close the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> dialog.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Complete SimulationConfig.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To execute the Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>SilentStopwatch</strong> symbol on the diagram and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Execute</strong>.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Run</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> button on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar to start the simulation. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class will then be executed without animation. You will need to wait until the runtime object of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> is at the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ready</strong> State. You can see the State of the runtime object at the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong> tab. The State of the object will be displayed in square brackets.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">start</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Now, you can see that the value of <strong>time:Integer</strong>, which is displayed in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab, will increase by one every second. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To stop the execution</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> object will go to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> State.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal again to move the object to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">final</strong> stage to close the simulation session. The latest value of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time:Integer</strong> will be saved into the slot value of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopwatch</strong> InstanceSpecification.</li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624680 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00352: (2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models

- page_id: `255624680`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624680/2026x+Execution+of+incomplete+or+dummy+models
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:53.767+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Activity simulation
- labels: ['dummy-models', 'incomplete-model-execution']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

376786264

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

376786266

376786262

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

376786265

Previous versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit did not support execution of incomplete or dummy models. From the 18.4 version and above, it will be possible to execute incomplete or dummy models.

Previously, the execution would halt. Now, if any input pin does not have an accompanying value, the Behavior of the Action will not be to halt execution, instead a null value token will be passed to that pin, or when simulating a **CallOperationAction** which does not have a target pin, a runtime object, which is the context of the Activity will be selected as the target. Null Value tokens do not trigger flow animation on triggered behaviors if the input pin multiplicity's lower bound is 0.

The information on this page describes the Behavior of an Action when any of the input or output pins, or any properties of the Action, are not specified or not present.

#### INFO: Supported Actions

Supported Actions

The Actions which are currently supported are as follows

- AddStructuralFeatureValueAction
- ReadStructuralFeatureValueAction
- CallBehaviorAction
- CallOperationAction
- SendSignalAction
- ValueSpecificationAction
- StartClassifierBehaviorAction and StateObjectBehaviorAction
- OpaqueAction

#### AddStructuralFeatureValueAction

An add structural feature value Action is a write structural feature Action for adding values to a structural feature. When any input pin is unspecified or empty, or when a **Structural Feature** is unspecified or does not exist, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- I f Object input pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.
- If Value input pin is not specified, this Action will be skipped.
- If Structural Feature is not specified or it does not exist in its own context, then this Action will be skipped.

If an **Object** input pin is empty or unspecified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used to add the value to the **Structural Feature** of the context.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation if an object pin is unspecified.

#### ReadStructuralFeatureValueAction

A read structural feature Action is a structural feature Action that retrieves the values of a structural feature. When any of the input or output pins are unspecified or not present, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- If the Object pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.
- If Structural Feature is not specified or if the Structural Feature has no values, then a n ull value token will be sent.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation if an object pin is not specified.

#### CallBehaviorAction

Call Behavior Action is a call Action that invokes a Behavior directly rather than invoking an operation that invokes the Behavior. When any of the **Argument** pins are either unspecified or not present, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- If any of the Argument input pins are unspecified, this Action will send a null value token for that parameter.
- If none of the Argument pins are unspecified, this Action will send null value tokens for all the Arguments .
- If the Result output pin is specified and this Action does not return any results, it will return a null value token instead.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation if an argument pin is unspecified (A null value token is passed to the pin.).

#### CallOperationAction

A call operation Action is an Action that transmits an operation call request to the target object, where it may cause the invocation of associated Behavior. When any input or output pin is unspecified or not present, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- This Action will send a null value token for each unspecified Argument input pin.
- In case no Argument pin is specified, this Action will send null value tokens for all the parameters.
- If the **Result** output pin is specified and this Action does not return any results, it will return a **null** value token instead.
- If the **Target** input pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] A demo showing the situation if an argument input pin is unspecified (A null value token will be passed.).

#### SendSignalAction

Send signal Action is an invocation Action that creates a signal from its inputs, and transmits it to the specified target object, where it may cause the firing of a State Machine transition or the execution of an Activity. When either **Argument** pin or **Target** input pin is unspecified or empty, this Action's Behavior will have the rules as follows

- If there are any incomplete Argument pins, this Action will send null value tokens for those parameters.
- If Argument pin is not present, this Action will send null value tokens for all parameters.
- If Target input pin is not present, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation when there are incomplete argument pins.

#### ValueSpecificationAction

A value specification Action is an Action that evaluates a value specification. When the value of a **ValueSpecification** Action is not specified, this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows

- If the value of a ValueSpecification Action is not specified, a **null** value token will be passed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation when the value of a ValueSpecification Action is unspecified (A null value token is passed.).

#### StartClassifierBehaviorAction and StateObjectBehaviorAction

A start Classifier Behavior Action is an Action that starts the Classifier Behavior of the input. If the **Object** input pin is not present or empty, this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows

- If the Object input pin is not present or empty, the StartClassifierBehaviorAction Action will use the runtime context of the Activity execution as the target.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation when the object pin is either not present or empty.

#### OpaqueAction

Opaque Actions are a type of Action that can be used to represent implementation information. They can also be used as placeholder Actions until you determine the specific type of Action to use. If the opaque Action does not return a value to the **OutputValue** pin, this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows

- If the opaque Action does not return any value to the OutputValue pin, a null value token will be passed to the pin at runtime.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### If an opaque Action does not return any value to the OutputValue pin, a null token will be passed to the pin.

#### INFO: Information

Information

A **"null value token"** is an **ObjectToken** with has no values defined.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e8fe2e6-3547-4435-8ac7-06a67d51bef6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786264</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6da8c9f3-723f-4700-b00a-441d2a9480ac"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786266</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ea691c5f-b691-4c2d-811b-6ddf7a53906c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786262</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7ceba2bf-9727-4011-bab3-bf9986f9d9c7"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">2</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e7d3e92-fa06-4441-b7e0-7a4fb2fc3682"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786265</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Previous versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit did not support execution of incomplete or dummy models. From the 18.4 version and above, it will be possible to execute incomplete or dummy models.</p><p>Previously, the execution would halt. When any of the input or output pins are unspecified or not present, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">will have the rules as follows</span></span></span></span></p><ul><li>If the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Object</strong> pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.</li><li>If <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Structural Feature</strong> is not specified or if the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Structural Feature</strong> has no values, then a <strong>n</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ull</strong> value token will be sent.<br /><br /></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="2.ReadStructuralFeatureValueAction.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A demo showing the situation if an object pin is not specified.</h6><h2>CallBehaviorAction</h2><p>Call Behavior Action is a call Action that invokes a Behavior directly rather than invoking an operation that invokes the Behavior. When any of the <strong>Argument</strong> pins are either unspecified or not present, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">will have the rules as follows</span></span></span></p><ul><li>If any of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Argument</strong> input pins are unspecified, this Action will send a <strong>null</strong> value token for that parameter. </li><li>If none of the <strong>Argument</strong> pins are unspecified, this Action will send <strong>null</strong> value tokens for all the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Arguments</strong>. </li><li>If the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Result</strong> output pin is specified and this Action does not return any results, it will return a <strong>null</strong> value token instead.<br /><br /></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3.CallBehaviorAction.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A demo showing the situation if an argument pin is unspecified (A null value token is passed to the pin.).</h6><h2>CallOperationAction </h2><p>A call operation Action is an Action that transmits an operation call request to the target object, where it may cause the invocation of associated Behavior. When any input or output pin is unspecified or not present, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">will have the rules as follows</span></span></span></p><ul><li>This Action will send a null value token for each unspecified <strong>Argument</strong> input pin. </li><li>In case no <strong>Argument</strong> pin is specified, this Action will send <strong>null</strong> value tokens for all the parameters. </li><li><p>If the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Result</strong> output pin is specified and this Action does not return any results, it will return a <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">null</strong> value token instead.</p></li><li><p>If the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Target</strong> input pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution  will be used as the target.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="4.CallOperationAction.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A demo showing the situation if an argument input pin is unspecified (A null value token will be passed.).</h6></li></ul><h2>SendSignalAction<span style="line-height: 1.42857;"> </span></h2><p>Send signal Action is an invocation Action that creates a signal from its inputs, and transmits it to the specified target object, where it may cause the firing of a State Machine transition or the execution of an Activity. When the value of a <strong>ValueSpecification</strong> Action is not specified, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows</span></span><br /></span></p><ul><li><span style="line-height: 1.42857;">If the value of a </span><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ValueSpecification</strong><span style="line-height: 1.42857;"> Action is not specified, a <strong>null</strong> value token will be passed.<br /></span></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6.ValueSpecificationAction.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A demo showing the situation when the value of a ValueSpecification Action is unspecified (A null value token is passed.).</h6><h2>StartClassifierBehaviorAction and StateObjectBehaviorAction </h2><p>A start Classifier Behavior Action is an Action that starts the Classifier Behavior of the input. They can also be used as placeholder Actions until you determine the specific type of Action to use. If the opaque Action does not return a value to the <strong>OutputValue</strong> pin, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows</span></span><br /></span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> If the opaque Action does not return any value to the </span><strong>OutputValue</strong> pin, a <strong>null</strong> value token will be passed to the pin at runtime.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="8.OpaqueAction.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">If an opaque Action does not return any value to the OutputValue pin, a null token will be passed to the pin.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="307ac12b-77b0-4277-87ed-2492ff681cf7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A <strong>&quot;null value token&quot;</strong> is an <strong>ObjectToken</strong> with has no values defined.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625253 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00353: (2026x) Exporting plots data

- page_id: `255625253`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625253/2026x+Exporting+plots+data
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:38.663+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Time Series Chart
- labels: ['plot-data', 'exporting-plot-data']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1415232085

1415232088

1415232086

A Time series chart provides two options for you to export your plot data in which time values (the values that appear on the horizontal axis) and the corresponding recorded values (the values that appear on the vertical axis) are plotted in a Time series chart. You can export the plot values to either a comma-separated value file (CSV file) or the slot values of an instance model.

1415232084

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7757e2c1-82c4-4192-8071-52c0a7bba4c0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232085</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9e35daef-221d-491b-9354-392e9172974b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232088</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7dba503c-636f-4036-9b3d-3b93e9ba67f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232086</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A Time series chart provides two options for you to export your plot data in which time values (the values that appear on the horizontal axis) and the corresponding recorded values (the values that appear on the vertical axis) are plotted in a Time series chart. You can export the plot values to either a comma-separated value file (CSV file) or the slot values of an instance model.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3f58372-71f4-439c-b558-4028a99cdab5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232084</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="c68ec771-4d65-4891-a7e5-50e55a78cc79" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625270 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00354: (2026x) Exporting plots data to a CSV file

- page_id: `255625270`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625270/2026x+Exporting+plots+data+to+a+CSV+file
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:40.871+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Time Series Chart > (2026x) Exporting plots data
- labels: ['plot-data-to-csv-file', 'exporting-plot-data-to-csv']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1415448634

1415448636

1415448635

The values plotted in a Time series chart are exportable to a CSV file.

To export plots data of a Time series chart to a CSV file

1. Click the **Export** **Data** toolbar button on the Time series chart's plot panel. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Enter a filename and select a location to save the file.
3. Click the Save button. The following figure shows plotted values in the Time series chart

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Plotted values in the Time series chart.

The following figure shows the exported plot values in a CSV file

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Exported plot values in a CSV file.

If you exported the plotted values in a Time series chart to a CSV file, the exported plots data in the CSV file would look like those shown in the first figure. The axes labels would appear in the first paragraph of the file as the column header.

1415448633

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting plots data' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625260 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00355: (2026x) Exporting plots data to an instance model

- page_id: `255625260`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625260/2026x+Exporting+plots+data+to+an+instance+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:39.389+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > (2026x) Time Series Chart > (2026x) Exporting plots data
- labels: ['exporting-plot-data-to-instance-model', 'plot-data-to-instance-model', 'auto-save-plot-values']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1415823846

1415823848

1415823847

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit also allows you to export plots data in a Time series chart to an Instance model. The model for exported plots data in the simulation profile comprises of DataSet and Data Classes. Whenever you export plots data to an instance model, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create an InstanceSpecification of a DataSet Class. The slot of a data property will contain a list of InstanceSpecifications of the Data Class. The first instance on the list is an InstanceSpecification containing the time values while the second one is an InstanceSpecification containing the values that appear on the vertical axis of the first plot. If a Time series chart has more than one plot, the third InstanceSpecification on the list will be an InstanceSpecification containing the values of the second plot and so on.

To export plots data to an instance model

1. Click the **Export** **to** **Instance** toolbar button of the Time series chart's panel whose values you want to export. The **Element Selection** dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select a package that will own the instance model you want to create.
3. Click the OK button. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create the instance model containing the plots data in a Time Series Chart as shown in the following figure

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The instance model containing the plots data in a Time series chart.

To auto-save plot values into model on every simulation run

1. Do one of the following

- Double-click the Time series chart.
- Right-click the Time series chart and select Specification .

The Specification window will open as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button beside the **Plot Result Location** property to edit its value. The**Plot Result Location** property value takes the name of the**Instance** to save the values, as in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Start the simulation. When the simulation terminates, the plot values will be written to the **Instance specification** selected in the previous step, as in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

1415823845

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting plots data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625574 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00356: (2026x) Exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications

- page_id: `255625574`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625574/2026x+Exporting+runtime+objects+to+InstanceSpecifications
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:49.934+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Simulation debugging > (2026x) Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['exporting-runtime-values', 'exporting-runtime-objects']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

45332699

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

45332701

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

45332700

You can export the values of a runtime object through the **Variables** pane to the following Specifications

- a new InstanceSpecification.
- an existing InstanceSpecification.
- the InstanceSpecification that created the runtime object. Once exported, the values of a runtime object will be set to the slots of the InstanceSpecification.

The type of InstanceSpecification you want to export the values of a runtime object to, depends on the element you used to create the runtime object. If the element is a Classifier, you can export the values of the runtime object to either of the following

- A new InstanceSpecification.
- An existing InstanceSpecification.

If the element is an InstanceSpecification, you can export the values of the runtime object to either of the following

- A new InstanceSpecification.
- The same InstanceSpecification that you used to create that runtime object.

To export a runtime value to a new InstanceSpecification

1. Do either of the following

- Click a runtime object whose value you want to export in the Name column and click the Export to New Instance icon on the Variables pane toolbar.
- Right-click the row and select Export Value To > New Instance

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. The **Select Owner** dialog will open. Select the owner of the new InstanceSpecification (the system folder).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click **OK**.

To export a runtime value to an existing InstanceSpecification

1. Do either of the following

- Click a runtime object whose value you want to export in the Name column and click the Export to Instance icon on the Variables pane toolbar.
- Right-click the row and select Export Value To > Instance .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

To export the value of a runtime object using this method, the runtime object must be created from a Classifier.

2. The **Select Instance** dialog will open. Select an InstanceSpecification that will be used to save the runtime object (You can select only the InstanceSpecification whose classifier is the same as that of the runtime object). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click **OK**.

To export a runtime value to the InstanceSpecification that has created the runtime object

1. Do either of the following

- Click a runtime object whose value you want to export in the Name column and click the Export to Instance icon on the Variables pane toolbar.
- Right-click the row and select Export Value To > Instance .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Once Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit exported and saved the values of the runtime object to the Instance Specification that created it, you can see the hyperlink to that particular Instance Specification in the **Simulation** **Console** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click the hyperlink, and you will see the exact Instance Specification in the Containment tree.

This method allows you to export the value of the runtime object to the slots of the Instance Specification that has created the object. You can see the notification of each successful export and a location hyperlink, if any, in the **Simulation** **Console** pane.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

To export the value of a runtime object using this method, the runtime object must be created from an Instance Specification.

45332698

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Instance Specification' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Variables pane' space='']
