<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625662 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00389: (2026x) Key features

- page_id: `255625662`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625662/2026x+Key+features
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:54.578+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Getting started
- labels: ['key-features', 'main-features', 'features']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of running your UML or SysML models. The key features of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are as follows

- Simulation Framework The general infrastructure (including the simulation toolbars, context menu, and panes) and Open API for simulation.
- Activity Engine (fUML Engine) The OMG fUML (a foundational subset of the Executable UML) standard.
- State-Machine Engine (SCXML Engine) The W3C SCXML (State Charts XML) standard, which is an open-source Apache implementation.
- Interaction Engine Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate interactions that are modeled with Sequence diagrams.
- Parametric Engine Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate SysML Parametric diagrams.

The simulation sample projects are available in the *<**install_root**>/samples/simulation* directory.

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity simulation engine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) State Machine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Interaction' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space='MT']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of running your UML or SysML models. The key features of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are as follows</p><ul><li><strong>Simulation</strong> <strong>Framework</strong> <br />The general infrastructure (including the simulation toolbars, context menu, and panes) and Open API for simulation.</li><li><strong>Activity Engine</strong> <strong>(fUML Engine)</strong> <br />The OMG fUML (a foundational subset of the Executable UML) standard.</li><li><strong>State-Machine Engine</strong> <strong>(SCXML Engine)</strong> <br />The W3C SCXML (State Charts XML) standard, which is an open-source Apache implementation.</li><li><strong>Interaction</strong> <strong>Engine</strong> <br />Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate interactions that are modeled with Sequence diagrams.</li><li><strong>Parametric Engine</strong> <br />Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate SysML Parametric diagrams. </li></ul><p>The simulation sample projects are available in the <em>&lt;<em><em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em></em>&gt;/samples/simulation</em> directory. </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Activity simulation engine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) State Machine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Interaction" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="SysML Parametric Diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626080 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00390: (2026x) Lifeline

- page_id: `255626080`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626080/2026x+Lifeline
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:30.641+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Interaction simulation > (2026x) Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['lifeline']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

1010481082

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

1010481084

1010481081

**On this page**

**33**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

1010481083

You can use a lifeline in a Sequence diagram to represent a property owned by a Class or a block that is the context of the diagram. Sending messages between objects specifying the properties presented by the lifelines can occur. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses lifelines to find a source and a target object of the message.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing how lifelines can be used to find the source and target of a message.

You can use a lifeline in a SysML project to represent a nested property of a Class or a block, which is the context of a diagram. A path to the nested property appears as a dot notation on the lifeline symbol. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the path on the lifeline symbol to find the object.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses a lifeline with a dot notation to represent a nested property when you record a simulation as a Sequence diagram. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Recording simulation as a Sequence diagram' space=''] for more information about recording a simulation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing how a lifeline with a dot notation can represent a nested property.

Because Messages recorded in the system are not executed that they are internal mechanisms, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit only executes Messages that are external to the system, e.g., a Message going from *User* Lifeline (without types) and *Found* Messages. All other Messages are not executed but verified and printed in the **Console** pane.

#### INFO: Information

Information

A *Found* Message is a Message where the occurrence of a receiving Event is known, but the occurrence of a sending Event is not.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Executing only external outgoing Messages, e.g., User Lifeline and Found Messages.

##### Storing nested property paths in a model

From version 2021x Refresh2, the Lifelines used to represent nested properties have the «NestedPropertyPath» stereotype applied to them. As a result, the path of a nested property is stored in the **propertyPath** tag of a Lifeline. Nested property paths of the Lifelines created with an earlier version of a modeling tool are stored in diagram symbols and you should save them in the model manually as described below.

To store nested property paths of Lifelines in a model

1. Right-click a Sequence Diagram in the Containment tree or an empty space in a Sequence Diagram.
2. In the context menu, select Refactor > Store Nested Property Paths in the Model . [ATTACHMENT filename='storing_nested_property_paths_in_model.png']

The nested property path of the selected Lifeline or the nested property paths of all Lifelines in the selected diagram are now stored in the model.

##### Lifeline selector

If a lifeline represents a part with the multiplicity of more than 1 (neither * nor 1..*), the list will also contain more than one object (not only one object) at runtime. The lifeline has the Selector expression that specifies the particular part represented by the lifeline.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports the lifeline selector which allows specifying an exact object from the Selector expression. The Selector expression will be evaluated as an index integer to get an Object from the object collection.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The ledger[3] object is selected according to the index integer of lifeline Selector 3 of the Ledger object.

If the selector of a receiver is invalid or cannot be evaluated as an index integer, e.g., "X", none of the objects will be used, and a warning message will display in the simulation console.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="25499c7f-9249-4ba6-9ce1-5c36da27b323"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481082</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0850e536-6317-448a-b379-246e79a80c97"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481084</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8985538e-15ce-45b5-8d8c-fd3f0f7e9012"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481081</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22833a8c-ad76-4544-ac98-21fc22209f26"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06a45f05-b7d0-4d89-ac03-0020e5cc5038"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481083</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can use a lifeline in a Sequence diagram to represent a property owned by a Class or a block that is the context of the diagram. Sending messages between objects specifying the properties presented by the lifelines can occur. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses lifelines to find a source and a target object of the message. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Lifelines and Their Objects in the Variables Pane at Run-time" ac:alt="Lifelines and Their Objects in the Variables Pane at Run-time"><ri:attachment ri:filename="lifelines_atRuntime.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Lifeline" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Simulation console showing how lifelines can be used to find the source and target of a message.</h6><p>You can use a lifeline in a SysML project to represent a nested property of a Class or a block, which is the context of a diagram. A path to the nested property appears as a dot notation on the lifeline symbol. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the path on the lifeline symbol to find the object.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d330ccbc-98f0-4145-827d-0b36f703c230"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses a lifeline with a dot notation to represent a nested property when you record a simulation as a Sequence diagram. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Recording simulation as a Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Recording a Simulation as a Sequence Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for more information about recording a simulation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Interaction Simulation with Lifelines Representing Nested Parts of a Diagram Context" ac:alt="Interaction Simulation with Lifelines Representing Nested Parts of a Diagram Context"><ri:attachment ri:filename="lifelines_repNestedparts.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Lifeline" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Simulation console showing how a lifeline with a dot notation can represent a nested property.</h6><p>Because Messages recorded in the system are not executed that they are internal mechanisms, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit only executes Messages that are external to the system, e.g., a Message going from <em>User</em> Lifeline (without types) and <em>Found</em> Messages. All other Messages are not executed but verified and printed in the <strong>Console</strong> pane.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0ab35ef-8fbb-44ac-87a9-e0d24124410a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A <em>Found</em> Message is a Message where the occurrence of a receiving Event is known, but the occurrence of a sending Event is not.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram with Found Messages.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Lifeline" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Executing only external outgoing Messages, e.g., User Lifeline and Found Messages.</h6><h3>Storing nested property paths in a model</h3><p>From version 2021x Refresh2, the Lifelines used to represent nested properties have the «NestedPropertyPath» stereotype applied to them. As a result, the path of a nested property is stored in the <strong>propertyPath</strong> tag of a Lifeline. Nested property paths of the Lifelines created with an earlier version of a modeling tool are stored in diagram symbols and you should save them in the model manually as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To store nested property paths of Lifelines in a model</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click a Sequence Diagram in the Containment tree or an empty space in a Sequence Diagram.</li><li>In the context menu, select <strong>Refactor</strong> &gt; <strong>Store Nested Property Paths in the Model</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="storing_nested_property_paths_in_model.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Lifeline" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p>The nested property path of the selected Lifeline or the nested property paths of all Lifelines in the selected diagram are now stored in the model.<br /><br /></p><h3>Lifeline selector</h3><p class="Textbody">If a lifeline represents a part with the multiplicity of more than 1 (neither * nor 1..*), the list will also contain more than one object (not only one object) at runtime. The lifeline has the Selector expression that specifies the particular part represented by the lifeline.</p><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports the lifeline selector which allows specifying an exact object from the Selector expression. The Selector expression will be evaluated as an index integer to get an Object from the object collection.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Lifeline.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Lifeline" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>The ledger[3] object is selected according to the index integer of lifeline Selector 3 of the Ledger object.</h6><p>If the selector of a receiver is invalid or cannot be evaluated as an index integer, e.g., &quot;X&quot;, none of the objects will be used, and a warning message will display in the simulation console.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626274 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00391: (2026x) Logical expression

- page_id: `255626274`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626274/2026x+Logical+expression
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:36.948+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator > (2026x) Evaluating expressions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

402984464

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

402984466

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

402984465

A logical expression is an expression that uses comparison operators. You can use a logical expression to define a constraint. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the expression to validate values at runtime.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

A logical expression must contain a comparison operator (==, <, >, <=, >=, and <>) as shown in the following examples:

- val1 >= "500" is a valid expression.
- val2.equals("500") == true is a valid expression.
- val2.equals("500") is an invalid expression.

#### Red background for the labels and text fields in UI

If some constraints failed during evaluation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit (CST) will highlight the values in the **Variables** pane with a red background to denote that the values fail the constraint. All parent nodes (Name column) that contain the failed constraints will have a red background (until the root one in the tree view), which indicates failures deeper in the structure tree. You can also see these values in the **Simulation Console** pane.

Furthermore, both the values and validation that are highlighted must always be consistent with the labels and text boxes of both the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''] and the HTML UI during simulation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Red backgrounds indicating failures during evaluation.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Neither green background nor tooltip will be used for "passed" verification status for both Swing mockups and HTML UI.

A tooltip will also appear if you hover the mouse over the failed constraint. Each parent of the failed constraint will appear with a contextual path "dot notation" starting from the node on which tooltip is shown.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Nodes with green background indicating valid values.

This example uses the [ATTACHMENT filename='equationSysml.png']block **Circle** previously shown in the section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Mathematical equation' space='']. If we modify the expression of the constraint block **Circle** **Area** by changing the assignment operator "**=**" to the equality operator "**==**", the parametric evaluator will use the expression to validate the values of both area and radius. If the result is false, the values will be highlighted in red (see Figure: The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree). But, if the result is true, the values will be highlighted in green (see Figure: Nodes with green background indicating valid values).

#### Finding a constraint object that binds the value property

You can find the constraint object to which the value of the property is bound in the **Variables** pane.

To find a constraint object to which a value property is bound

- Right-click a constraint object row in the **Variables** pane, and select **Go** **To**. The constraint to which the value of the property is bound will appear, as shown in the figure below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Finding a constraint object that binds the value property through the shortcut menu.

When you export the object to the instance specification, if the exported object contains a constraint object (an object of a constraint block) and if the expression of the constraint block is a boolean expression, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the result of the evaluation, which is a literal boolean, to a slot defined by the constraint property typed by the constraint block.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Exporting an instance as a slot value of the constraint property.

402984463

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Mathematical equation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b499149-7d9e-4c32-a902-b91152c31ca9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984464</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a523c23c-1cd5-4ddf-8b2b-fc889766c906"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984466</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="491e0a58-cf88-478e-81bb-67182c8b8264"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984465</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A logical expression is an expression that uses comparison operators. You can use a logical expression to define a constraint. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the expression to validate values at runtime.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee9f0260-2353-4e52-b390-032a8411a006"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A logical expression must contain a comparison operator (==, &lt;, &gt;, &lt;=, &gt;=, and &lt;&gt;) as shown in the following examples:</p><ul><li><em>val1 &gt;= &quot;500&quot;</em> is a valid expression.<em><br /></em></li><li><em>val2.equals(&quot;500&quot;) == true</em> is a valid expression.</li><li><em>val2.equals(&quot;500&quot;)</em> is an invalid expression.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Red background for the labels and text fields in UI</h2><p>If some constraints failed during evaluation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit (CST) will highlight the values in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane with a red background to denote that the values fail the constraint. All parent nodes (Name column) that contain the failed constraints will have a red background (until the root one in the tree view), which indicates failures deeper in the structure tree. You can also see these values in the <strong>Simulation Console</strong> pane. </p><p class="Standard">Furthermore, both the values and validation that are highlighted must always be consistent with the labels and text boxes of both the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) UI modeling diagram simulation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[UI modeling diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and the HTML UI during simulation.</p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="redbackground.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Logical expression" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">Red backgrounds indicating failures during evaluation.</h6><p class="Standard"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="460efdba-3bcb-43ba-9399-06b4ef36d21b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Neither green background nor tooltip will be used for &quot;passed&quot; verification status for both Swing mockups and HTML UI.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard">A tooltip will also appear if you hover the mouse over the failed constraint. Each parent of the failed constraint will appear with a contextual path &quot;dot notation&quot; starting from the node on which tooltip is shown. </p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Logical expression" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree.</h6><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression3.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Logical expression" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">Nodes with green background indicating valid values.</h6><p>This example uses the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="equationSysml.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Logical expression" /></ri:attachment><ac:link-body>block <strong>Circle</strong></ac:link-body></ac:link> previously shown in the section <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Mathematical equation" /></ac:link>. If we modify the expression of the constraint block <strong>Circle</strong> <strong>Area</strong> by changing the assignment operator &quot;<strong>=</strong>&quot; to the equality operator &quot;<strong>==</strong>&quot;, the parametric evaluator will use the expression to validate the values of both area and radius. If the result is false, the values will be highlighted in red (see Figure: The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree). But, if the result is true, the values will be highlighted in green (<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cc81ea82-e052-46f3-9aaf-f96d747383e1">see Figure: Nodes with green background indicating valid values</ac:inline-comment-marker>). </p><h2>Finding a constraint object that binds the value property</h2><p>You can find the constraint object to which the value of the property is bound in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p>To find a constraint object to which a value property is bound</p><hr /><ul><li><p>Right-click a constraint object row in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, and select <strong>Go</strong> <strong>To</strong>. The constraint to which the value of the property is bound will appear, as shown in the figure below.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression4.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Logical expression" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Finding a constraint object that binds the value property through the shortcut menu. </h6></li></ul><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />When you export the object to the instance specification, if the exported object contains a constraint object (an object of a constraint block) and if the expression of the constraint block is a boolean expression, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the result of the evaluation, which is a literal boolean, to a slot defined by the constraint property typed by the constraint block. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression5.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Logical expression" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Exporting an instance as a slot value of the constraint property.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8570066-61e1-4e77-8ffc-27dedf29d275"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984463</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Mathematical equation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255624548 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00392: (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation

- page_id: `255624548`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255624548/2026x+Magic+Model+Analyst+Cameo+Simulation+Toolkit+Documentation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:41:48.922+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides the first in the industry extendable model execution framework based on OMG fUML and W3C SCXML standards. It extends UPDM plugin to validate system behavior by executing, animating, and debugging SysML Parametric models in the context of realistic mock-ups of the intended user interface.

##### Why Simulation?

The purpose of a simulation is to gain system understanding without manipulating the real system, either because it is not yet defined or available, or because it cannot be exercised directly due to cost, time, resources or risk constraints. Simulation is typically performed on a model of the system. 
 
With the plugin, users can test how the system reacts to user interaction or predefined testing data and execution scenarios.

##### Business Benefits

Users will find that one of the key business benefits of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is cost efficiency. Employing Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will assist enterprises in significantly reducing project costs, allowing users to identify design errors before the actual production of components.

To learn how to install and use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit, see:

**Documentation of earlier versions**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625159 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00393: (2026x) Managing active simulations

- page_id: `255625159`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625159/2026x+Managing+active+simulations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:21:12.599+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Server-side simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can view and manage all active server-side simulations in the Task Manager. The Task Manager not only provides information about all currently running and queued simulations but also allows you to terminate them and open the simulation UI window.

To view and manage all active simulations, you need to have the Manage Simulations permission. Without the Manage Simulations permission, you can only view and manage the simulations you have started yourself.

To view and manage active simulations

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resources application' space='MCS'] or a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud' space='MCS'] .
2. In the top right corner of the screen, click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] and select Task manager .
3. In the Task manager dialog, select the Simulation tab.
4. Depending on what you need, complete one or several of the following actions:
  - In the **Task manager** dialog, view all active simulations and their information, like the simulated projects, users who started the simulations, their run time, and the simulation status. The **Run at** column shows the simulation run time in the local time zone of your computer.
  - Click a simulation line to see the simulation ID and the Simulation Configuration name.
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='open_ui.png'] next to a simulation to open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Simulation with UI' space=''] if the simulated model has a UI mockup, Time Series chart, or Timeline chart.
  - Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to a simulation to terminate it.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Task manager** dialog allows you to view and manage active simulations.

## PAGE 00394: (2026x) Manipulating global variables

- page_id: `255625092`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625092/2026x+Manipulating+global+variables
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:21.500+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Action languages > (2026x) Action scripts APIs > (2026x) Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

You can use global variables in ALH to share data across scripts during simulation runtime. Unlike standard variables, they are not limited to a single script and retain their values. You can check if a global variable exists, get its value, add new data, or remove it when no longer needed.

##### Checking an existing global variable

You can use script tocheck if a global variable is defined in an environment by using **isGlobalVariable("variableName")** argument.

If the global variable is specified by the given variable name in the scripting environment, then the argument returns true. Otherwise, it returns false.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to check if a global variable, e.g., GLOBAL_COUNT, is defined through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Getting a value from a global variable

During the execution of a model, you can obtain a value of a global variable which is already defined by calling **getGlobalVariable("variableName")** of the ALH API. This argument returns a Java object, which is the value of the global variable specified by the given variable name.

```text

```

The example code is as follows:

```text

```

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can obtain the value of a global variable directly by using its name. 
a = "GLOBAL_COUNT" + 1;

##### Adding a value to a global variable

A global variable is a variable that is accessible from any session in the same execution. You can use ALH API to create a global variable and specify its value by calling**ALH.setGlobalVariable("variableName", value)**.

```text

```

The code example is as follows

```text

```

The example code assigns 1 to a global variable named GLOBAL_COUNT. If the global variable with the given name does not exist, this method will create a new global variable with the same name and add the value to it.

##### Removing a defined global variable

You can remove a global variable that is previously defined by calling a **removeGlobalVariable("variableName")** of the ALH API.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to remove a global variable, e.g., *GLOBAL_COUNT*, that was previously defined through ALH API.

```text

```

If the global variable with the given name does not exist, this method will create a new global variable with the same name and add the value to it.</p><h3>Removing a defined global variable</h3><p>You can remove a global variable that is previously defined by calling a <strong>removeGlobalVariable(&quot;variableName&quot;)</strong> of the ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0513a0d7-1d02-43ea-b6ba-a7a5f1711c7f"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeGlobalVariable("variableName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following code fragment shows how to remove a global variable, e.g., <em>GLOBAL_COUNT</em>, that was previously defined through ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f6ea4d87-c715-4129-ab7d-e1fca10254ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeGlobalVariable("GLOBAL_COUNT");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625000 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00395: (2026x) Manipulating runtime objects using scripts

- page_id: `255625000`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625000/2026x+Manipulating+runtime+objects+using+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:42:15.392+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Action languages > (2026x) Action scripts APIs
- labels: ['alh-api']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Manipulating run-time objects with scripts lets you dynamically control simulation environment, structural elements, and behavioral workflows. You can modify structural and global variables, invoke behaviors and operations. You can also create and send signals, and retrieve key runtime data like token values, simulation time, and callers of the scripts. Additionally, you can evaluate expressions and check behaviors to gain information about the simulation.

Action scripts can be used where the scripts are evaluated during simulation, for example in the Opaque Behaviors, Actions, Guards, or directly in the simulation **Console** pane). You can use the following types of action scripts to manipulate runtime objects:

- fUML object syntax, which simplifies some ALH actions. Example: object.get("property")
- ALH (Action Language Helper) scripts. Example: ALH.getValue(object, "property")
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Alf Plugin Documentation' space='MAA'] - Action Language for fUML (requires an additional plugin). Example: object.property

It is easy to access elements that are deeper in the hierarchy tree, but accessing parent elements is more complicated. Therefore, it is important to plan where executing a particular script would be the most efficient.

##### Specifying context

Scripts are context sensitive, whenever you execute a script, you must specify its context (object). Mostly, it is the current simulation context (marked as "self" in a script). However, it can also be an input pin (pin name) or an object created in a script itself (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a runtime object' space='']). When evaluating from Simulation console, the context depends on the selected element in the variables pane. For example:

To get the*Diameter*Value Property when the script is executed in the*Car*Block, the script should be**self.get("tyre.wheel.Diameter")**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Getting the *Diameter* Value Property when the simulation context is the *Car* Block.

To get the*Diameter*Value Property when the script is executed after selecting the wheel, the script should be **D****iameter.**

The first level properties can be directly accessed using their name.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Getting the *Diameter* Value Property when the script is executed in the Console pane.

To learn more, refer to the following topics:

**Additional resources**

- You can find more examples of API usage at <install_root> \samples\simulation\SmallTestSamples.mdzip (Tests section).
- For more information, see the JavaDoc at <install_root> \openapidocs\SimulationJavaDoc .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

You can modify structural and global variables, invoke <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c5aacf3c-ef98-4b02-89e7-c39ac0c31943">behaviors and operations</ac:inline-comment-marker>. You can also create and send signals, and retrieve key runtime data like token values, simulation time, and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="dfea78c4-da82-422a-b86d-a0a51ab58d70">callers of the scripts</ac:inline-comment-marker>. Additionally, you can evaluate expressions and check behaviors to gain information about the simulation.</p><p>Action scripts can be used where the scripts are evaluated during simulation, for example in the Opaque Behaviors, Actions, Guards, or directly in the simulation <strong>Console</strong> pane). <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625284 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00396: (2026x) Manipulating simulation information

- page_id: `255625284`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625284/2026x+Manipulating+simulation+information
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:27.585+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Animation > (2026x) Simulation information in diagrams
- labels: ['changing-runtime-values', 'sending-singals']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Prerequisites

1903536946

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Prerequisites

1903536952

1903536944

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Prerequisites

1903536951

When simulating a model, you can manipulate simulation information not only in the **Simulation** window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can change runtime values or send a trigger directly on Part shapes and Port lablels.

#### INFO: Types of diagrams

Types of diagrams

You can manipulate simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram.

##### Changing runtime values

You can change the runtime values of Part properties and Port labels directly in diagrams the same way you change them in the **Variables** pane of the **Simulation** window.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To display and change runtime values in diagrams, the **Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes**and/or ****Show Runtime Values on Port Labels****animation options must be enabled. For more information, see[Disabling and enabling animation options](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Displaying+simulation+information#Displayingsimulationinformation-Disablingandenablinganimationoptions).

To change runtime values on Part shapes or Port labels

1. During model simulation, select a Part shape or Port labels and click a runtime value you want to change.
2. Enter a new runtime value and press Enter. The figure below shows how to change different types of values. 33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing a numeric runtime value.33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing an enumeration runtime value.33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing a boolean runtime value.

When you change runtime values of Value or PortProperties as described above, the simulation data is updated in real-time accordingly.

##### Sending a signal

When simulating a model, you cansend a signaldirectly from a Part shape in a diagram the same way you would send it in the **Sessions** pane of the **Simulation** window. If it is possible to send a signal, a special button is displayed on a Part shape after selecting it.

To send a signal from a Part shape

1. During model simulation, select the shape of the Part from which you want to send a signal .
2. Click the signal icon next to the part shape as displayed below. If you can send more than one signal, the icons of all available signals are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='sending_signal.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255625165 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00397: (2026x) Manipulating structural values

- page_id: `255625165`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255625165/2026x+Manipulating+structural+values
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:47:25.365+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Action languages > (2026x) Action scripts APIs > (2026x) Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

You can retrieve, modify, add, or remove structural feature values using the Action Language Helper (ALH) API and action scripts in the modeling tool. ALH allows you to dynamically control object properties, and update data during a simulation. You can also retrieve tag values, which store additional metadata.

##### Getting a structural feature value

You can use script to get a structural value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with the **object.get("****featureName")***.* featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:

- value = self.get("car.speed");

Using Groovy scripting language, you can get nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, value=car.speed

Alternatively, structural value can also be acquired using ALH API.

```text

```

The following example shows how to get the value of the structural feature indicated above in ALH API.

```text

```

#### INFO: Information

Information

While using Rhino JavaScript, the return value of ALH.getValue() is not automatically UnBoxed to a primitive type. Users need to handle such UnBoxing by themselves, by using ALH.getValue(object, "speed").intValue() to get the value of primitive integer type.

##### Specifying a structural feature value

You can use script to specify a structural value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with the **object.set("featureName", value)***.* featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:

- value = self .set("speed", 10);

Using Groovy scripting language, you can set nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, car.speed=100

Alternatively, structural value can also be specified using ALH API.

```text

```

The following example shows how to specify the value of the structural feature in ALH API:

```text

```

In addition, the RuntimeObject can be omitted. The following example shows how to set a structural feature value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:

```text

```

##### Adding a value to an object

You can use script to add a value to an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with**object.addValue("featureName", value)**and**object.addValueAt("featureName", value, insertAt)***.*For example:

- self.addValue("p1",10); // System.p1 = [10]
- self.addValue("p1",30); // System.p1 = [10, 30]
- self.addValueAt("p1",20,2); // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]

Alternatively, structural value can also be added using ALH API.

```text

```

If you have more than one value in an object and if you want to add a value at a specific position, you can use insertAt argument.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to add values to an object, e.g.,*System.p1*, through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Removing a value of an object

You can use script to remove a value from an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.removeAt("****featureName", removeAt)***.*For example:

- print(self); // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]
- self.removeAt("p1",3); // System.p1 = [10, 20]

If feature has only one value, removeAt index 1 should be used.

Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.

```text

```

You can also use the fUML object syntax, with**object.remove("featureName", objectToRemove)**to remove an exact fUML object from a feature. For example:

- self.remove("myPart", myPart)

object.remove("featureName", object) is useful when you have direct reference to fUML object you want to remove. Remove can be used in conjunction with [get method](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Manipulating+structural+values#Manipulatingstructuralvalues-Gettingastructuralfeaturevalue) to get a direct reference to the fUML object.

Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.

```text

```

##### Getting a tag value

You can use script to get a tag value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.getTagValue("tagName")***.* For example:

- self.getTagValue("uniform.max"); // 10

Alternatively, tag value can also be acquired using ALH API.

```text

```

If the RuntimeObject is omitted,**$context$**or**self**will be used as an object. The context object in the parametric is the constraint block object that owns the constraint, not the actual context of the diagram. The following example shows how to get a tag value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get tag values, e.g.,*max*, through ALH API. Also refer to[Value access and references by tags](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Value+access+and+references+by+tags).

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

For example, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cbe26d64-109a-4b84-a300-e16eaf933033">car.speed=100</ac:inline-comment-marker></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be specified using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f440c3d-30d1-4e49-af0b-580dc4b66608"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.setValue(object, "featureName", value)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following exam<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="eb7a6c1b-afb4-4ff7-bd58-ed1fa3f708a2">ple shows ho</ac:inline-comment-marker>w to specify the value of the structural feature in ALH API:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c608c3e-a40e-456f-86ac-4427a5ad2506"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[value = ALH.setValue(self, "speed", 10);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In addition, the Runtime<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="69118d9e-6442-40fe-b289-9c043e8d5a5d">Object can be om</ac:inline-comment-marker>itted. The following example shows how to set a structural feature value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38b0ddfb-2cb0-4750-815f-12dc55bb4044"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.setValue("featureName", value)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Adding a value to an object</span></h3><p>You can use script to add a value to an object. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can use the fUML object syntax, with </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.addValue(&quot;featureName&quot;, value)</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> and </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.addValueAt(&quot;featureName&quot;, value, insertAt)</strong><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> For example:</span></p><ul><li>self.addValue(&quot;p1&quot;,10);          // System.p1 =<span class="error"> [10]</span></li><li>self.addValue(&quot;p1&quot;,30);          // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 30]</span></li><li>self.addValueAt(&quot;p1&quot;,20,2);    // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20, 30]</span></li></ul><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be added using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a324661f-5913-4703-8ecb-1a834cf4721a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.addValue(object, "featureName", value)
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">If you have more than one value in an object  a<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="28798f2d-b609-49c9-be7a-531c4993a9f9">nd if you want</ac:inline-comment-marker> to add a value at a specific position, you can use insertAt argument.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3670fff-b34c-4593-b11b-cb0f8599085e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.addValueAt(object, "featureName", value, insertAt)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to add values to an object, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">System.p1</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, through ALH API.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7d47be2-6ab7-4876-a9b8-4c763586e0e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.addValue(self,"p1",10);            // System.p1 = [10]
ALH.addValue(self,"p1",30);            // System.p1 = [10, 30]
ALH.addValueAt(self,"p1",20,2);        // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6fa068df-ca4f-48bd-88c2-3f2affa282e7">Removing a value of an object</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>You can use script to remove a value from an object. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.removeAt(&quot;</strong><strong>featureName&quot;, removeAt)</strong></span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="89bff52b-7be2-45f5-b135-1d766858079d">For example:</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><ul><li>print(self);                                   // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20, 30]</span></li><li>self.removeAt(&quot;p1&quot;,3);               // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20]</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac4ca2c0-f7f4-4ba0-ad05-b9a9ab7b226a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If feature has only one value, removeAt index 1 should be used.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="593b7398-271a-46d5-ab04-1562b476483c">Alternatively, str</ac:inline-comment-marker>uctural value can also be removed us<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1c9da7ff-c75e-40ed-adf3-d89b2fc26560">ing ALH API.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54276771-a4a3-4a39-b30b-cb3236c5e349"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeValueAt(object, "featureName", removeAt)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">You can also use the fUML object syntax, with<strong><span> </span>object.remove(&quot;featureName&quot;, objectToRemove) </strong>to remove an exact fUML object from a feature. For example:</p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">self.remove(&quot;myPart&quot;, myPart)</span></li></ul><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.remove(&quot;featureName&quot;, object) is useful when you have direct reference to fUML object you want to remove. Remove can be used in conjunction with <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Manipulating+structural+values#Manipulatingstructuralvalues-Gettingastructuralfeaturevalue">get method</a> to get a direct reference to the fUML object.</span></p><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60cd9055-11ec-4638-b436-5e2665690d99"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeValue(object, "featureName", objectToRemove)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Getting a tag value</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can use script to get a tag value. </span>You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.getTagValue(&quot;tagName&quot;)</strong><em>.</em> For example:</p><ul><li>self.getTagValue(&quot;uniform.max&quot;);  // 10</li></ul><p>Alternatively, tag value can also be acquired using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a52cbee4-d7c7-4d66-84f8-d15bd36b291e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue(object, "tagName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">If the RuntimeObject is omitted, </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span class="confluence-link">$context$</span></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> or </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">self</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> will be used as an object. The context object in the parametric is the constraint block object that owns the constraint, not the actual context of the diagram. The following example shows how to get a tag value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76aac9e1-855d-4bb5-b98c-b9d2f153848b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue("tagName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to get tag values, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">max</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, through ALH API. Also refer to </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/(2026x) Value+access+and+references+by+tags" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Value access and references by tags</a><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3cb6ad5-0daa-4d6d-af44-9d520c1f7b9a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue(self, "uniform.max");	// 10]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255626327 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00398: (2026x) Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator

- page_id: `255626327`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255626327/2026x+Manual+value+updates+using+the+Parametric+Evaluator
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T18:43:39.045+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit > (2026x) Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['value-updates-with-parametric-evaluator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

468317576

468317578

468317577

Changing values on a property of an object will cause the parametric evaluator to automatically update the other related values with the constraint defined on the object. Or, you may want to update the values with the constraint once you set all necessary values of the properties. The latter is possible by executing your model with the SimulationConfig whose fireValueChangeEvent = false (see section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''] for more information about the SimulationConfig and its tag definitions) and call the API provided by the parametric evaluator whenever you desire. The following figure shows an example of updating values manually with the parametric evaluator.

**Signature**: com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation.parametrics.ParametricsEngine.executeObject(Object object)

**Parameter**: Object is an object whose internal values will be updated by the parametric evaluator.

You can call the API with the MagicDraw script engine. For example, you may define it in the body of an opaque behavior. Then, use a call behavior action to call the opaque behavior somewhere in your model.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Manual Value Update with Activity in CylinderPipe.mdzip.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the **CylinderPipe.mdzip** sample model that comes with the modeling tool.

To open this sample, do one of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='CylinderPipe.mdzip'] **
- Find in the modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\simulation\Parametrics\CylinderPipe.mdzip..

468317575
